1: Healing is not a victory over the disease. This is finding enough strength so that the disease is no longer needed.
8: In 460 BC, a man was born on the island of Kos, whom two and a half millennia later we still call the “father of medicine.” Hippocrates was not just a doctor. He was an observer. He looked at the patient the way an astronomer looks at the sky—seeing not a single star, but a system in which every movement is connected with every other.
9: When a man came to him with a cough, Hippocrates asked about dreams. When I had a headache, I was interested in food and grief. When the wound did not heal, he asked about fears and unfinished business. This was not superstition. This was logic that we are only now beginning to replicate in laboratories.
10: Hippocrates understood what modern science has proven only in recent decades: man is an indivisible system. Body, mind and environment exist in constant dialogue. To break this dialogue means to stop understanding what is happening.
11: But that's exactly what we did.
13: The history of medicine is the history of a gradual, brilliant and somewhat tragic division.
14: In the 18th century, Giovanni Battista Morgagni made a revolution: he dissected thousands of corpses and showed that diseases live in the organs. Before him, illness was a cloud, a fog—now it has received an address. This was a colossal step forward.
15: In the 19th century, Rudolf Virchow went deeper: the disease lives in cells. Not in the entire organ, but in specific cells that have changed, mutated, gotten out of control. Medicine received a microscope, and the world became smaller, more precise, and more manageable.
16: In the 20th century we mastered molecules. DNA, receptors, neurotransmitters. We have learned to read the genome and edit it with CRISPR scissors. We have created drugs that hit one single molecule at one single point in the biochemical chain.
17: Each of these steps is a true triumph of the human mind. And each of them took us a little further from the patient as a person.
18: Today, a cardiologist is waiting for you at a large clinic who does not know about your anxiety disorder. A gastroenterologist who doesn't know about your divorce three years ago. An endocrinologist who sees only the thyroid gland, although your fatigue originates in your head. Each of them is highly qualified. Everyone looks at the part. Nobody sees the whole.
19: This is called specialization. And this is both the greatest achievement and the unhealed wound of modern medicine.
21: Imagine an orchestra. One hundred musicians, each a virtuoso. But everyone has their own score, and they don’t hear each other. Instead of a symphony there is noise.
22: This is roughly how the health care system works in most countries of the world.
23: A patient with chronic back pain sees an orthopedist, neurologist, rheumatologist and physical therapist. Everyone assigns their own. Nobody asks what is going on in his life. Nobody knows that his mother died three years ago, that he sleeps for five hours, that work has long become a cage. No one associates cortisol with inflammation, inflammation with pain, pain with depression, depression with immunodeficiency.
24: But this is one chain. One person.
25: In 1977, psychiatrist George Engel published an article in the journal Science that created a quiet revolution. He called the dominant model of medicine “biomedical” and proposed replacing it with “biopsychosocial.” His thesis was simple: illness cannot be understood without taking biology, psyche, and social context into account simultaneously.
26: The medical community nodded in agreement... and continued to work as before. Because changing the system is more difficult than writing an article.
28: While Western medicine went deeper—to the molecule and the gene—other traditions never lost sight of the whole.
29: For three thousand years, Chinese medicine has described the body as a system of flows. Blocked flow is a disease. Restoring flow is healing. This sounds metaphorical, but behind the metaphor there is an accurate observation: when circulation (blood, lymph, nerve impulses, hormonal signals) is disrupted, the system begins to malfunction. Modern science confirms this—in other words.
30: Ayurveda speaks of a person's constitution—prakriti—as an individual pattern that determines vulnerabilities and resources. Today we call this “genetic predisposition” and “phenotype.” The essence is the same.
31: Tibetan medicine does not cure illness—it restores balance. Literally: Tib. gso ba—“return to balance.” Homeostasis. The same idea that was discovered in the 19th century by physiologist Claude Bernard, who introduced the concept of “the internal environment of the body.”
32: The ancient systems were not unscientific. They were pre-scientific—that is, they existed before we had the language to accurately describe what they had been observing for millennia.
33: Integrative medicine is not nostalgia for the past. This is a return to a question that has never lost its relevance: what is health, and how to restore it in a particular person?
34: THE BIRTH OF A NEW APPROACH
35: The term "integrative medicine" appeared in American universities in the 1990s. Andrew Weil in Arizona, Dean Ornish in California, Jon Kabat-Zinn in Massachusetts—each from his own point—began to build bridges between evidence-based science and a holistic approach to the person.
36: They were united not by mysticism, but by methodology: to look at the patient completely. Take everything into account. Prove everything.
37: In 1999, the National Center for Complementary and Integrative Medicine at the National Institutes of Health was created in the United States—with an annual budget of hundreds of millions of dollars. Science demanded an answer to the question: does it work?
38: And the answer gradually came. Research has shown that meditation literally changes the structure of the brain—increasing the volume of gray matter in the brain in areas of attention and compassion. That nutrition affects gene expression. That social isolation increases the risk of death as much as smoking 15 cigarettes a day. That chronic stress destroys telomeres—the protective caps of chromosomes—accelerating biological aging.
39: This is not alternative medicine. This is medicine. Just looking wider.
41: It is important to say right away what integrative medicine is not.
42: It is not a waiver of evidence. Antibiotics for a bacterial infection, insulin for type 1 diabetes, surgery for appendicitis—no one cancels what works and is proven.
43: It is not a collection of exotic practices under one roof. Reiki, aromatherapy and homeopathy are a separate conversation that requires separate rigor.
44: Integrative medicine is a principle. The principle that says: treat the person, not the disease. Look at the system, not the symptom. Use everything that is proven to work—from a molecule to meditation, from a scalpel to nutrition. And always, always ask: why did this person get sick right now?
45: This question—why now?—opens doors that standard protocol doesn't even know exist.
47: This book was written by a doctor. But it is not written only for doctors.
48: It is written for you, a person who may have long felt that something is wrong, but the tests are “normal.” For a person who is tired of conveyor belt medicine, where an appointment takes seven minutes and a ready-made prescription. For a person who intuitively understands that the body speaks to us, we just need to learn to listen.
49: It is written for a doctor who remembers why he came into the profession - and sometimes loses this memory in the flow of documentation and protocols.
50: The path we will walk together begins here—in history. Because to understand where to go, you need to know where you came from. We lost something important on the road from Hippocrates to molecular biology. Not because they were wrong, but because they moved in one direction for too long.
52: Key message: Integrative medicine is not a step back to a pre-scientific past. This is a step forward, towards a science mature enough to return to the integrity of man.

61: CHAPTER 2. MEDICINE OF GENERATIONS: FAMILY AS A SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
66: Imagine two twin brothers. Same genome. One mother, one father, one kindergarten. But at thirty-five, one suffers from chronic anxiety and an autoimmune disease, and the other is energetic, healthy, full of life.
67: Genetics is silent. She doesn't explain the difference. History explains.
68: In 2004, a team of scientists led by Michael Meaney at McGill University published a study that revolutionized understanding of how maternal behavior shapes the brains of their offspring. Rats that were actively licked by their mother in the first days of life grew up less anxious, coped better with stress and lived longer. Rats whose mothers licked little grew up hyper-reactive—their HPA axis (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) was tuned to constant anxiety.
69: The most amazing thing is that this effect was passed on through generations. A rat raised in conditions of low maternal care became a less caring mother itself. And it wasn't encoded in DNA. It was an epigenetic record—invisible marks on genes that turned anxiety on or off without changing the DNA sequence itself.
70: The family is not just a social unit. Family is a biological program.
72: When we say “it runs in our family,” we usually mean genes. “All of us have heart problems,” “all of us are anxious,” “all of us are overweight.” This is a half-truth.
73: Genes create opportunities. Epigenetics decides which of these possibilities are realized.
74: The word epigenetics literally means “above genetics”—it is a regulatory system that controls the activity of genes without changing their sequence. The main tools of this system are DNA methylation (the attachment of chemical groups to certain sites, which “silences” the gene) and histone acetylation (changes in the proteins around which DNA is wound, which “opens” or “closes” the gene for reading).
75: A key discovery of the last twenty years: these marks can be passed on from parents to children. Not through education, but literally through biology.
76: In 2016, Rachel Yehuda of the Icahn School of Medicine published a study of Holocaust survivors and their children. Children whose parents survived concentration camps were found to have altered expression of the FKBP5 gene, a stress response regulator. The same characteristic as the survivors themselves. Trauma transmission—the transmission of trauma across generations—turned out to be not a metaphor, but a molecular fact.
77: FAMILY AS A PRIMARY HEALTH ENVIRONMENT
78: The ACE (Adverse Childhood Experiences) study is one of the largest in the history of medicine. Begun in 1995 as a collaboration between the Centers for Disease Control and Kaiser Permanente, it has reached more than 17,000 adults.
80: Children who experienced four or more types of adverse experiences (physical abuse, emotional neglect, parental alcoholism, divorce, parental incarceration) had:
81: —2.5 times higher risk of coronary heart disease;
83: -12 times more likely to abuse alcohol;
84: -20 years less life expectancy in extreme cases.
85: These are not psychological fairy tales. This is physiology. Chronic stress in childhood changes the architecture of the brain, disrupts the functioning of the immune system, and accelerates biological aging at the cellular level—shortening telomeres.
86: Family turns out to be the most powerful health factor known. More powerful than most drugs.
87: FAMILY AS A SCHOOL OF BODY PATTERNS
88: But family influence goes beyond trauma. It permeates every aspect of our physiological existence.
89: How we breathe is something we learned in our family. We saw how we react to pain in our parents. How we eat, how we sleep, how we endure illness, how we treat our body—all this is learned in the first years of life through observation and unconscious imitation.
90: Neuroscientist Stephen Porges described what he called “neuroception”—an unconscious system for assessing the safety of the environment. Our autonomic nervous system constantly scans the space: is it safe? Is there a threat? Can I relax?
91: If the main background in the family was tension, unpredictability, and fear, the child’s nervous system is tuned to chronic readiness for threat. The sympathetic nervous system operates in “always on alert” mode. This means: chronic inflammation, poor digestion, disrupted sleep, anxiety—as an underlying condition that seems normal because it has always been normal.
92: Dr. Nadine Burke Harris, who became California's first chief pediatrician, described the phenomenon this way: “Toxic stress in childhood is like constantly putting pressure on the accelerator of the biological stress system. Over time, the engine wears out."
94: But family is not only about risks. These are resources that cannot be obtained from anywhere else.
95: Research on resilience shows that the main protective factor for a child under stress is the presence of at least one stable, caring adult. Not two, not three, at least one. Grandmother, uncle, older brother, school teacher.
96: This is not just psychological support. This is a neurobiological anchor. The presence of a safe adult literally changes the biochemistry of a child's brain: it reduces cortisol levels, stimulates the production of oxytocin, and activates the parasympathetic nervous system.
97: Family also conveys patterns of meaning. How do we treat illness—as a punishment, as a weakness, as a signal, as an opportunity? How do we cope with loss? How do we ask for help (or not ask)?
98: These patterns are an invisible inheritance that determines our medical biography no less than genetic risks.
99: MEDICINE FOR GENERATIONS: A NEW DIAGNOSTIC VIEW
100: In integrative medicine, the anamnesis—the medical history—does not begin with the first symptom or with the patient’s birth. It starts with his parents. Sometimes from grandparents.
102: —What diseases did your parents suffer from?
103: —What was the emotional climate in your family as a child?
104: —Have there been any traumatic events in your family history?
105: —How did your family relate to the body—to pain, to illness, to medicine?
106: —Who in the family was the “bearer of health”—the one who taught how to take care of yourself?
107: These questions sound unusual in a medical office. But they reveal insights that no blood test can provide.
108: The family is the first medical system we encounter. And the most long-lasting.

118: CHAPTER 3. WHY MODERN MAN LOSES CONNECTION WITH THE BODY
122: A STRANGER IN HIS OWN BODY
123: Try this right now: close your eyes and feel your body. Don't think about it—feel it. Is there tension somewhere? Where exactly do you hold your breath? How does your stomach feel—relaxed or contracted? How are your legs standing - stable or stiff?
124: Most people find this experiment difficult. Not because they don't have a body. But because they have long forgotten how to hear him.
125: We live in an era of unprecedented disconnect between mind and body. People know that they need to eat right, but they eat on the go, not noticing the taste. They know they need to rest, but they can’t stop. They experience pain, but ignore it until it becomes unbearable. And then they wonder: how did this happen?
126: It happened gradually. And this is not a weakness of character. This is a cultural program that we inherited.
128: Neuroscientist Bud Craig of the Barrow Institute described a system that most people know nothing about, even though it works for them every second. He called it interoception—the ability to perceive the internal state of the body.
129: Interoception is more than just “feeling pain.” This is a rich information system: the feeling of hunger and satiety, thirst signals, the feeling of heartbeat, warmth and cold in different parts of the body, the feeling of “gut feeling”, anxiety as a bodily contraction, joy as lightness in the chest.
130: Craig showed that interoceptive information is processed primarily in the insula, a brain structure that is also involved in empathy, decision making, and self-awareness. Poorly developed interoception is associated with anxiety disorders, eating disorders, chronic pain and psychosomatic illnesses.
131: This means that the body doesn't just get us from point A to point B. It constantly sends out the signals we need to make healthy decisions. And when we stop hearing them, we lose a vital navigational tool.
132: HOW WE LOSE CONNECTION: FIVE MECHANISMS
134: The average person in 2024 spends more than 7 hours a day in front of screens. Smartphone, computer, TV. Screens capture attention from the outside—and literally crowd out the ability to notice the inside.
135: Research shows that the more time a person spends on social networks, the worse they recognize their own emotions. This is not a metaphor. This is a measurable neurobiological effect.
137: The body is made for movement. For most of evolutionary history, humans moved 6–8 hours a day. Today, most people in developed countries spend more than 10 hours sitting.
138: Physical inactivity doesn't just weaken muscles. It impairs proprioception—the sense of the body's position in space—and reduces sensitivity to bodily signals in general.
140: Our culture treats pain as a problem that needs to be fixed immediately. A pill for the head, a pill for the stomach, a pill to fall asleep, a pill to not worry. Medicine has turned the symptom into an enemy rather than a signal.
141: When we learn to immediately drown out every discomfort, we lose the ability to discern: what is this body trying to tell me?
143: Psychological dissociation—withdrawal from bodily sensations—is a defense mechanism that develops in response to pain that cannot be tolerated: physical or emotional.
144: Children who have experienced abuse often describe how they "left the body" during traumatic episodes. This is an adaptive response. But when it becomes established, the person continues to live in partial dissociation as an adult, without realizing it.
146: The modern world values ​​output—result, product, achievement. The body is perceived as a tool: it should work, not break, not require attention. Fatigue—weakness. Illness is failure. “I can’t be sick—I have a deadline.”
147: This is an installation that is literally programmed to ignore bodily signals until the system begins to fail seriously.
148: THE PRICE OF BREAKING: WHAT HAPPENS IN THE BODY
149: When we stop hearing the body, the body begins to scream.
150: The global burden of disease is growing despite technological advances in medicine. According to the GBD Study 2019, published in The Lancet, chronic non-communicable diseases (cardiovascular, metabolic, oncological, mental) account for more than 70% of all mortality in the world. Most of them are lifestyle diseases. Diseases of the gap between man and his biological nature.
151: Specific consequences of loss of interoceptive communication:
152: -Chronic inflammation. Stress that is not recognized as stress (because the person does not notice the body's signals) continues to smolder. Cortisol, adrenaline, pro-inflammatory cytokines—a constant background fire that slowly damages blood vessels, joints, and the brain.
153: —Eating disorders. A person who has lost connection with the body does not distinguish between hunger and emotional pain. Eats when anxious. Doesn't eat when grieving. Food turns from a signal of biological need into a way to manage unbearable feelings.
154: -Chronic pain. Research shows that many cases of chronic pain are not associated with physical tissue damage. They are due to the fact that the nervous system is “stuck” in alarm mode and can no longer distinguish between a real threat and a memory of it.
155: RETURN: HOW TO HEAR THE BODY AGAIN
156: The good news is that interoception is a skill that can be restored. Neuroscience shows that these brain structures maintain plasticity throughout life.
157: Mindfulness is the most studied tool. Formal practice of mindfulness meditation increases the volume of gray matter in the insula, the very area that processes interoceptive information. This is not esoteric: this is MRI data obtained by Sarah Lazar's team at Harvard.
158: But even without meditation, there are practices for returning to bodily awareness:
159: —Daily “body scan”: a few minutes when you simply notice sensations without judgment
160: —Movement with attention: yoga, tai chi, somatic practice—where movement serves as a tool of perception, not just training
161: —Conscious eating: eat without a screen, noticing taste, satiety, pleasure
162: —Breathing practices: observing the breath—the fastest way to bodily presence
163: THE BODY IS AN ALLY, NOT A HINDERER
164: Generational Medicine begins with a fundamental shift in attitude: the body is not a machine that needs to be fixed. This is a living system that constantly communicates with us.
165: Every symptom is a message. Every voltage is a signal. Every discomfort is an invitation to dialogue.
166: The doctor in this system is not a mechanic. He is a translator. He helps the patient decipher his own body language. And to do this, you first need to learn to hear.

180: THE LANGUAGE WE HAVE LEARNED TO UNDERSTAND
181: In 1981, a young doctor named Dean Ornish began an internship at Harvard with a radical idea: What if chronic illnesses were not just malfunctions of a biological machine, but messages that something was deeply wrong in a person's life?
182: The medical community greeted him with skepticism. Years have passed. Ornish published the results of his lifestyle program for coronary heart disease in The Lancet and then in the Journal of the American Medical Association. Coronary atherosclerosis, a disease that was considered irreversible, began to regress in patients who changed their diet, began to meditate and work with a psychologist.
183: The symptom became the door. And not just a malfunction.
185: In the medical dictionary, a symptom is a subjective manifestation of a disease: what the patient feels and reports to the doctor. Pain, fatigue, dizziness, anxiety.
186: But there is another understanding of the symptom, which is closer to the truth of biology: a symptom is the system’s response to an imbalance.
187: Let's take inflammation. In modern medicine, inflammation is often perceived as a problem—it needs to be suppressed with an antibiotic, antihistamine, or corticosteroid. But inflammation is primarily a defensive reaction. Redness, swelling, pain are signals of mobilization: “Here! Integrity is broken here! Need help!
188: When we immediately suppress a symptom, we silence the message. Rarely is right and necessary. But often we lose vital information about what is happening in the system.
189: Israeli medical sociologist Aharon Antonovsky posed a question that changed thinking about health: “Why do people stay healthy despite stress?” He called this approach salutogenesis—the study of the origins of health, as opposed to pathogenesis—the study of the origins of disease.
191: Our body is a system with phenomenally accurate navigation. The problem is that we have forgotten how to read a map.
192: Let's look at a few examples.
193: Chronic fatigue. Standard medicine first excludes organic causes (anemia, hypothyroidism, oncology), and if there are none, it often tells the patient: “You are healthy, you need to rest.” An integrative view will ask: What in this person's life requires so much energy that the system has to turn off "drive mode"? Not getting enough sleep? Chronically overloaded? Has it been a long time since he lives what he considers important? Suppresses some kind of truth, what does the energy go to?
194: Chronic back pain. A study published in The Lancet in 2018 found that in most cases of chronic back pain, there is no correlation between the degree of “visible” changes on MRI (hernias, protrusions) and the intensity of the pain. Many people with hernias have no pain. Many with severe pain have “normal” MRIs.
195: Back pain was found to be closely associated with psychological distress, feelings of helplessness, fear of movement, and job satisfaction. The body “spoke”—just not what the doctors thought.
196: Recurrent infections. A person who suffers from ARVI 6-8 times a year may hear from a therapist: “Your immunity is reduced.” But the question is: why is it reduced? Lack of sleep? Chronic stress? Poor nutrition? Or a chronic feeling of helplessness and lack of meaning in life? All of these factors have a measurable effect on immune function. The symptom—immunodeficiency—is the final expression of a whole system of overloads.
197: ALEXITHYMIA: WHEN THE WORDS CAN'T BE FOUND
198: In 1973, psychiatrist Peter Sifneos described a phenomenon he called alexithymia (from the Greek a—“without,” lexis—“word,” thymos—“emotion”): the inability to recognize and describe one’s own emotions.
199: People with high levels of alexithymia are unable to say “I am anxious” or “I am sad.” They can only say: “My chest hurts” or “I don’t feel well.” An emotional experience that cannot find a word is converted into a bodily symptom.
200: This is not weakness or fiction. This is a limitation of the neurobiological system for processing emotions. And this limitation has measurable consequences: Research consistently shows that high alexithymia is associated with a higher risk of psychosomatic disorders, chronic pain and cardiovascular disease.
201: Emotion recognition training—literally—reduces the frequency of doctor visits in patients with functional disorders.
203: Antonovsky introduced the concept of “Sense of Coherence”—a person’s inner conviction that:
204: 1. The world is understandable and predictable (comprehensibility)
205: 2. He has the resources to cope (manageability)
206: 3. Life has meaning and is worth the effort (meaningfulness)
207: He showed in large samples that a sense of connectedness is a powerful predictor of health. People with a high sense of coherence get sick less often, recover faster, and cope better with chronic diseases.
208: This means: meaning is not a luxury, not a philosophical category. It is a physiological resource that directly affects the functioning of the immune system, neuroendocrine regulation and inflammatory processes.
209: A symptom in this belief system is not just a breakdown. This is a signal of a breakdown in connectivity. That something in a person’s life has become incomprehensible, uncontrollable or meaningless.
210: HOW TO READ A SYMPTOM: QUESTIONS FOR THE DOCTOR AND PATIENT
211: The integrative approach does not propose to replace biomedical diagnostics, but to expand it. Next to the questions “what hurts” and “since when”, ask questions of a different level:
212: —What was happening in your life during the period when the symptom appeared?
213: —Is there something that you have known for a long time, but don’t do? Or something you do even though you know you shouldn’t?
214: -If a symptom told you something, what could it be?
215: —When a symptom intensifies, what happens in life? When does it weaken?
216: —What would change in your life if the symptom went away?
217: The last question is one of the most diagnostically valuable. Sometimes the patient discovers that the symptom performs an important function: it gives permission to rest, protects against unwanted demands, and attracts the attention of loved ones. This is not manipulation—it is an unconscious adaptation of the system. And to understand it means to find a real solution.

226: CHAPTER 5. MAN AS A SYSTEM OF RELATIONSHIPS
231: In 1987, researchers conducted an experiment that will go down in medical history. The two groups of breast cancer patients were given the same chemotherapy treatment. But one group additionally received weekly group psychotherapy sessions—they met, talked, and supported each other.
232: Ten years later, researchers checked the data. Patients in the psychotherapy group lived on average twice as long.
233: This study by David Spiegel shocked the oncology community. How can talking affect cancer survival? The answer required a new language—the language of systems.
234: A SYSTEM, NOT A COLLECTION OF ORGANS
235: The reductionist approach to medicine—the study of parts—gave us antibiotics, vaccines, surgery, molecular oncology. These are real victories that have saved billions of lives. But it has a fundamental limitation: the parts do not explain the whole.
236: A neuron is just a neuron. But 86 billion neurons in the most complex connections give rise to consciousness, a phenomenon that is not contained in any single neuron.
237: An immune system cell is just a cell. But the immune system as a whole has memory, learns, distinguishes between its own and someone else’s, and makes decisions.
238: Physicist Philip Anderson called this principle “More is Different”: at each level of organization of complex systems, properties arise that do not exist at the level below.
239: That is why “treating an organ” is a fundamentally incomplete task. A person is an emergent system: his health occurs at the intersection of biological, psychological, social and environmental levels, and cannot be understood only through biology.
241: In 1975, psychologist Robert Ader accidentally discovered that the immune system is susceptible to psychological conditioning—a conditioned response.
242: He gave the rats saccharin along with an immunosuppressive drug. Then he gave me only saccharin—without the drug. The rats' immune systems were suppressed. The conditioned reflex worked where no one expected: in immune cells. The immune system's "brain" responded to the signal—the taste of saccharin—the same way the brain reacts to a memory.
243: This discovery revolutionized the idea of ​​the independence of the immune system from the psyche. It turned out they speak the same language.
244: Today, psychoneuroimmunoendocrinology (PNIE) is an established scientific discipline that describes a continuous dialogue between:
249: Each system talks to all the others. Constantly. Through molecules—neuropeptides, cytokines, hormones, neurotransmitters.
250: ALLOSTASIS: HOW THE BODY MAINTAINS BALANCE
251: Physiologist Walter Cannon in the 1930s introduced the concept of “homeostasis”—the body’s desire to maintain a constant internal environment. Blood pH, body temperature, glucose levels are all regulated within a narrow range.
252: But in 1988, Peter Sterling and Joseph Ayer proposed a more precise concept: allostasis—maintaining stability through change. Unlike homeostasis, allostasis describes predictive adaptation: the system does not simply return to “norm”, it predicts needs and reconfigures in advance.
253: Bruce McEwen expanded on this idea by introducing the concept of “allostatic load”—the accumulated wear and tear from years of adaptive effort. This is the price of chronic stress: the cardiovascular system, immune system, and metabolism slowly wear out from constant reconfiguration.
254: Allostatic load is measurably related to:
260: This is why stress kills. Not instantly. Slowly, through the system.
261: EXAMPLE: CHAIN ​​OF RELATIONS IN A REAL PATIENT
262: Let's consider a case. A forty-year-old woman comes in with complaints of: chronic fatigue, joint pain, recurring infections, depression, digestive problems.
263: Specialists see each differently: rheumatologist—joints, gastroenterologist—intestines, immunologist—immunodeficiency, psychiatrist—depression.
264: The integrative view sees the system:
265: 1. Chronic stress (divorce two years ago + difficult work conflict) activates the HPA axis → chronically elevated cortisol.
266: 2. Cortisol suppresses immune function → repeated infections.
267: 3. Cortisol disrupts the permeability of the intestinal barrier → dysbiosis, inflammation.
268: 4. Intestinal inflammation penetrates the brain through cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) → depression (inflammatory model of depression).
269: 5. Depression reduces motivation to move → physical inactivity increases inflammation in the joints.
270: 6. Increasing pain impairs sleep → sleep disturbance increases cortisol → the circle closes.
271: This is one system in one person. Treating each symptom separately is treating the effects, not the causes.
272: NETWORKED MEDICINE: THE SCIENCE OF INTERNATIONALITY
273: Physicist Albert-László Barabási and his team applied network theory to disease biology. They showed that diseases are not isolated failures of one gene or organ. Diseases are patterns in a biological network.
274: They created a “disizome”—a disease map in which every two diseases are connected by a line if they have a common genetic component. It turned out that diseases form clusters. Autoimmune diseases—in one cluster. Metabolic - in another. Neurodegenerative - in the third. But many diseases belong to several clusters at once.
275: This means: combinations of diseases are not an accident. These are structural patterns in a single biological system.
276: And this means: to treat effectively means to work with the system, and not with isolated points in it.

285: CHAPTER 6. EPIGENETICS AND RESPONSIBILITY FOR YOUR OWN HEALTH
291: In the 1940s, an event occurred in the Netherlands that decades later became one of the most important natural experiments in the history of medicine. In the winter of 1944–1945, the Nazi occupation doomed the country to severe famine—the so-called “hunger winter” that claimed the lives of more than 20,000 people.
292: Children conceived or born during this famine grew up. Thirty, forty, fifty years later, researchers discovered something startling: these people had a significantly higher risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease and schizophrenia. Moreover, those who experienced hunger in the first trimester of their mother’s pregnancy had different risks than those who experienced it in the third.
293: But the most surprising thing: the children of these people—grandchildren of starving mothers—also showed increased metabolic risks. The famine left a biological trace across three generations.
294: It's not genetics. The genome has not changed. His expression changed.
296: Think of the genome as a vast library—23,000 books (genes), each containing instructions for making proteins. Most books are closed most of the time. Epigenetics is a cataloging system that decides which books to open today and which to put on the far shelf.
297: The main tools of this system:
298: DNA methylation is the addition of methyl groups (CH₃) to certain sections of DNA, especially to the so-called CpG islands. A methylated gene usually “silences”—its information is not read. This is a normal mechanism: this is how genes that are needed only in neurons are “silent” in pancreatic cells, and vice versa.
299: Histone acetylation is a change in the proteins (histones) around which DNA is wound. Acetylated histones “unfold” DNA, making the gene accessible for reading. Deacetylation—“collapses,” making the gene inaccessible.
300: Both systems are sensitive to the environment: nutrition, stress, physical activity, sleep, toxins, social relationships.
302: In 2008, Dean Ornish's team published a groundbreaking study in The Lancet Oncology. Patients with low-risk prostate cancer were divided into two groups. One received standard observation. Another spent three months in an intensive program: plant-based diet, moderate aerobic exercise, stress management (meditation, yoga), group support.
303: In the second group, the expression of more than 500 genes changed. Cancer-protective genes were turned on. Genes associated with inflammation and tumor growth were turned off. Three months of lifestyle rewrote the biochemistry of the cell.
304: In 2013, another Ornish study found that the same program lengthened telomeres by 10% over five years. This is unprecedented: telomeres only shorten with aging. The lifestyle reversed this trend.
305: Dasatinib, one of the most expensive oncology drugs, costs thousands of dollars a month. Healthy eating, meditation and walking are incomparably cheaper. And biologically no less powerful.
307: An important stop must be made here.
308: When we say that lifestyle influences gene expression and disease risk, some are tempted to conclude: “So people themselves are to blame for their illnesses.”
309: This is wrong. And this is a dangerous misconception. Disease, like everything else in the body, is a protective (adaptive) reaction. The disease protects against imminent death. Additional strength is needed for the body to remove the adaptive reaction in the form of illness. Moreover, in each specific case these are different threats. In order for the body to remove the adaptive reaction in the form of a disease, different amounts of force are needed for different cases with the same diagnosis. The patient is not to blame - he is not aware of his destructive desires, which he is unable to fulfill, and if he takes the path of fulfillment, he will quickly kill himself.
310: Epigenetics teaches not guilt, but opportunity. It shows: biology is plastic. What appears to be "given" can be changed. But it also shows that our epigenetic patterns are shaped by conditions, many of which we did not choose—the hunger of our grandmothers, the stress of our mothers, the poverty of our childhoods, environmental toxicity.
311: Responsibility is not guilt for the past. This is the ability to influence the future.
312: People of low socioeconomic status have a significantly higher allostatic load—not because they are “irresponsible,” but because the replacement of enlightenment with education, the lack of training in awareness of the unconscious, the chronic stress of poverty, housing insecurity, poor nutrition and limited access to health care is literally written into the genome. Health policies that talk only about “personal responsibility” ignore half the equation.
314: However, the data is clear: within the limits of our biography and our capabilities, much can be changed.
315: Breath. A study published in the journal Epigenetics found that 20 minutes of exercise altered DNA methylation in muscle cells after just a few hours. Long-term training consistently changes the epigenetic profile.
316: Dream. Chronic sleep deprivation alters the expression of more than 700 genes. Including genes associated with inflammation, immune function and stress response.
317: Nutrition. Improper nutrition promotes the proliferation of bacteria and parasites, which directly affects the purposeful behavior of the owner. A number of nutrients directly affect epigenetic marks. Folic acid, vitamin B12, methionine are involved in the production of methyl groups. A deficiency of these substances disrupts normal methylation. Polyphenols (from berries, for example) affect the activity of histone-modifying enzymes.
318: Stress management. A study by Dasuk et al found that relaxation response practice altered the expression of 2,209 genes, including genes associated with inflammation, energy metabolism, and stress response.

327: CHAPTER 7. WISDOM OF THE BODY AND INTELLIGENCE OF THE CELL
332: In 1985, neuroscientist Candace Pert made a discovery that changed her professional life—and our understanding of the body. While researching opioid receptors (for which she, by the way, did not receive a well-deserved Nobel Prize, although the discovery was revolutionary), she discovered something amazing: receptors for neuropeptides—the molecules of mood and emotion—are not only found in the brain.
333: They are everywhere. In the cells of the immune system. In intestinal cells. In the heart muscle. In the skin.
334: Pert articulated a message that the medical community was slow to absorb: “The body is the unconscious mind.” Each cell doesn't just receive commands from the brain. She processes information herself, reacts to the environment, and “makes decisions.”
335: This is not a metaphor. This is molecular reality.
337: One cell is not a brick in the wall. This is a highly complex machine with built-in intelligence.
338: Sensory. The cell is equipped with thousands of receptors—molecular antennas tuned to specific signals. One receptor responds to insulin, another to adrenaline, a third to neuropeptide Y (associated with appetite), and a fourth to oxytocin (“relationship hormone”). Each signal changes the behavior of the cell: changes gene expression, activates or inhibits enzymes, triggers division or apoptosis.
339: Memory. Immune cells remember pathogens they encountered decades ago. This is precisely the principle of vaccination. But cellular memory is broader: epigenetic marks keep a record of the environmental conditions that the cell (and its predecessors) experienced in the past.
340: Communication. Cells constantly communicate with each other: through direct contacts (gap junctions), through paracrine signals (substances that act on neighbors), through endocrine signals (hormones traveling through the blood), through neurotransmitters, through exosomes (tiny bubbles with RNA that cells “send” to each other like letters).
341: Solution. The cell “chooses”: to divide or not, to move or stay in place, to produce this or that protein, to start inflammation or to slow it down. This is not a conscious choice, but it is an adaptive response to information.
343: Neuroscientist Antonio Damasio observed patients with damage to the ventromedial prefrontal cortex. Outwardly, they seemed normal: intelligence was preserved, memory was functioning, logic was impeccable. But they were unable to make normal life decisions: they chose disastrous business strategies, were unable to maintain relationships, and destroyed their own lives—despite their fully preserved ability to think rationally.
344: What were they missing? Bodily signals.
345: Damasio proposed the somatic marker hypothesis: we make decisions not only with logic, but also with the help of bodily sensations—micro-changes in the body (heartbeat, muscle tension, sensations in the stomach) that accompany the memory or imagination of a particular situation. These markers—quick, nonverbal—guide choices before the mind can formulate arguments.
346: “Intuition” is not mysticism. This is accumulated experience stored in the body and read faster than conscious thinking.
348: Until recently, fascia—the connective tissue that envelops muscles, organs, and bones—was considered something of a wrapping material for the body. Functionally insignificant.
349: Today we know that fascia is one of the largest sensory systems in the body. More mechanoreceptors (pressure and movement sensors) have been found in fascia than in muscles. It is richly innervated by autonomic nerve fibers. It contains cells of the immune system.
350: Robert Schleip, one of the leading researchers on fascia, showed that fascia is capable of contracting independently of muscle activity—it has its own “muscle” activity, regulated by the autonomic nervous system.
351: Fascia reacts to mechanical stress, emotional state, and temperature. Chronic fascial tension is not just physical “stiffness.” This is a record of experience: situations in which the body shrank from fear, prepared for a blow, froze from grief. The body remembers—literally, in the tissue.
353: The classic idea of ​​the nervous system: neurons transmit signals through synapses—point contacts. Fast, accurate, targeted.
354: But this is only half the truth.
355: In parallel, there is a “liquid” nervous system: neuropeptides—chains of amino acids (endorphins, substance P, neuropeptide Y, oxytocin, VIP and dozens of others)—are released into the intercellular fluid and travel diffusely, finding receptors in a variety of tissues.
356: It is a slow, panoramic communication system. It regulates mood, pain, immune response, appetite, sexuality, social connections.
357: Candice Pert calls this a “psychosomatic network”—a decentralized neurochemical system in which there is no control center. Information is processed distributedly: in the intestines, in immune cells, in the skin—everywhere.
358: “The mind,” said Pert, “is not in the brain. The mind is an information system distributed throughout the body."
359: PRACTICE: HOW TO HEAR THE WISDOM OF THE BODY
360: If the body has such a rich information system, a practical question arises: how to connect to it?
362: Practice interoception. Regular practice of a body scan—methodically moving your attention across different parts of the body—increases interoceptive awareness and is associated with improved emotion regulation.
363: Somatic practices. Bodywork modalities—osteopathy, Peter Levine Somatic Experience, bioenergetics, Feldenkrais, dance/movement therapy—use movement and touch as a gateway to information stored in the body.
364: Working with breathing. Breathing is the only autonomous function of the body that we can voluntarily control. This makes it a bridge between the conscious and the unconscious, between the somatic and the mental.
365: “Trust your guts” - that’s what people say. Science now explains why this is—quite literally—good advice.

375: CHAPTER 8. THE SUBSTANCE OF LIFE: ANCIENT AND MODERN CONCEPTS
381: There is a question that all the great medical traditions of the world have asked themselves. A question that the West in the 19th century considered unscientific and put aside. And which in the 21st century returns again—in other words, through other tools, but with the same persistence.
383: What distinguishes the living from the dead? Not at the level of chemical reactions—we can describe them. And at the level of the organizing principle that makes an organism out of molecules, and a person out of organs?
384: It was called differently in different cultures. Prana—in Ayurveda. Qi (Ki)—in Chinese medicine. Pneuma - among the Greeks. Vitalis—in Western natural philosophy. Orgon - from Wilhelm Reich. Rukh - in the Arab medical tradition.
385: This is not a coincidence. Independently of each other, in different parts of the world, different civilizations came to the same conclusion: in a living body there is something more than the totality of its parts. Organizing force. Guiding flow.
386: PRANA, QI, PNEUMA: WHAT IS THE ESSENCE
387: The Ayurvedic concept of prana is not simply "air" or "breath", although the word comes from the root "pra-an" - to breathe. Prana is the primary life force that permeates all living things. In the body it moves along nadis—subtle channels, analogues of meridians. Disturbance in the flow of prana is the root cause of disease. Flow restoration is a treatment.
388: The Chinese concept of Qi is the closest analogue. Qi is what flows along the meridians (经络), what connects organs with the surface of the body (hence acupuncture), what circulates in nature and in humans according to a single principle. Disease is a disturbance in the flow or balance of Qi.
389: Greek pneuma (πνεῦμα) in Galen is a subtle vital spirit that is produced in the heart, carries vital force and is distributed throughout the body through the blood and nerves.
390: What's in common? All of these concepts describe the vital force as something that moves, organizes, sustains life, and is disrupted by illness. Different languages ​​- one intuition.
392: When vitalism—the doctrine of the life force—was officially supplanted by mechanistic biology in the 19th century, the question seemed closed. Life is chemistry. Nothing more.
393: But 21st century biology poses questions that pure chemistry cannot answer.
394: ATP: the molecular "currency" of life. Mitochondria—organelles inside every cell—produce ATP (adenosine triphosphate), the universal carrier of energy. Every second, our body produces and consumes approximately as much ATP as it weighs. This is a continuous flow of energy that supports everything from protein synthesis to muscle function.
395: Mitochondria are not just “energy stations.” They control apoptosis (programmed cell death), generate free radicals as signaling molecules, and are involved in inflammation, aging, and neurodegeneration. Mitochondrial diseases are diseases of the life force at the molecular level.
396: Biophotons: cell light. In the 1970s and 80s, German biophysicist Fritz-Albert Poppa discovered that living cells emit ultraweak photons—biophotons. This is not a random glow: the intensity and patterns of biophoton emission correlate with the state of the cell, its stress, division, and apoptosis.
397: Poppa suggested that biophotons could be part of a system of intracellular and intercellular communication. This hypothesis remains debatable, but the phenomenon of biophoton emission itself is an experimentally confirmed fact.
398: Morphogenetic fields. Biologist Rupert Sheldrake proposed a concept that has been met with great skepticism by academic science: that living systems are organized not only by genetic code, but also by “morphogenetic fields”—intangible patterns that guide development. This is an extreme position. But the question of how different organs develop from identical cells with the same genome still does not have a complete explanation only in terms of biochemistry.
400: One of the most intriguing areas of modern biology is bioelectrics. Michael Levine of Tufts University has shown that cells and tissues generate electrical fields that literally “tell” other cells what to do.
402: —Changing the bioelectric field of planarians (flatworms) completely changed the shape of the regenerating organism: the worms grew a head instead of a tail.
403: —Bioelectric signals control the location of organs in embryonic development.
404: —Bioelectrical abnormalities precede visible structural changes in cancer.
405: It is not energy in the metaphorical sense. These are measurable electrical potentials that carry morphological information. And this is modern scientific language to describe what ancient medicine called the “flow of life force.”
407: What can be honestly said about the “substance of life”?
408: First: life is truly organized by principles that cannot be reduced to a simple sum of molecules. Emergence, self-organization, bioelectrics, information networks—all this is real and measurable.
409: Second: ancient concepts were intuitive maps of reality. Imperfect. But they point to something important—that life is a continuous flow, an organization, a dynamic balance.
410: Thirdly: honest science knows how to recognize the limits of its knowledge. Just because we can't explain it right now doesn't mean it doesn't exist. But this also does not mean that any explanation is correct.
411: Integrative medicine takes a balanced position: it respects traditional systems as the concentrated clinical observation of millennia—and at the same time demands evidence for every practice that claims a place in the clinic.
412: Wisdom and science are not enemies. They talk about different facets of the same mystery.

420: CHAPTER 9. CONSCIOUSNESS OF A DOCTOR: WHO HEALS—THE PERSON OR THE METHOD?
426: In 2008, Ted Kaptchuk of Harvard conducted an experiment that broke several established rules. Patients with irritable bowel syndrome were divided into three groups. The first received sham acupuncture (needles that do not penetrate the skin) in a cold, impersonal environment. The second was the same fictitious acupuncture, but the doctor conducted an extended consultation: he listened carefully, asked questions, and showed concern. The third one is on the waiting list.
427: Result: the second group showed the best therapeutic response. Not because the method was more effective. But because the doctor’s attitude—his attentiveness, warmth, the time he devoted—turned out to be a therapeutic factor in itself.
429: PLACEBO: THE BRAIN AS A PHARMACY
430: The word "placebo" in medicine has acquired a negative connotation: "it's just a placebo" means "it doesn't really work." But this is a deep misconception.
431: The placebo effect is a demonstration that the brain is capable of producing real physiological changes in response to an expectation, belief, or attitude.
432: Neuroscientist Thor Wager showed in an MRI that when a person believes that he is receiving a painkiller (even if it is saline), opioid receptors in his brain are activated and endogenous opioids are released. The pain really decreases. This is not an illusion: it is neurochemistry.
433: Fabrizio Benedetti of the University of Turin—the world's leading placebo researcher—showed that the expectancy effect involves specific neurotransmitter systems: opioid (for pain), dopamine (for Parkinson's disease), cholinergic (for cognitive impairment). Different diseases—different neurochemical placebo mechanisms.
434: This means: what the doctor says, how he says it, what expectations he creates—all of this literally changes the neurochemistry of the patient.
435: NOCEBO: THE DARK SIDE OF THE POWER OF THE WORD
436: If a positive expectation can heal, then a negative one can harm. This is called the nocebo effect.
437: Research shows that patients who are told “this may cause pain” experience more pain. Patients who are told a difficult diagnosis in a harsh, cold manner have a worse prognosis than those who are told the same information with support and perspective.
438: In one study, patients with an aspirin allergy were given aspirin and told it was safe—there was no reaction. They gave a safe substance, saying that it was aspirin; some patients developed an allergic reaction.
439: The doctor's words are not neutral. They are biologically active.
440: “You will live with this for the rest of your life”, “You have a chronic disease that is only progressing”, “At your age this is normal” - such phrases, uttered without taking into account their psychobiological impact, can become part of the disease, not the cure.
442: Physician Ronald Epstein of the University of Rochester has described what he calls “mindful practice.” This is the ability of a doctor to be completely present in contact with a patient: not to think about the next patient, not to plan dinner, not to mentally flip through protocols. To be here. Now. With this specific person.
443: Research shows that patients physically feel the difference. When the doctor is truly present, the patient feels heard, understood, and safe. And this is a direct neurobiological effect: activation of the parasympathetic nervous system, release of oxytocin, reduction of cortisol.
444: In a safe environment, the body regulates itself better, responds better to treatment, and recovers faster.
445: WHOM WE HEAL: A DISEASE OR A PERSON
446: Legendary Canadian physician William Osler wrote in 1903: “It is more important to know what kind of a man has a disease than to know what kind of disease a person has.”
447: This is not just a beautiful maxim. This is the software principle of medicine. Let us remember that illness is an adaptive reaction and the same illness can be caused by different unconscious aspirations.
448: The same disease unfolds differently in different people. One person diagnosed with type 2 diabetes completely normalizes their levels within five years through lifestyle changes. The other one—with the same diagnosis, the same medications—continues to progress. The difference is in the psychological environment, in beliefs about one’s illness, in social support, in the meaning that a person puts into his health.
449: The mood that the doctor creates in the patient is an external force that can be added to the patient’s internal strength. A study by Cornell's John Aronson found that patients whose doctors expressed optimism about treatment had better clinical outcomes than patients of the same doctors who expressed neutrality or pessimism. The doctor's prognosis influences the patient's prognosis.
451: In all cultures of the world—from Siberian shamans to Egyptian priests, from Ayurvedic vaidyas to the Greek Asclepiads—the figure of the healer possessed certain qualities: knowledge, presence, the ability to create a healing space.
452: This is no coincidence. Perhaps there is a neurobiological reality behind this: human presence—warm, attentive, non-judgmental—activates in another person a social safety system (according to Porges: the ventral vagal branch), which literally creates the physiological conditions for recovery.
453: Technology can do a lot. An AI algorithm can diagnose melanoma more accurately than most dermatologists. MRI sees what the hand cannot feel. But to create a space of trust, to hear a person in his pain, to say: “I am here with you”—this remains an exclusively human function.

461: CHAPTER 10. INTEGRITY AS A SCIENTIFIC CATEGORY
466: In 1972, Nobel laureate physicist Philip Anderson published an essay in the journal Science with the provocative title “More is Different.” He argued that when simple components are assembled into complex systems, fundamentally new properties emerge that cannot be predicted or explained by studying the components individually.
467: The water is wet. But neither hydrogen nor oxygen are wet. Wetness is an emergent property that occurs only with a certain organization of molecules.
468: Consciousness is something. But no individual neuron is conscious. Consciousness arises from the organization of 86 billion neurons into a highly complex dynamic network.
469: Life is something. But carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen, taken individually, are simply elements. Life arises from their specific organization.
470: Integrity is not a metaphor or a philosophical category. This is a scientific principle: complex systems have properties that their parts do not have.
472: Reductionism—the method of explanation through decomposition into parts—has been and remains the most powerful tool of science. It was he who made it possible to decipher the genome, understand the molecular mechanisms of cancer, and create vaccines and antibiotics.
474: Physiologist Denis Noble described it this way in his book The Music of Life: Imagine trying to understand a Beethoven symphony by studying the vibrations of individual molecules in the air. You will receive the most accurate physical description of sound waves. But you won't understand the symphony. A symphony is a pattern, an organization, a meaning. It exists at a level that is inaccessible to molecular analysis.
475: Human health is a symphony. Reductionism describes individual notes. Integrative medicine tries to hear the whole music.
477: In 2002, biologist Hiroki Kitano published a seminal article in Science, “Systems Biology: A Brief Overview.” He described a new scientific discipline that studies not individual genes or proteins, but networks of interactions.
478: Systems biology builds mathematical models of biological networks: genetic regulatory networks, metabolic networks, signaling cascades. It shows how the properties of a system—its stability, flexibility, adaptability—depend on the topology of the network, and not just on the properties of individual elements.
480: Resilience through redundancy. Biological systems have numerous redundant pathways. If one path is blocked, the other takes over its function. This explains why knocking out one gene rarely causes disaster: the system compensates.
481: Fragility in critical areas. Despite all their stability, biological networks have “hubs”—highly loaded nodes, damage to which leads to system collapse. TP53 (tumor suppressor gene) is one of these hubs: it is mutated in more than 50% of all cancers.
482: Nonlinear interactions. Small changes in one place can cause large effects in another—the “butterfly effect” in biology. This explains why diseases often develop not gradually, but spasmodically, after a long period of latent changes. unattainable aspirations give rise to small changes, which at a critical moment can turn on the disease as an adaptive reaction.
484: Physicist Albert-László Barabási—one of the originators of network theory—applied the mathematics of complex networks to medicine. His team built an “interactome”—a map of all known protein interactions in a human cell. And then she overlaid disease data on it.
485: It turned out that genes associated with one disease are usually located in one “region” of the protein network (disease module). Diseases whose modules overlap often coexist in the same patient.
486: This discovery has a practical consequence: a drug that affects one node in the network will also affect connected nodes. Hence the side effects. But here comes the opportunity: targeting the right network node can treat multiple diseases at once. Commercial medicine also knows this: having corrected one thing, it spoils another, for the correction of which it will require another payment. The solution is total and long-term monitoring of therapeutic interventions, which will give a complete picture of what is happening not only to specialists in integrative medicine, but also to potential patients of commercial medicine.
487: Network medicine is the future of pharmacology. Transition from commercial pharmacology to integrative pharmacology. Dissemination of a systems view of disease.
489: A non-obvious but important conclusion from the science of complex systems: health is high complexity.
490: Biophysicist Arie Goldberger has shown that healthy physiological signals—heart rate, breathing, bioelectrical activity in the brain—show a highly variable, fractal structure. Illness is a loss of complexity: the rhythm becomes more monotonous, less adaptive.
491: This is counterintuitive: we are used to thinking that health is regularity. But in reality, a healthy heart rate is not a metronome. It is a rich, variable, unpredictable pattern that can instantly adapt to change. The unconscious desire for an unattainable goal is the main reason for such phenomena. Creating an irresistible and long-term mood not only forces the body to readjust itself, but also suppresses signals from the outside and inside that require answers.
492: Loss of this complexity precedes disease—and may serve as an early diagnostic marker.
494: What does integrity mean in a specific clinical context?
495: This means: asking not only the question “what hurts”, but also “how this person lives.” Not only “what are the markers of inflammation”—but also “what is inflamed in his life.”
496: This means: seeing a symptom not as the final point of diagnosis, but as an entry into the system. And move through the system—up (to the causes of causes) and down (to specific mechanisms).
497: This means: treating one is not treating the whole. Removing a symptom without changing the system is removing the alarm without correcting the fire.
498: Integrity is not mysticism. This is a rigorous scientific principle that requires new tools and new education. Medicine of the 21st century is gradually learning this language.

507: CHAPTER 11. MODERN PHYSIOLOGY IN THE CONTEXT OF A HOLISTIC APPROACH
513: For many years, physiology described the body as a reactive system: external influence → internal response. It became cold → vasoconstriction. Sugar rose → insulin release. A pathogen has appeared → activation of the immune system.
515: Peter Sterling, a neuroscientist at the University of Pennsylvania, has proposed a fundamentally different model: the body doesn't just react—it predicts. It constantly makes predictions about what will be needed in the near future and prepares in advance.
516: Even before you get out of bed, your cortisol begins to rise—your body “knows” that you are going to be active and prepares energy resources. Even before you eat, the digestive system has already begun secreting—the Pavlovian conditioned reflex—a physiological anticipatory regulation.
517: This is called allostasis—maintaining stability through anticipation rather than just reaction.
518: AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM: CONDUCTOR OF THE BODY
519: If the body is an orchestra, then the autonomic nervous system (ANS) is its conductor. It controls everything that we do not consciously control: heartbeat, digestion, breathing, immune response, vascular tone, and the functioning of internal organs.
520: The ANS is divided into three branches (according to Stephen Porges):
521: Sympathetic nervous system - "gas": mobilizes resources for action (fight or flight). Dilates pupils, increases heart rate, increases blood flow to muscles, and inhibits digestion. Hormonal companion - adrenaline and norepinephrine.
522: The parasympathetic nervous system (dorsal vagal branch) is an “emergency brake”: if it is impossible to fight or escape, it freezes. This is an evolutionarily ancient reaction: “play dead.” Expressed in dissociation, numbness, emotional stupor.
523: The ventral vagal branch (according to Porges, the “social” nervous system) is unique to mammals: it is activated under conditions of safety. Provides social interaction, empathy, play. Regulates facial expression, voice intonation, hearing in the range of human speech.
524: Health is the ability to flexibly switch between these states. The disease is often “stuck” in one mode: chronic sympathetic activation (chronic stress), or chronic dorsal vagal activation (depression, dissociation).
525: HEART RATE VARIABILITY: A WINDOW TO THE NERVOUS SYSTEM
526: One of the most informative markers of the state of the autonomic nervous system is heart rate variability (HRV—Heart Rate Variability).
527: HRV is not your heart rate. This is a variation of the intervals between beats. A healthy heart does not beat like a metronome—there are subtle, ever-changing differences between beats. This variability is a sign of the flexibility of the autonomic nervous system, its ability to adapt to changing conditions.
534: High HRV is a marker of health, adaptive reserve, and emotional flexibility.
535: Important: HRV can be trained. Meditation, conscious breathing, physical exercise, social support—all of this significantly increases HRV.
537: The gastrointestinal tract contains about 500 million neurons—more than the spinal cord. This is the enteric nervous system (CNS, or “second brain”), which is capable of functioning autonomously, without commands from the brain.
538: The CNS produces more than 30 neurotransmitters, including about 95% of all serotonin in the body. Serotonin is not only a “happiness hormone”, it regulates intestinal motility. But it also affects mood: this is why intestinal disturbances are so often accompanied by mood changes and vice versa.
539: The gut-brain connection is bidirectional. The vagus nerve carries information in both directions: 80% of its fibers are from the intestines to the brain. This means that most of the “dialogue” between the gut and the brain is the gut talking to the brain, not the other way around.
540: Hence the concept of the gut-brain axis, which explains the connection between dysbiosis and depression, irritable bowel syndrome and anxiety, intestinal inflammation and cognitive impairment.
542: Traditionally, the nervous system and the hormonal system were viewed as separate systems. Modern physiology sees them as a single neurohumoral system.
543: The hypothalamus—a small region of the brain about the size of a walnut—is the master regulator of neuroendocrine integration. It receives information from the cerebral cortex (thoughts, emotions), from the limbic system (instincts, memory, affects), from internal organs (visceral signals)—and translates this information into hormonal commands.
551: This means: our thoughts and emotions literally control the hormonal system through the hypothalamus. Chronic psychological stress = chronically disrupted hormonal axes = systemic physiological changes.

560: CHAPTER 12. NEUROENDOCRINE-IMMUNE NETWORK: THE LANGUAGE OF THE BODY
565: THE ACCIDENTAL DISCOVERY THAT CHANGED MEDICINE
566: 1974, University of Rochester. Psychologist Robert Ader conducted a standard aversive conditioning experiment in rats. He gave them saccharine-containing water—and at the same time the drug cyclophosphamide, which caused nausea. The rats quickly learned to avoid sweet water: a conditioned reflex was developed.
567: But then something unexpected happened. When Ader gave rats saccharin water without the drug, they began to get sick and die. Not from poisoning. From infections.
568: Ader understood: cyclophosphamide, in addition to causing nausea, is a powerful immunosuppressant. And the conditioned reflex—developed for nausea—took over the immune system. Sugar water, which did not contain a single gram of the drug, suppressed the immune system. A conditioned reflex is like Pavlov’s dog, only in immune cells.
569: The immune system turned out to be capable of learning. And this meant: she is in continuous dialogue with the brain. Psychoneuroimmunology was born.
571: Psychoneuroimmunoendocrinology (PNIE) describes the ongoing molecular dialogue between four body systems:
572: The nervous system speaks to the immune system through:
575: —direct innervation of lymphoid organs (thymus, spleen, lymph nodes)
576: The immune system speaks to the nervous system through:
577: —cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ)—molecular messengers of inflammation that penetrate the blood-brain barrier or transmit signals through the vagus nerve
578: The endocrine system is involved as an intermediary:
582: —sex hormones modulate inflammation (hence the differences between men and women in autoimmune diseases)
584: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis (HPA-axis) is the main pathway for translating psychological stress into physiological changes.
586: 1. The brain perceives a threat (real or imagined)
590: Cortisol is a multifunctional stress hormone. It mobilizes glucose for muscles, suppresses inflammation (short term), enhances cognitive function. In acute stress, this is a life-saving adaptation.
591: With chronic stress, cortisol becomes destructive:
592: —Chronically suppresses immune function → increased vulnerability to infections and cancer
593: —Disturbs neurogenesis in the hippocampus → cognitive impairment, depression
595: —Destroys collagen → premature aging of skin, joints, blood vessels
597: DISEASE BEHAVIOR: HOW IMMUNITY CONTROLS THE BRAIN
598: In the late 1980s, neuroscientist Robert Danzer discovered what he called “sickness behavior.” When the immune system is activated (infection, inflammation), it releases cytokines that enter the brain and cause a specific set of changes:
606: It's not "just weakness." This is an adaptive program: the body forces us to lie down and save resources while the immune system fights the threat.
607: But it also explains the link between chronic inflammation and depression. Inflammatory model of depression: Chronic low-level inflammation (from obesity, poor nutrition, dysbiosis, stress) supports chronic “sickness behavior”—and this looks like depression. Chronic inflammation is found in 30–40% of patients with depression.
609: If the nervous, endocrine and immune systems form a single network, then:
610: 1. Psychological interventions, an unconscious desire for an unattainable (from the point of view of the body’s predictive apparatus) goal, have immunological consequences. Psychotherapy, meditation, social support are not “soft” methods. They change the cytokine profile, NK cell activity, and the level of inflammatory markers.
611: 2. Immunological interventions have psychological consequences. It’s not just propaganda or advertising that creates unattainable goals with subsequent changes in attitude. Chronic inflammation makes you moody, anxious, tired—and reducing inflammation (through nutrition, probiotics, anti-inflammatory nutrients) improves mood. Thus, the disease itself can give rise to a destructive unconscious desire - usually more dangerous, requiring a more powerful adaptive response (more severe illness).
612: 3. You cannot treat one thing without affecting the rest. An antidepressant changes immune function. The immunosuppressant changes your mood. An antibiotic changes the microbiome, and through it, the gut-brain axis.
613: Integrative medicine is medicine that always remembers this network. And he seeks and finds strength that will help the patient overcome an unattainable goal, so that the body removes the adaptive reaction in the form of illness.

622: CHAPTER 13. MICROBIOME AS AN ORGAN OF COMMUNICATION WITH THE WORLD
626: WE ARE NOT WHO WE THINK
627: Take a mirror and look at your reflection. Human? Not really.
628: More precisely, a superorganism. According to the latest estimates by Ron Sander and colleagues (2016), the number of microbial cells in the human body is approximately equal to the number of human cells—about 38 trillion versus 30 trillion. Moreover, the microbial genome—the microbiome—contains 150–400 times more genes than our own.
629: We are not owners and tenants. We are a cooperative organism in which the boundary between “me” and “them” is fundamentally blurred.
630: And it changes everything we think about health.
632: The microbiome is not a passive cohabitant. It is an active organ with many functions:
633: Metabolic functions. Intestinal bacteria ferment dietary fiber, producing short-chain fatty acids (SCFA)—butyrate, propionate, acetate. These molecules nourish intestinal epithelial cells, regulate inflammation, and affect fat and carbohydrate metabolism. Butyrate is an important regulator of gene expression through inhibition of histone deacetylases.
634: Immune functions. 70–80% of the body's immune cells are concentrated in the intestines. The microbiome “trains” the immune system to distinguish its own from someone else’s—precisely in early childhood. Children raised in a sterile environment (caesarean section birth, early use of antibiotics, formula feeding instead of breastfeeding until 9 months, poor nutrition) have a higher risk of autoimmune diseases, allergies, atopic dermatitis.
635: Neurochemical functions. Intestinal bacteria produce neurotransmitters: serotonin (up to 95% of the total), GABA, dopamine, norepinephrine. They synthesize tryptophan, a precursor to serotonin. Through the vagus nerve and endocrine signals, they participate in the regulation of mood, anxiety, and cognitive functions.
636: Protective functions. Normal microflora competes with pathogens for nutrients and attachment sites—this is called “colonization resistance.” Destroying the microbiome with antibiotics opens the way for clostridia, candida and other opportunistic organisms.
638: In 2012, John Crean and Ted Dinan of University College Cork published a review in Nature Reviews Neuroscience that consolidated the accumulated evidence and introduced the concept of the gut-brain axis into widespread use.
639: This axis operates through several parallel channels:
640: -Vagus nerve: direct “telephone line” from the intestines to the brain (80% of the fibers are afferent, that is, from the intestines)
641: —Enteric nervous system (500 million neurons in the intestinal wall)
645: The clinical data are striking. Studies on gnotobiotic (germ-free) mice show that without a microbiome, animals exhibit abnormal behavioral responses to stress, impaired anxiety, and an altered HPA axis. Transplanting the microbiome from anxious mice to calm ones transmits anxiety. And vice versa.
646: In humans: dysbiosis (imbalance of the microbiome) is associated with depression, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia, and autism. Cause-and-effect relationships are still being established, but the associations are stable.
648: Dysbacteriosis—a disruption of the normal composition and function of the microbiome—manifests itself in different parts of the body:
650: The most studied and clinically significant. Decrease in diversity, decrease in the proportion of Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, increase in the proportion of opportunistic Clostridium, Fusobacterium. Associated with: IBS, IBD, obesity, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, depression, autism. Key triggers: antibiotics, highly processed foods, stress, alcohol.
652: The vaginal microbiome of a healthy woman is 70–90% Lactobacillus—a unique situation compared to other niches of the body. Lactobacillus produces lactic acid, which lowers the pH to 3.5–4.5, which protects against pathogens. Vaginal dysbiosis (bacterial vaginosis) is associated with an increased risk of STDs, premature birth, and HIV infection. The situation is no better for men.
654: The oral microbiome of 700+ species normally keeps pathogens at bay. Imbalance is the first step to caries and periodontitis. Periodontitis is associated with systemic diseases: coronary artery disease, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis. Oral bacteria can reach the heart, joints, and brain through the bloodstream.
656: Diet is the main tool. A diet rich in plant fiber (prebiotics) feeds beneficial bacteria. Plant diversity in the diet correlates with microbiome diversity. Fermented foods (fermented cucumbers, zucchini, squash) supply live bacteria.
657: Probiotics—clinical data are moderate but existent. The most studied effects are on: antibiotic-associated diarrhea, IBS, reducing the risk of C. difficile, and some forms of anxiety.
658: Reducing stress. Stress disrupts the intestinal barrier (increases permeability, “leaky gut”), changes the composition of the microbiome through neurohumoral signals.
659:
Avoiding unnecessary antibiotics. One course of antibiotics can alter the microbiome for months. This does not mean abandoning antibiotics when indicated; it means being mindful of their use.

Taking antifungal drugs. With low leukocytes, taking antibacterial antibiotics (in parallel), along with proper nutrition, it is necessary to take antifungal drugs. Nystatin is best, as practice shows, if you take at least five ten-day courses once a month.
