668: CHAPTER 14. METABOLISM AND ENERGY EXCHANGE: ARCHITECTURE OF LIFE
674: MITOCHONDRIA: NOT JUST A POWER PLANT
675: There are between 300 and 2000 mitochondria in every cell in your body. Biology textbooks call them the “powerhouses of the cell.” This is true, but incredibly insufficient.
676: Mitochondria are ancient bacteria that were absorbed into our cells about 1.5 billion years ago. They retained their own DNA (mtDNA), their own ribosomes, their own reproduction cycle. These are not just organelles—they are symbiotic organisms inside our cells.
677: And they do much more than produce ATP.
678: Mitochondria control apoptosis—programmed cell death. They are the ones who decide whether a cell should die when it is damaged or whether it should be allowed to live. Errors in such decisions are the basis of oncogenesis.
679: Mitochondria generate reactive oxygen species (ROS)—free radicals, which in small quantities are signaling molecules and in large quantities cause oxidative stress and DNA damage.
680: Mitochondria are sensors of cellular stress: they respond to nutritional signals, hormones, physical activity, temperature, hypoxia—and adjust the cellular response.
681: Mitochondrial dysfunction underlies: type 2 diabetes mellitus, neurodegenerative diseases (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's), heart failure, chronic fatigue syndrome, decrepitness of the body.
683: How does a cell produce ATP? Three sequential processes:
684: Glycolysis. In the cytoplasm, a molecule of glucose is split into two molecules of pyruvate, producing 2 molecules of ATP. Does not require oxygen—it is an anaerobic pathway.
685: Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle). In mitochondria, pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA, which enters a cycle of chemical reactions producing NADH and FADH₂—“charged” electron carriers. Not much direct ATP is produced, but it creates “fuel” for the next step.
686: Oxidative phosphorylation. On the inner membrane of mitochondria is a respiratory chain of protein complexes. Electrons are transferred along this chain, and the released energy is used to “pump” protons—creating a proton gradient. Protons are returned through ATP synthase, a molecular motor that synthesizes ATP. One motor revolution = 3 ATP molecules. This produces another 34 molecules of ATP from one molecule of glucose.
687: Total from one glucose molecule: 36–38 ATP molecules. Your body produces its weight in ATP every 24 hours.
688: METABOLIC SYNDROME: AN EPIDEMIC OF THE XXI CENTURY
689: Metabolic syndrome is a constellation of disorders: abdominal obesity, dyslipidemia, hypertension, insulin resistance. Together, they multiply the risk of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes.
690: According to WHO, 20–25% of the world's adult population have metabolic syndrome. In some countries—up to 40%.
691: The basis is insulin resistance. Cells stop responding adequately to insulin, and the pancreas is forced to produce more and more of it. Chronic hyperinsulinemia triggers a cascade:
692: —Fat cells grow and produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (adipokines)
695: —Accumulation of fat in the liver → non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)
696: Gunter Hotamisligil from Harvard showed that obesity is an inflammatory disease. In obesity, adipose tissue is infiltrated by macrophages that release TNF-α, IL-6, and monocytes—inflammatory molecules.
698: In 2016, Japanese cell biologist Yoshinori Ohsumi received the Nobel Prize for his discovery of the mechanisms of autophagy.
699: Autophagy (from the Greek “self-eating”) is the process by which a cell disassembles and processes its own damaged components: defective proteins, damaged mitochondria, intracellular pathogens.
708: —age-related decline (one of the mechanisms of aging)
709: Impaired autophagy is associated with: neurodegeneration (accumulation of aggregates of α-synuclein, tau protein, β-amyloid), cancer, infections.
710: Intermittent fasting (16/8, 5:2 and other protocols) activates autophagy, one of the biological mechanisms of its benefits.
712: The connection between metabolism and psyche is bidirectional.
713: Depression disrupts metabolism: it reduces physical activity, changes eating patterns, disrupts sleep—all of which worsens insulin sensitivity.
714: Metabolic disorders cause depression: chronic inflammation (from obesity) → proinflammatory cytokines → neuroinflammation → depression.
715: Mitochondrial dysfunction is a common denominator: disruption of brain cell energy underlies both bipolar disorder and depression and PTSD.
716: This means: metabolic health is not just physical health. This is the foundation of mental health too.

724: CHAPTER 15. PSYCHONEUROIMUNOLOGY: THE SCIENCE OF THE UNITY OF SOUL, MIND AND BODY
730: For many decades, the immune system was considered completely autonomous—an “army” independent of the command of the brain. This seemed logical: after all, it works both during anesthesia, and in sleep, and in the absence of consciousness.
731: But this idea collapsed in the 1970s. Robert Ader showed that the immune system is subject to conditioning. Hugo Besidowsky demonstrated a bidirectional molecular connection: activated immune cells release cytokines that alter the electrophysiological activity of hypothalamic neurons. The immune system spoke to the brain—in real time, with molecules.
732: Psychoneuroimmunology (PNIE) is the scientific discipline that studies this dialogue. Its key thesis: the psyche, nervous system and immune system constitute a single integrated system. Separating them is like studying the cardiovascular system without knowing that the heart is connected to the vessels.
733: STRESS AND IMMUNITY: WHAT THE DATA SAYS
734: One of the most elegant studies at PNIE was conducted by Sheldon Cohen of Carnegie Mellon University. His team measured the psychological stress levels of volunteers and then deliberately injected them with cold viruses (with the participants' written consent). Result: the higher the initial psychological stress, the higher the likelihood of getting sick.
735: But an important detail: it was not acute stress that predicted illness, but chronic stress—lasting more than a month. Short-term stress, on the contrary, sometimes increased the immune response. This is consistent with the principle of hormesis: small stress adapts, large and chronic stress destroys. This is also consistent with the principle of illness as an adaptive response to an unconscious desire (prolonged) for an unattainable goal.
736: A meta-analysis by Segerström and Miller (2004), covering 30 years of research and almost 300 papers, found:
737: —Acute short-term stress may enhance certain aspects of the immune response
738: —Chronic stress suppresses both cellular and humoral immunity
739:
—The most destructive: long-term interpersonal conflicts, caring for a seriously ill relative, social isolation.
Author's observation: these same situations are highly likely to contain unresolved unconscious aspirations - which is consistent with the concept developed in the epilogue of this book, but goes beyond the data of the meta-analysis itself.
741: "Emotion" sounds abstract. But every emotion is a specific molecular event.
742: Fear = release of adrenaline, norepinephrine, cortisol → constriction of peripheral blood vessels, increased heart rate, inhibition of digestion, activation of the immune system (short-term).
743: Sadness and grief = chronic activation of the HPA axis → immunosuppression. This is why people often get sick after the loss of a loved one.
744: Joy and laughter = release of endorphins, decrease in cortisol, activation of NK cells (natural killer cells - the first line of defense against viruses and cancer cells).
745: Love and social connection = release of oxytocin → decreased inflammatory markers, improved immune function. Holt-Lunstad meta-analysis: People with strong social ties have a 50% higher chance of survival (all conditions).
746: Forgiveness = decreased cardiovascular reactivity, decreased cortisol levels. Toussaint's research has shown that the ability to forgive is associated with better heart health.
748: If psychological stress suppresses immunity, then psychological interventions should restore it. And the data confirms this.
749: Meditation. Richard Davidson Study (Wisconsin, 2003): An eight-week mindfulness program increased antibody activity after flu vaccination compared to a control group.
750: Psychotherapy. Kathleen Andersen (Ohio, 1998): Psychosocial interventions in women with breast cancer improved NK cell profiles and prolonged survival.
751: A letter about emotions. James Pennebaker: 15-20 minutes of daily writing about deep emotions (for 4 days) reduced the frequency of doctor visits, improved immune function, and accelerated wound healing.
752: Social support. Reduces mortality in cancer and cardiovascular diseases, accelerates recovery after surgery. Biological mechanism: decrease in cortisol, activation of oxytocin pathways.
754: One of the most important PNIE discoveries of the last decade: chronic low-level inflammation is a common pathway of most chronic diseases.
755: Cardiovascular disease—inflammation of the vascular wall. Type 2 diabetes is inflammation of adipose tissue. Depression—neuroinflammation. Oncology—inflammatory tumor microenvironment. Neurodegeneration—inflammation of neuroglia. Metabolic syndrome is systemic chronic inflammation.
756: And all these conditions have common triggers: chronic stress, dysbiosis, poor nutrition, physical inactivity, social isolation, sleep disturbance.
757: PNIE explains why "psychological" factors are medical. Because the psyche is not a separate system. It is part of a biological organism.

772: 2017 The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Geoffrey Hall, Michael Rosbash and Michael Young for their discovery of the molecular mechanisms of circadian rhythms.
773: Their work on fruit flies (Drosophila) revealed the elegant molecular mechanism of the internal clock: PER and TIM proteins accumulate at night, block their own production, are destroyed during the day—and the cycle repeats with a period of exactly 24 hours.
774: This mechanism—with the same molecules, with the same feedback logic—turned out to be universal: from flies to mice to humans. Almost every cell in our body has its own molecular clock, synchronized by the main oscillator—the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus.
775: Life is rhythmic at the molecular level. And disruption of this rhythm is a disease.
777: The circadian rhythm (from the Latin circa - “about”, dies - “day”) is a biological cycle with a period of about 24 hours, which regulates almost all physiological processes:
778: Metabolism: insulin sensitivity is maximum in the morning, minimum in the evening. The same food eaten in the evening causes a greater increase in blood sugar than in the morning.
779: Immunity: immune activity is higher at night, attacks by bacteria and viruses are most dangerous at certain times of the day. The timing of vaccination affects the immune response.
780: Oncogenesis: DNA repair is most active at night. Circadian rhythm disturbance in shift workers is a proven carcinogenic factor (IARC, 2007).
781: Cognitive functions: concentration, reaction speed, working memory—all have daily peaks and valleys that vary individually depending on chronotype.
782: Cardiovascular system: heart attacks and strokes are most common in the early morning hours (6–10 am)—when cortisol and adrenaline levels increase and fibrinolytic activity is minimal.
784: Till Rönneberg from Munich introduced the concept of “social jetlag”—a discrepancy between a person’s biological clock and his social schedule.
785: A study of 65,000 participants found that most people wake up 1-2 hours earlier than their biological optimum on weekdays. On weekends, they “get enough sleep”—shifting the rhythm back. This is a chronic mini-jet lag that occurs weekly.
787: —Social jetlag of 1 hour increases the risk of obesity by 33%
788: —Associated with increased consumption of alcohol, caffeine, tobacco
790: —Reduces academic performance in schoolchildren (this is one of the scientific justifications for later start of classes)
791: In adolescents, the biological clock is physiologically shifted 2 hours ahead compared to adults. Starting school early at 7-8 am means chronic sleep deprivation for most teenagers.
793: Chronotype—an individual version of the circadian rhythm—is determined genetically. Twin studies show that 50% of chronotype variation is hereditary.
794: “Larks” (“morning” chronotype): peak activity in the morning, early falling asleep.
795: “Owls” (chronotype “evening”): peak activity in the evening, late fall asleep.
797: Important: chronotype is not a matter of discipline. This is biology. “Night owls” cannot “just go to bed early”—their melatonin begins to be released 2–4 hours later than that of “larks.”
798: In a world organized around early morning schedules, night owls live in a chronic mismatch between biology and social demands. Rönneberg's study found that night owls, on average, have a higher risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, depression and anxiety.
799: CHRONOPHARMACOLOGY: THE TIME OF TAKEN MEDICATIONS MATTER
800: If physiology is rhythmic, then pharmacology must be rhythmic. This is called chronopharmacology.
802: Statins (cholesterol lowering): Most effective when taken in the evening—because cholesterol synthesis is greatest at night.
803: Antihypertensive drugs: Nocturnal hypertension is a distinct phenotype with an increased risk of stroke. Taking certain medications at night reduces this risk.
804: Chemotherapy: the time of day of administration of cytostatics affects both the effectiveness (maximum during the period of maximum division of cancer cells) and toxicity (minimum during the period of rest of normal cells).
806: The main regulator of external synchronization is light. Blue light (wavelength ~480 nm) through the retina suppresses the secretion of melatonin and signals to the SCN: “it is daytime.”
808: —Bright light in the morning (preferably sunny) shifts the rhythm forward, speeds up awakening
809: —Blue light in the evening (from screens) delays falling asleep by 30–90 minutes
810: —Warm, dim light in the evening—physiologically correct

820: CHAPTER 17. INFORMATION FIELDS AND CELLULAR COMMUNICATION
826: Imagine 37 trillion cells in your body. Each is a separate organism with its own metabolism, its own information system, and its own tasks. How do they coordinate their actions? How does the liver cell know to make more bile acids while the immune cell in the gut fights the pathogen?
827: The answer is: they talk to each other—all the time, through multiple channels.
828: And some of these channels were unexpected.
830: The most studied are chemical ones:
831: Endocrine signaling: Hormones travel through the blood to reach target cells in distant organs. Cortisol, synthesized in the adrenal gland, acts on the cells of the brain, muscles, immune system, and skin.
832: Paracrine signaling: molecules act on nearby neighbors. Prostaglandins released by the damaged cell signal to neighbors about danger.
833: Autocrine signaling: the cell responds to molecules released by itself. Important in oncology: tumor cells often create autocrine loops that stimulate their own growth.
834: Gap junctions: direct channels between neighboring cells through which ions and small molecules (second messengers: cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) pass. Provide electrical synchronization—important for the heart, where cardiomyocytes must contract in a strictly coordinated manner.
836: One of the most exciting discoveries of the last decade is exosomes.
837: Cells continually produce and secrete tiny membrane vesicles called nanovesicles with a diameter of 30–150 nm. Inside them are microRNA, messenger RNA, proteins, lipids. These "letters" are absorbed by other cells and change their behavior.
838: Exosomes travel through the blood and lymph. They can cross the blood-brain barrier. They "speak" from a distance.
839: Cancer cells use exosomes to prepare the “soil” for metastases in other organs—suppressing local immune defenses and altering the matrix. This revolutionary discovery explained one of the main mysterious phenomena in oncology: why metastases prefer certain organs.
840: Exosomes are potential carriers for drug delivery and biomarkers in liquid biopsy. This is one of the hottest fronts of modern medicine.
842: In the 1970s and 80s, German biophysicist Fritz-Albert Poppa was engaged in research that at first seemed exotic: ultra-weak light radiation from living cells—biophotons.
843: Every living cell emits photons in the range of visible and near ultraviolet light. The intensity is incredibly weak (a few photons per second per square centimeter), but measurable using ultra-sensitive photodetectors.
845: —The intensity of biophoton emission correlates with the state of the cell: higher radiation under stress, less at rest
846: —Cancer cells emit differently than normal cells
847: —Biophoton emission is not random: it has coherent (laser-like) properties
848: Poppa suggested that biophotons may be involved in the regulation of cellular processes—the “light music notation” of intracellular and intercellular communication. This is a hypothesis that remains the subject of research. But the phenomenon itself is an experimentally reproduced fact.
850: Michael Levine of Tufts University studies bioelectric fields—electric potentials and currents that result from ion channels in cell membranes.
851: These fields not only accompany life, they organize it. Studies on planarians (flatworms with phenomenal regenerative abilities) have shown that the bioelectrical pattern precedes the morphological one. By altering the bioelectrical "programming" through pharmacological agents or electrodes, Lewin could cause the planaria to grow a second tail instead of a head or regenerate an eye elsewhere on the body.
852: This means: body shape is not only the result of a genetic program. It is also encoded in bioelectrical “programs” that can be changed independently of DNA.
853: Potential applications: control of tissue regeneration, early diagnosis of tumors (bioelectrical abnormalities precede anatomical changes).
854: MORPHOGENETIC FIELDS: BETWEEN SCIENCE AND HYPOTHESIS
855: Rupert Sheldrake, a British biologist, proposed the concept of morphogenetic fields: intangible information structures that guide the development and behavior of living organisms.
856: Academic science was extremely skeptical about this idea. Most of the phenomena described by Sheldrake (for example, “morphic resonance”—the transfer of behavior through space without physical contact) have not found experimental confirmation in independent studies.
857: This is important to emphasize: the line between a bold hypothesis and pseudoscience lies in reproducibility and openness to refutation. Sheldrake's ideas remain a hypothesis that has not received sufficient experimental support.
858: However, the question behind this concept—how different organs emerge from genetically identical cells, how bodily form is maintained—remains open and is being actively explored through bioelectrics (Lewin), mechanobiology (Ingber), and systems biology.

867: CHAPTER 18. GENOME, EPIGENOME AND BIOGRAPHICAL TRACE
873: You have two genetic texts. The first is the genome: 3.2 billion letters (nucleotides) written in 23 pairs of chromosomes, identical in every cell of the body. This is a fixed text received from parents. It does not change throughout life, except for rare mutations, or unless you resort to gene editing for severe genetic diseases (a problem with the gene - for example, carbamoylphosphate synthetase 1 (CPS1) deficiency or even a problem with chromosomes - for example, Down syndrome).
874: The second is the epigenome: a system of marks on top of DNA that decides which words of this text are read and which are suppressed. The epigenome is dynamic. It changes in response to your environment, life events, what you eat, how you sleep, how you feel.
876: The same score sounds different in the hands of different orchestras. The same genome is realized differently in different living conditions. Twins with the same DNA at fifty years old have fundamentally different epigenetic profiles—a record of their different biographies.
878: The Human Genome Project ended in 2003. Then it seemed: we received the book of life, and now everything will be clear. Everything will be written in the genes.
879: Disappointment came quickly. We expected 100,000 genes—it turned out to be about 20,000–25,000 (about the same as in a mouse). Most disease-associated genetic variants (found through GWAS—genome-wide association studies) accounted for only a small fraction of the genetic risk. “Missing heritability” has become the main mystery of genomics.
880: The answer turns out to be partly epigenetics.
881: Not “what is written in the genes”—but “how they are read.” Not consistency, but regulation.
882: In addition, we found that more than 98% of the genome is not protein-coding sequences. For a long time this was called “junk DNA.” Now we know: these are regulatory elements, non-coding RNAs, transposons—the most complex regulatory architecture.
885: Childhood experience. Research by Michael Meaney showed that rats that received little maternal care in the first days of life had increased methylation of the glucocorticoid receptor gene promoter in the hippocampus in adulthood. This meant: fewer cortisol receptors → the stress system turns off worse → chronic hyperreactivity to stress.
886: Injury. Rachel Yehuda studied the descendants of Holocaust survivors. The children showed altered epigenetic marks on the FKBP5 (stress response regulator) gene—the same pattern as their parents. It is not the DNA, but the epigenetic trace of the trauma that has passed on through generations.
887: Nutrition. Dutch "Hunger Winter" (1944–45): Children conceived during the famine had altered methylation of the IGF2 (insulin-like growth factor) gene in adulthood, which correlated with an increased risk of obesity and metabolic disorders.
888: Stress. Research shows that experienced stressful events leave epigenetic traces in genes associated with HPA axis regulation, neuroplasticity, and inflammation.
889: REVERSIBILITY: THE EPIGENOME CAN BE CHANGED
890: A key property of epigenetics is plasticity. Unlike DNA mutations, epigenetic marks are fundamentally reversible.
891: This is the scientific basis for optimism.
892: Psychotherapy changes epigenetic marks associated with stress and anxiety. Studies of PTSD show changes in methylation after successful treatment.
893: Nutrition. Certain nutrients are directly involved in epigenetic regulation: folic acid, vitamin B12, methionine (methyl group donors), blueberries, blueberries, blackberries (DNA methyltransferase inhibitor), resveratrol (histone deacetylase inhibitor).
894: Physical activity. Regular afternoon exercise changes methylation patterns in thousands of genes associated with inflammation, metabolism, and neuroplasticity.
895: Meditation. Research shows changes in the expression of inflammation-related genes (NF-κB, COX-2) after mindfulness practice. The mechanism is partly epigenetic.
896: Epigenetics is the biological answer to the question: “Why does what I do every day matter?” Because every day you write or erase marks on your genome.
898: Epigenetic biomarkers. Horvath's “epigenetic clock” is a methylation pattern of 353 CpG sites that highly accurately predicts biological age, regardless of chronological age. Biological age can be years ahead of chronological age (in people with chronic stress or an unhealthy lifestyle) or behind it (in healthy centenarians).
899: Oncology. Many tumors are characterized by abnormal hypermethylation of tumor suppressor genes (they “silence”) and hypomethylation of oncogenes (they “scream”). Epigenetic drugs (DNA methyltransferase inhibitors, histone deacetylase inhibitors) are already used in the clinic.
900: Liquid biopsy. Circulating tumor DNA in the blood carries specific epigenetic patterns—this allows cancer to be diagnosed using a blood test.

909: CHAPTER 19. THE PHENOMENON OF ADAPTATION: FROM STRESS TO RESILIENCE
914: HANS SELIE AND THE RAT WHO CHANGED EVERYTHING
915: In 1936, Hungarian-Canadian physician Hans Selye published a one-page letter in Nature. This letter turned out to be one of the most cited scientific texts of the 20th century.
916: He described the observation: when rats were administered a variety of substances (organ extracts, formaldehyde, even just exposure to cold, surgical procedures, pain), the body’s reaction was nonspecifically similar: hypertrophy of the adrenal cortex, involution of the thymus and lymph nodes, stomach ulcers. No matter what exactly threatened the body, the answer was universal.
917: Selye called this “general adaptation syndrome” and coined a word that has become one of the most commonly used words in the 20th and 21st centuries: stress.
918: He also made a critical distinction: distress is pathological, destructive stress. Eustress—useful, stimulating, necessary for growth.
920: Selye's general adaptation syndrome unfolds in three stages:
921: 1. Alarm reaction. The body is mobilized: the sympathetic nervous system, adrenaline, cortisol. Primary protection.
922: 2. Stage of resistance (resistance). The body adapts: stress hormones are reduced to a moderate level, adaptive mechanisms maintain balance. The person is “coping.”
923: 3. Exhaustion stage. If the stressor is not eliminated, adaptation resources are exhausted. Immunity decreases, regulation is disrupted. Disease.
924: Most people live chronically in the second stage—in permanent resistance. Not anxious, not exhausted—but not recovered either. This is a slow depletion of the adaptive reserve.
926: Homeostasis is a classic concept: the body strives for constancy. But Peter Sterling and Joseph Ayer proposed a more precise term: allostasis—“stability through change.”
927: Unlike homeostasis, allostasis describes proactive adaptation: the body does not just react to changes, it anticipates them and rebuilds in advance. The brain constantly builds predictive models of future needs and prepares the body for them.
928: Allostatic load is the price of many years of adaptation efforts. McEwen developed an allostatic load index that includes biomarkers from several systems: cortisol, DHEA, epinephrine, norepinephrine, IL-6, IL-1β, C-reactive protein, fibrinogen, insulin, HbA1c, BMI, systolic blood pressure.
929: High allostatic load is associated with decreased cognitive function, accelerated aging, and an increased risk of cardiovascular and metabolic diseases.
930: HORMESIS: LOW STRESS AS A MEDICINE
931: Paradoxical discovery: moderate stress is beneficial. This is called hormesis.
932: Exercise is stressful. Moderate hypoxia (high altitude)—stress. Intermittent fasting is stress. Cold exposure—stress. Phytochemicals (resveratrol, EGCG) cause mild oxidative stress.
933: All these minor stressors activate protective mechanisms: antioxidant systems (Nrf2 pathway), heat shock proteins, autophagy, neurogenesis, mitochondrial biogenesis.
934: Mark Mattson from the NIH (Fasting and Neuroplasticity Research) showed that intermittent fasting activates BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), hippocampal neurogenesis, autophagy—and protects neurons from damage.
935: This explains the “exercise paradox”: exercise produces free radicals (oxidative stress) but reduces oxidative damage in the long term. Because mild oxidative stress activates antioxidant systems, which then work more efficiently.
936: “What doesn't kill me makes me stronger” is not just the philosophy of the Stoics. Increscunt animi, virescit volnere virtus. This is the biology of hormesis.
938: Allan Bonanno of Columbia University has studied people's reactions to bereavement and traumatic events. His discovery surprised many: most people do not develop PTSD after severe stress. Most demonstrate resilience—maintaining adaptive functioning despite difficult circumstances.
939: Resilience is neither rare nor an exception. This is a typical human response.
941: —Meaning: the ability to find meaning in difficult events (Frankl)
943: —Flexibility: the ability to emotionally regulate and switch strategies
944: —Physical health: adequate sleep, physical activity, nutrition
945: —Narrative: the ability to tell yourself the story of difficulty as a growth experience
946: Stephen Southwick and Dennis Charney, authors of Resilience: The Science of Mastering Life's Greatest Challenges, identified 10 factors of psychological resilience based on interviews with prisoners of war, Nazi concentration camp survivors, and other people who experienced extreme stress.
948: The concept of post-traumatic growth (PTG—Post-Traumatic Growth), proposed by Richard Tedeschi and Lawrence Calhoun, describes the phenomenon that many people after difficult experiences report not only recovery, but real growth—in their understanding of themselves, values, relationships, and meaning in life.
949: This is not a denial of suffering. Growth is possible precisely through it, and not in spite of it. The fight against the consequences of trauma is in itself a process that transforms the personality.
950: Biologically: adaptation to stress changes neural networks, strengthens regulatory circuits, and creates new coping resources. Experienced and processed stress has a different biology than chronic unresolved stress.

958: Chapter 20. The Science of Longevity and Regeneration
964: In Sardinia (Italy), Okinawa (Japan), Ikaria (Greece), Loma Linda (California, USA) and the Nicoya Peninsula (Costa Rica)—five locations on the planet, scattered across different cultures and climates—live a disproportionate number of people who have crossed the age of one hundred. And they don't just live long. They live great.
965: Dan Buettner, a National Geographic explorer, called these places "Blue Zones" and spent years studying their inhabitants. His question was simple: what do they have in common?
966: The answer was not in genes. Sardinians, Okinawans and Loma Linda Adventists are genetically very different populations. But their lifestyle is strikingly similar.
969: 1. Move naturally. Centenarians don't go to gyms. They live in an environment that requires constant moderate movement: walking in the mountains, gardening, manual labor.
970: 2. Goal (Ikigai for the Japanese, Plan de Vida for the Costa Ricans). A conscious reason to get up in the morning. Research shows that having a goal reduces the risk of death by 15–20%.
971: 3. Reduce stress. All centenarians have rituals to relieve stress: prayer, naps during the day (Sardinia, Icaria), “happy hour” among Adventists.
972: 4. 80% rule (Hara Hachi Bu for the Japanese). Stop eating when you feel 80% full. Reducing calories without hunger.
973: 5. Plant-based diet. Legumes are the basis of nutrition in all zones. Meat—rarely, in small quantities.
974: 6. Moderate alcohol consumption (except for Adventists). Sardinians have 1–2 glasses of wine a day—in a social context.
975: 7. Belonging to a community. 258 of the 263 centenarians surveyed belonged to religious or spiritual communities. 4 meetings per month reduce the risk of death by 14%.
976: 8. Family comes first. Constant contact with children and grandchildren. Duty of care for the elderly.
977: 9. The right environment. A close circle of people who share healthy habits.
978: TELOMERES: THE CLOCK OF CELLULAR AGING
979: Biologist Elizabeth Blackburn and her colleagues discovered telomeres, repeating nucleotide sequences (TTAGGG) at the ends of chromosomes that protect them from degradation. For this discovery, the Nobel Prize was awarded in 2009.
980: With each cell division, telomeres shorten. When they become critically short, the cell enters senescence (senile state) or apoptosis. Short telomeres are a biological marker of cellular aging. It should also be noted that all cells of the body are constantly renewed at a certain speed from stem cells. And in stem cells, telomeres do not shorten due to divisions. Aging of the body occurs due to a decrease in the rate of regeneration with age, and not due to shortening of telomeres in non-stem cells. Various external and internal reasons force somatic cells to divide more times than necessary under favorable conditions, since an unprogrammed acceleration of the rate of division of stem cells can cause cancer. The telomeres of somatic cells are shortened, which makes the genome less stable. This increases the toxic background due to internal reasons, which affects cognitive functions, health, and life expectancy.
981: Telomerase is an enzyme that restores telomeres. In most somatic cells of the adult body it is suppressed. But it is possible to slow down the need for cell division in somatic cells.
996: Ornish's study (Lancet Oncology, 2013) was the first to show an increase in telomere length in somatic cells in people who underwent a 5-year program of intensive lifestyle changes.
997: MOLECULES OF LONGEVITY: SIRTUINS AND NAD+
998: Sirtuins are a family of deacetylase proteins that regulate many aging processes: DNA repair, mitochondrial biogenesis, inflammation, metabolism, apoptosis. Their activity depends on NAD+ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide), a molecule whose levels decrease with age.
999: Leonard Guarente from MIT has shown that activation of sirtuins (through calorie restriction, resveratrol, NMN, the precursor to NAD+) extends life in model organisms.
1000: People are not completely clear—translating the results from mice to people has been more difficult than expected. However, the molecular biology of aging is one of the most active research fronts.
1001: “The Nine Signs of Aging” by Lopez-Otín and colleagues (Cell, 2013)—a catalog of molecular mechanisms: genomic instability, telomere shortening, epigenetic changes, loss of proteostasis, impaired nutrient sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell depletion, impaired intercellular communication. But it is better to call them signs of decrepitude, signs of a decrease in life expectancy. Organismal aging is associated with a decrease in the rate of division of stem cells, and this causes all these and other phenomena.
1002: Each of the signs of senescence is a potential therapeutic target until the technology for safely restoring the rate of stem cell division is created.
1003: REGENERATION: A POTENTIAL WE DON'T USE
1004: Salamanders regrow amputated limbs. Planarians regenerate an entire organism from a single cell. Why can't people?
1005: In fact, we can. Partially. The liver regenerates up to 75% of the tissue. The bone is fused. Skin—closes wounds. The intestinal epithelium is completely renewed in 3–5 days. Even neurons in the hippocampus multiply throughout life (neurogenesis).
1006: But many tissues have limited regenerative potential. Why?
1007: The answer is in stem cells. Adult stem cells are a reserve for restoration. With age, the rate of their division (tissue restoration, wound healing), and their number decreases. Key factors supporting stem cells: proper physical activity, proper calorie restriction, autophagy, reduced inflammation.
1008: Regenerative medicine—tissue from stem cells, management of growth factors, periodic radical rejuvenation—is a future that is already taking shape in laboratories. You can grow any organ by temporarily deactivating the protein that prohibits it. It is also possible to restore the rate of division of stem cells by replacing selectively accumulated old centrioles in them with artificially created new ones. But even today, by properly maintaining a healthy lifestyle, we support the regenerative potential of our body.

1017: CHAPTER 21. THE PATIENT AS A PARTICIPANT IN THE PROCESS
1022: THE END OF THE PASSIVE PATIENT
1023: The model of commercial medicine is clear: the doctor knows, the patient listens. “The doctor said it means it’s necessary.” The patient comes, receives instructions and leaves. His role is to be an obedient object of treatment.
1024: This model not only degrades human dignity. It doesn't work. Moreover: symptomatic treatment without addressing the cause often only moves the problem - eliminating one symptom without removing the systemic imbalance. This is not an argument against evidence-based pharmacotherapy, which saves lives for many diseases. This is an argument for pharmacotherapy to be embedded in, rather than replace, a broader context.
1025: The data on adherence is staggering: it is estimated that 30% to 50% of patients with chronic diseases do not take their medications as prescribed. About a third never pick up a prescription from a pharmacy.
1026: Why? Because the person does not understand why. Because he was not involved in the decision making. Because he was not asked about his preferences, fears and values.
1027: Medicine that ignores the subjectivity of the patient loses its effectiveness even in the doctor’s office.
1029: Shared Decision Making (SDM) is a model in which the doctor and patient jointly decide on a treatment plan, taking into account medical data, the patient's preferences and values.
1030: This does not mean that the patient commands the doctor. This means that the patient is a full participant in the process, with information and the right to vote.
1031: A study by Charles, Gault, and Stradling (1997) described three basic models of physician-patient interaction:
1032: Paternalistic: the doctor decides, the patient executes.
1033: Informational: the doctor informs, the patient decides independently.
1034: Collaborative: The doctor and patient review information together, exchange preferences, and make decisions together.
1035: Data shows that the collaborative model improves both decision quality, implementation, and patient satisfaction.
1037: Judith Hibbard from the University of Oregon developed the Patient Activation Measure (PAM), a scale of patient “activation”: the degree to which he understands his role in managing his own health, has knowledge and motivation.
1038: The results of studies with PAM were striking: patients with high activation had:
1039: —Better clinical results in chronic diseases (diabetes, ischemic heart disease, COPD)
1043: And vice versa: low activation is a predictor of poor outcomes, regardless of the severity of the disease.
1044: Patient activation can be increased: through education, support, changing the style of consultation. It's an investment that pays off.
1046: Rita Sharon of Columbia University created a discipline she called “narrative medicine”—medicine based on narrative. Her main thesis: disease is not just a biological event. This is a story that a person tells.
1047: And the doctor's ability to hear this story—attentively, without interruption, without reduction to diagnosis—is a therapeutic act.
1048: Sharon teaches doctors close reading: analyzing narrative elements in a patient's story—time sequence, emphasis, omissions, metaphors. Who is the hero in the story? Who is the villain? What does the patient perceive as a threat? What—as a resource?
1049: Illness is always inscribed in life history. Treatment must take this history into account.
1050: Narrative medicine does not replace evidence-based medicine. It complements it: it allows you to understand not only “what,” but also “who” and “why”—and build treatment that makes sense for a particular person.
1052: Psychologist Albert Bandura introduced the concept of self-efficacy—a person’s belief that he is capable of taking the actions necessary to achieve the desired result.
1053: In a medical context: a patient with high self-efficacy believes that he is able to change lifestyle, take medications, and manage symptoms. A patient with low—considers himself a helpless observer of his own illness.
1057: —Rate of recovery after illness or surgery
1059: The main thing: self-efficacy can be developed. Through the mastery of small steps, through role models (“others did it—and I can do it”), through persuasion and interpretation (“what you felt better today is not an accident”).
1060: A doctor who helps a patient feel capable is a healer. Not only with advice, but with the attitude itself.

1069: CHAPTER 22. HOW DISEASE IS BORN: THE LANGUAGE OF BALANCE DISORDERS
1076: Two smokers. Both have smoked a pack a day for thirty years. One dies of lung cancer at sixty. Another lives to ninety in relative health. What's the difference?
1077: Two people with the same BRCA1 genotype. One develops breast cancer at forty. The other - never.
1078: Medicine is used to asking: “what” (what is the diagnosis?). The integrative approach adds: “why this person, right now?”
1079: Illness is not an accident. This is a pattern—often a natural response of a system to a set of loads that have exceeded the adaptation reserve.
1081: Over the past twenty years, it has become clear that chronic low-level inflammation is the common biological denominator of most chronic diseases.
1082: Atherosclerosis is not just “cholesterol deposits.” This is an inflammatory process in the wall of the vessel. Peter Libby from Harvard showed that rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque (the cause of a heart attack) is an inflammatory event. Plaques rupture not at the site of greatest narrowing, but at the site of greatest inflammation.
1083: Type 2 diabetes is an inflammatory disease of adipose tissue and cytokine-mediated insulin resistance.
1084: Depression—in 30–40% of patients—an inflammatory disease of the brain (cytokine-mediated neuroinflammation).
1085: Alzheimer's disease is a neuroinflammatory process with the accumulation of pathological proteins (β-amyloid, tau protein) and chronic activation of microglia.
1086: Oncology is an inflammatory tumor microenvironment in which immune cells (which constantly eliminate cancer cells) are suppressed and serve the tumor rather than fight it.
1087: This does not mean that all diseases are the same. But this means: they have a common biological language: inflammation. And influencing it through lifestyle has a wide therapeutic spectrum.
1088: MULTIFACTORIALITY: WHY ONE IS SICK AND THE OTHER IS NOT
1089: The disease almost never has one cause. It has a “cause of cause”—a chain of factors that have developed in a certain way in a particular person—to a goal that is impossible to achieve due to lack of strength.
1090: Functional medicine uses the concept of "antecedents, triggers and mediators":
1102: Mediators—biochemical processes that support the disease:
1108: The disease is born at the intersection of all three layers. Treating only one means ignoring the rest.
1110: One of the problems of commercial medicine is the “tyranny of the norm”: the patient is told “everything is fine with you,” although he feels unwell.
1111: Reference ranges for laboratory parameters are based on the statistical norm of the population—including people with unhealthy lifestyles. This is “the norm among the unhealthy.”
1112: For example, TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone): laboratory norm is 0.5–4.5 mIU/l. But many clinicians believe that values ​​should be in the range of 0.5–2.0 mIU/L for optimal health. A patient with TSH = 3.8 is “normal”—but feels tired, gains weight, and feels cold.
1113: The functional approach looks at the optimal range rather than the statistical norm. And he asks the question: not “is this the norm for the population”, but “is this optimal for this person”?
1115: There is a gray area between health and disease—a condition that medicine often ignores because it cannot be clearly classified.
1116: The person feels worse than before. Gets tired easier. The brain works slower. My sleep got worse. Digestion is unpredictable. But the tests are “normal.” There is no diagnosis.
1117: This is not a simulation. This is a pre-morbid state—a disruption of systemic regulation that has not yet reached the threshold of visible biochemical abnormalities.
1118: Integrative medicine views this gray area as the most valuable point of intervention. Before the disease manifests itself, it can still be reversed.
1119: Preventive medicine in its true meaning is not “screening for early stages of disease.” This is the identification and correction of systemic disorders before the development of the disease.

1127: CHAPTER 23. DIAGNOSTICS IN INTEGRATIVE MEDICINE: FROM LABORATORY TO PERCEPTION
1133: A standard initial examination in most commercial clinics takes 7–15 minutes. During this time, you need to collect complaints, order tests, make a diagnosis, and write a prescription. This is a diagnosis of a disease. But this is not a diagnosis of a person.
1134: Integrative diagnostics adds measurements that are not included in the standard protocol:
1135: —Functional level: how the system works, not just whether it deviates from the norm
1136: —Environmental level: what in the patient’s life is a load on the system
1138: —Narrative level: medical history in the context of life
1139: This is not a replacement for standard diagnostics. This is its extension.
1141: Jeffrey Bland, one of the founders of functional medicine, proposed the Matrix as a clinical tool for organizing patient information.
1142: The matrix covers seven biological networks:
1148: 6. Structural integrity (from membranes to musculoskeletal system)
1150: The matrix allows you to see the pattern of dysfunction: in which nodes of the system is there an overload? What violations create a breeding ground for the rest?
1151: This is a systemic diagnosis, not a symptomatic one.
1153: Standard tests (complete blood count, urinalysis, liver biochemistry with bilirubin, urea, creatinine, glucose) are a necessary but insufficient basis, especially considering that textbooks and “scientific” articles are written by commercial medicine. Doctors are no longer taught to understand the meaning of the answers in these tests. They are taught to recognize numbers and shout out “diagnosis” with the corresponding miracle drug.
1154: Functional diagnostics adds markers that reflect the state of systems over time:
1156: —High-sensitivity CRP (hs-CRP)—a marker of low-intensity systemic inflammation
1157: —Homocysteine—a marker of inflammation and risk of atherosclerosis
1158: —Ferritin (hyperferritinemia—a marker of inflammation and metabolic disorders)
1164: —Extended lipid profile: LDL particles (not just total LDL)
1166: -25(OH)D3 (vitamin D)—deficiency in 50–70% of temperate climate populations
1177:
—TSH + free T3, T4

But without being able to read and understand the answers from routine clinical tests, the answers from functional or any other tests are useless.
1178: CLINICAL CASE STUDY: A LOST ART
1179: Technology has given medicine incredible diagnostic tools. But they also led to a partial loss of the art of clinical observation.
1180: An experienced doctor is able to obtain a huge amount of information simply by looking at the patient:
1182: —Skin: anemia (pallor), jaundice, acne (chronic inflammation in the intestines that develops into hormonal disorders), pigmentation (Addison’s disease)
1183: —Nails: Beau lines (stress, systemic diseases), spoon-shaped nails (iron deficiency)
1184: —Hair: male pattern baldness in women (hyperandrogenism), diffuse hair loss (hypothyroidism, iron deficiency, stress)
1188: Inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation are diagnostic tools that provided information before the advent of clinical tests. They have not lost their value.
1190: There is a dimension of diagnosis that is not recorded in any protocol: the doctor’s perception.
1191: The intuition of an experienced clinician is rapid pattern recognition accumulated over thousands of meetings with patients. Gary Klein, a researcher of decision making under uncertainty, has shown that expert decisions are often made not analytically, but through pattern recognition.
1192: “Something is wrong with this patient” is a feeling that an experienced doctor cannot immediately explain, but which often turns out to be correct. Neurobiology explains this through somatic markers (Damasio): the doctor’s body reacts to signals before conscious analysis is activated.
1193: This does not mean that intuition is infallible. It requires verification. But it should not be discarded in favor of algorithms.
1194: The best diagnosis is a dialogue: between laboratory data, clinical observation, the patient's history and the doctor's perception.

1203: CHAPTER 24. THE ART OF LISTENING: HOW A DOCTOR SENSES THE PATIENT'S SYSTEM
1209: In 1984, Howard Beckman and Richard Frankel conducted a study that would change medical education. They recorded actual medical consultations and analyzed them.
1210: Conclusion: On average, the doctor interrupted the patient 18 seconds after the start of his story. 69% of patients were unable to complete a description of their chief complaint.
1211: Fifteen years have passed. In 1999, Marvel and colleagues conducted a similar study—and found that the time until the patient was interrupted was... 23 seconds. Medical education has been talking all along about the importance of listening. The result improved by 5 seconds.
1212: This is not just an ethical issue. This is a clinical disaster: most diagnostic errors begin with the patient not being listened to completely.
1213: WHAT IT MEANS TO REALLY LISTEN
1214: Hearing is a physiological process. Listening is neurobiological and psychological.
1215: Neuroscientist Yuri Hasson from Princeton has shown that when a person truly listens to another, their brain activity synchronizes. The neural patterns of the storyteller and the listener begin to coincide. The better the listening, the higher the synchronization.
1216: This is not a metaphor for “understanding.” This is literal neuronal synchronization.
1217: Active, attentive listening is an act of neurobiological intimacy. And that's why it's therapeutic.
1219: —Physical presence: eye contact, open posture, removing barriers (computer, table)
1220: -Minimal encouragement: “yes”, nod, “continue” - signals that the doctor is here
1221: —Reflective listening: repeating the patient’s key phrases in his own words
1222: —Pause: the ability to withstand silence without immediately filling it
1223: —Open-ended questions: “Tell me more about this” vs “Is the pain sharp or dull?”
1224: NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION: WHAT THE BODY SAYS
1225: Research shows that more than 60% of information in interpersonal communication is transmitted through non-verbal channels: facial expressions, gestures, posture, tone of voice, distance.
1226: A patient who says “I’m fine” with pursed lips and looking away is telling the doctor something completely different.
1228: Voice. A monotonous, muffled voice is a sign of depression or dissociation. Tense, hasty—anxiety. Anger that breaks through in a story about “calm” events is suppressed emotions.
1229: Breath. Shallow chest breathing—activation of the sympathetic nervous system, stress. Holding your breath on certain topics is an emotional blockage.
1230: Posture and movements. Crossed arms, clenched fists, tense shoulders—defense, anxiety. Collapse of the body, drooping shoulders—depression, helplessness. Constant movement of legs, restlessness—anxiety.
1231: Facial expressions. Microexpressions—short-term (1/5–1/25 of a second) involuntary facial expressions—carry true emotions that a person is trying to hide. Paul Ekman showed their versatility and developed a system for recognizing them (FACS).
1233: Empathy in medicine is not synonymous with emotional fusion with the patient. This is fine tuning to the emotional state of another while maintaining one's own stability.
1234: Helen Riess of Harvard developed the educational program E.M.P.A.T.H.Y. for doctors, based on neuroscience:
1236: M—Muscles of facial expression (facial expression muscles)
1240: H—Hearing the whole patient (hear the whole person)
1242: Her research has shown that clinical empathy training increases patient satisfaction, reduces complaint rates, and improves clinical outcomes.
1243: HEAR THE SYSTEM, NOT THE SYMPTOM
1244: The integrative listener hears not only “what hurts”, but also the context:
1245: —When a patient talks about a headache and casually mentions that “I’m not getting along with my husband,” this is not a distraction. This is part of the clinical picture.
1246: —When a patient calls a symptom “my trouble,” this word carries a semantic load: the disease is perceived as a problem, that is, something that came from the outside, uncontrollable. This is diagnostically significant.
1247: —When a patient says “I knew for a long time that I would have cancer—everyone in the family had it”—this is an epigenetic and psychological history that affects the prognosis.
1248: The art of listening is the ability to perceive text, subtext, context and intertext simultaneously. It's a skill. And it can be developed.

1264: A forty-two-year-old woman comes in with a five-year diagnosis of chronic fatigue and tests that are “normal.” She visited a dozen specialists.
1265: An integratologist conducts a long consultation—not seven minutes, but an hour. He asks to talk not only about symptoms, but about life. And gradually the story unfolds:
1266: A girl in an alcoholic family. Childhood is a constant readiness for unpredictability. The mother is anxious, overprotective. At thirty-five years old - a difficult divorce and at the same time moving to another city, losing a job. A year later, the first symptoms of fatigue appear.
1267: This is not a random biography. This is a clinical story. And without it, there is no diagnosis, no treatment.
1269: Biology recognizes the existence of critical (sensitive) periods—time windows during which the system is especially plastic and sensitive to environmental influences.
1270: Prenatal period. The fetus “hears” the world through the mother’s biochemistry: cortisol during stress, nutrients, toxins. The Dutch “winter of hunger” is a case in point: fetuses that were starved in the first trimester developed epigenetic changes that persisted throughout their lives.
1271: The first three years of life. A period of intense brain development: neural connections are formed at incredible speed. The quality of attachment during this period is the main predictor of psychological stability in adulthood. Bowlby and Ainsworth's attachment theory is one of the most clinically relevant psychological theories.
1272: Puberty. Hormonal revolution, brain restructuring (especially the prefrontal cortex), identity formation. Stress during adolescence can leave significant epigenetic traces.
1273: Pregnancy and childbirth (for women). A powerful biological transition, often accompanied by immunological changes, hormonal changes—and the appearance or exacerbation of autoimmune, thyroid, and mental disorders.
1274: Menopause/andropause. The next major hormonal transition, a vulnerable period for the cardiovascular system, bone tissue, and cognitive functions.
1275: ACE STUDY: CHILDHOOD AS A PROGNOSIS
1276: The Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACE) study included more than 17,000 adults. It asked ten questions about childhood:
1286: The result was linear: the higher the ACE score, the higher the risk of almost all chronic diseases: cardiovascular, cancer, autoimmune, mental, metabolic.
1287: With four or more points: the risk of depression increases by 4.5 times, the risk of drug use increases by 7.4 times, and suicide attempts increases by 12.2 times.
1288: Childhood experience is a predictor of adult health. Not because “the psyche is weak,” but because chronic stress literally changes neurobiology, immunology and epigenetics.
1289: HOW TO BUILD A BIOGRAPHICAL CARD
1290: A biographical health record is a clinical tool that charts a patient's life course in relation to their health.
1291: Time axis: from conception to the present.
1293: 1. Key biological events: diseases, surgeries, pregnancies, injuries
1294: 2. Key psychosocial events: moves, losses, conflicts, achievements, turning points
1295: 3. Lifestyle changes: changing diet, starting/stopping physical activity, abuse
1296: 4. Symptoms and their chronology: when did the first symptoms appear? What happened shortly before this?
1297: Often, when building this chronology, both the doctor and the patient discover a pattern: “every time my eczema started, something happened in the relationship” or “fatigue appeared three months after my father died.”
1298: This does not mean that the disease is “made up.” This means that biological processes unfolded in a biographical context.
1300: A biographical chart is not psychoanalysis. It does not require years of research into the past. Its task is pragmatic: to find the turning points of the system and create a plan that takes into account the whole picture.
1301: “You see that the symptoms appeared six months after losing a job that you really loved. At the same time, nutrition and physical activity changed. And it was during this period that your first tests showed an increase in CRP. Let's start by restoring resources—sleep, movement, social support—and at the same time look at markers of inflammation.”
1302: This is an integrative plan. Biologically accurate. And humanly understandable.

1309: CHAPTER 26. EMOTIONS AND BODY: PSYCHOSOMATIC BRIDGE
1317: “Psychosomatics” is a word that can kill the dialogue between doctor and patient.
1318: “This is all psychosomatic for you” is one of the most offensive phrases that can be heard in a medical office. It means: “Nothing is real. It's in your head. Go to a psychiatrist."
1319: But psychosomatics never meant “made up.” Psychosomatics is the science of the interaction between psychological processes and biological functions of the body.
1320: The pain is real. Inflammation is real. Immune failure is real. The question is what is their cause and supporting factor. And sometimes—often—the answer involves psychological processes.
1322: Emotions are not just “moods”. These are complex neurobiological events including:
1323: —Changes in brain neurochemistry (neurotransmitters: serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, GABA)
1325: —Changes in the autonomic nervous system (heart rate, pressure, peristalsis, sweating)
1327: —Neuroplastic changes (restructuring of neural networks in chronic emotions)
1328: Fear and anxiety activate the amygdala, an evolutionarily ancient structure responsible for responding to threats. Chronic activation of the amygdala → chronic release of cortisol → systemic inflammation.
1329: Grief is a neurobiologically distinct process: it activates social pain zones in the cerebral cortex that overlap with physical pain zones. It is no coincidence that a “broken heart” hurts.
1330: Joy and laughter: activate the dopamine reward system, reduce cortisol, increase NK cell activity. Hung and colleagues showed that watching funny videos before vaccination increased the immune response.
1332: One of the most reproducible observations in psychosomatics: the suppression of emotions is associated with poor health.
1333: James Pennebaker conducted a series of studies showing that people who suppress emotions get sick more often, visit the doctor more often, and have higher markers of stress. At the same time, when they began to write about their deep experiences (expressive writing), immune function improved, the number of doctor visits decreased, and symptoms decreased.
1334: Explanation: suppressing emotions requires constant work—active inhibition of emotional states. This work consumes autonomic nervous system resources and maintains chronic sympathetic activation.
1335: Another mechanism: suppression impairs interoception—the ability to notice bodily signals. A person stops feeling hunger, fatigue, pain—until the system screams with a symptom.
1336: TYPOLOGY OF EMOTIONAL PATTERNS AND DISEASE
1337: Research has identified a number of emotional patterns associated with certain illnesses. It is important to make a reservation: these are statistical associations, not determinism. “Emotional types” are not labels or judgments.
1338: Type A: behavior of a “charged” personality—competitiveness, hostility, urgency. Associated with risk of coronary artery disease (original finding by Friedman and Rosenman, 1959; clarified: hostility is a major component of risk).
1339: Type C: suppression of negative emotions, social subservience, denial of one's own needs. In a number of studies, it is associated with the risk of cancer through the mechanism of immunosuppression during chronic suppression.
1340: Type D: distressed personality—negative affectivity + social inhibition. Associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular events in coronary artery disease.
1341: Again, these patterns do not mean that “cancer is from repressed emotions.” They mean that emotional life is one of the factors influencing immune and neuroendocrine regulation.
1342: Type B was identified by the same cardiologists Friedman and Rosenman as the opposite of Type A. These are people whose lifestyle and emotional reactions are considered the most healthy for the cardiovascular system.
1344: Low level of stress: They rarely experience time pressure. Even under deadlines, they remain calm.
1345: Lack of Hostility: Unlike Type A, they have no internal need to compete with everyone around them or prove their superiority.
1346: Ability to relax: People of this type can relax without feeling guilty. They know how to “disconnect” from work tasks.
1347: Reflexivity: They tend to think about a situation before reacting, which protects them from spikes in cortisol and adrenaline.
1349: In medical research, Type B often serves as the control group. Since it is not associated with an increased risk of specific diseases (like ischemic heart disease or oncology), less is written about it—medicine is more interested in studying pathology than the “norm.”
1353: Modern psychosomatics and behavioral medicine are gradually moving away from the rigid division into letters. Now more attention is paid to coping strategies (how a person copes with stress in the moment). However, the Type B concept remains the standard of “mental toughness.”
1355: SOMATIZATION: WHEN THE PSYCHE SPEAKS WITH THE BODY
1356: Somatization is the conversion of psychological distress into bodily symptoms.
1357: Functional neurological symptoms (formerly: “conversion disorder”): blindness, paralysis, seizures without an organic cause. This is not a simulation - these are real violations of neural functions under the influence of psychological conflicts.
1358: Functional pain syndromes: fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, chronic headache—in many patients have a significant psychological component, although the biological mechanisms are real.
1359: Skin manifestations: eczema, psoriasis, urticaria are often exacerbated by stress—through neuropeptides (substance P, CGRP) that affect skin mast cells.
1360: “There are no diseases that are purely psychological or purely physical. There is an unbroken chain,” said psychiatrist George Engel.

1369: CHAPTER 27. WHEN THE BODY SAYS “NO”—RESISTANCE SIGNALS
1376: A fifty-year-old company director comes in with chronic lower back pain. Three MRIs, two surgeries, six doctors. The pain doesn't go away.
1377: During an extended consultation, it turns out that he has hated his job for eight years. He feels trapped - family, mortgage, employees depending on him. He can't "just leave." But it’s also unbearable to stay.
1378: Back pain is the only “legitimate” way out. When it hurts, you don’t have to come to the meeting. You can say “no” without explanation. You can finally rest—“forcedly,” which means—without guilt.
1379: This is not a simulation. This is an unconscious adaptation: the body has found a solution where the mind has reached a dead end.
1381: Peter Levine—one of the founders of the somatic approach to trauma therapy—described a phenomenon he calls the “incomplete defense response.” When the body encounters a threat, it prepares for action: fight or flight. But if action is impossible (social situation, inability to move, dissociation), the energy of mobilization remains “stuck” in the nervous system.
1382: This is not a metaphor. This is a neurobiological reality: the activated sympathetic nervous system is not discharged through action, and stuck arousal chronically keeps the body in threat mode.
1383: In animals in the wild, this is resolved through tremor (trembling) after leaving danger—a literal release of accumulated excitation. People tend to suppress this tremor (considering it a sign of weakness or loss of control)—and do not discharge the system.
1384: Chronic pain, muscle tension, anxiety, dissociation are often manifestations of this incomplete reaction.
1386: The concept of “secondary gain”—the adaptive function of a symptom—is often perceived as an accusation: “you pretend to be sick for profit.”
1388: Secondary gain is an unconscious process. A person does not “choose” to get sick in order to get time off or attention. His nervous system finds a way to satisfy a real need (rest, attention, permission to stop) through an accessible channel—a symptom.
1390: —The need for rest (which a person cannot take without a “good reason”)
1391: -Need for attention and care (which cannot be asked for directly)
1392: —The need for release from obligations (which are perceived as impossible)
1393: —The need for self-punishment (with chronic feelings of guilt)
1394: Understanding secondary benefit means understanding the real need. But the real need can be satisfied directly, without the mediation of the disease.
1396: Some patients do not respond to treatment—despite the correct diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and a conscientious physician. They are called "resistant".
1397: Standard response: intensify treatment, switch drug, add another therapy.
1398: Integrative Question: What Maintains Disease? What function does the symptom serve in the system of this particular person?
1399: John Sarno, a New York rehabilitation physician, developed the theory of psychosomatic pain (TMS—Tension Myoneural Syndrome). He argued that most chronic back pain is a physiological manifestation of repressed emotions, primarily anger. His clinical results (articles in Spine, hundreds of patients cured) were impressive.
1400: How universal this is is debatable. But his observation is important: many patients with “unexplained” chronic pain have a hidden psychological layer, working with which changes the clinic.
1402: Dissociation—partial or complete withdrawal from bodily experience—is a defense mechanism that develops when pain (physical or emotional) is intolerable.
1406: —Partial anesthesia (do not feel parts of the body)
1408: Chronic pain and dissociation often coexist—paradoxically. A person is dissociated from a part of the body—and it is this part that hurts chronically. Mechanism: Dissociation does not eliminate tension in the body, it only turns off conscious perception. Tension builds up and eventually screams out in pain.
1409: Somatic Experiencing (EMDR) works with exactly this: it helps restore safe contact with bodily experience, discharging accumulated arousal.

1413: Rothschild, B. (2000). The body remembers: The psychophysiology of trauma and trauma treatment. W. W. Norton.
1414: Sarno, J. E. (1998). The mindbody prescription: Healing the body, healing the pain. Warner Books.
1415: Shapiro, F. (2001). Eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR): Basic principles, protocols, and procedures (2nd ed.). Guilford Press.
1416: van der Kolk, B. A. (2014). The body keeps the score: Brain, mind, and body in the healing of trauma. Viking.
1424: The amygdala—an almond-shaped structure deep in the brain—works as a 24-hour early warning system. It scans the environment for threats: sounds, faces, smells, situations reminiscent of past dangerous experiences.
1425: When the amygdala detects a threat, it triggers a reaction before the cerebral cortex even has time to realize what is happening. The neuronal signal from the amygdala to the adrenal glands is faster than from the amygdala to the conscious centers of the brain. We are startled by a sharp sound before we realize it is a door slamming.
1426: Fear is an evolutionarily perfect survival mechanism. The problem begins when it works constantly—in a world that does not pose a mortal threat, but is perceived by the nervous system as dangerous.
1427: Chronic fear = chronic inflammation = systemic destruction.
1429: Fear is not just a psychological state. This is a physiological storm.
1431: —Adrenaline: acceleration of the heart, dilation of the pupils, blood to the muscles
1435: —Chronic increase in cortisol → insulin resistance, immunosuppression, hippocampal atrophy
1439: Robert Sapolsky, in Why Zebras Don't Get Ulcers, described it with characteristic clarity: a zebra being chased by a lion experiences acute stress—and, having survived, discharges it through physical action. A person in the modern world experiences chronic fear—without physical resolution—and literally gets sick from it.
1441: If fear is a reaction to an external threat, then guilt is a reaction to internal judgment. We judge ourselves for what we have done or not done. For illness. For insufficiency. For not meeting one's own or others' expectations.
1442: Chronic guilt has measurable physiological consequences:
1443: —It supports chronic activation of the HPA axis (cortisol)
1444: —It is associated with decreased self-efficacy (“I’m bad/not enough—why take care of myself?”)
1445: —It correlates with depression and its immunological consequences
1446: Patients convinced that “they are to blame for their illness” have worse clinical outcomes. This is not mysticism—this is the neurobiology of self-blame.  Often this is the result of a doctor unsuccessfully telling him that the disease is a defensive reaction against death due to lack of strength while unconsciously striving (for a long time) for an unattainable goal, or a cultural narrative about “personal responsibility.” I hope this book will help correct such mistakes.
1448: Scientific psychology in the early 21st century has accumulated significant data on the therapeutic value of forgiveness—both of oneself and of others.
1449: Everett Worthington of the University of Virginia—one of the leading researchers on forgiveness—showed:
1450: —Forgiveness reduces hostility—the main psychological risk factor for CHD
1452: —Forgiveness is associated with better immune outcomes
1453: —The process of forgiveness reduces physiological reactivity to the memory of the offender (less cortisol, lower heart rate)
1454: Lucia Tusain from Luther University conducted a meta-analysis showing that forgiveness is associated with better heart health, lower levels of depression and anxiety, and a higher quality of life.
1455: Forgiveness is not about “letting the offender go.” This is to free yourself from the chronic physiological response to resentment.
1457: There is one fear that permeates the entire medical context: the fear of death.
1458: It influences how we make medical decisions. Fear of death often leads to invasive, painful, expensive medical interventions—even when they do not improve quality of life. Data on “overtreatment” at the end of life shows that many patients receive intensive care in their final weeks—and die in hospital, under sedation, rather than at home with loved ones.
1459: Integrative medicine offers a different conversation: not “how not to die,” but “how to live well, including at the end of life.”
1460: Palliative medicine is about quality of life, not length of life. This is medicine that recognizes death as a part of life and deals with existential anxiety as a medical problem.
1461: When a person finds a way to integrate the fear of death, he, paradoxically, begins to live better and longer.

1468: CHAPTER 29. LIFESTYLE TRANSFORMATION AS A THERAPEUTIC PROCESS
1473: In 1990, the Lancet published an article that was supposed to revolutionize cardiology. Dean Ornish, a young doctor working in San Francisco, published the results of a randomized controlled trial that showed something that was thought impossible.
1474: Atherosclerosis can be reversed. No drugs. No operations. Only through lifestyle changes.
1475: Patients with severe coronary heart disease followed a program: a plant-based, extremely low-fat diet, moderate exercise, stress management (meditation, yoga, group support), and smoking cessation. A year later, coronary angiography showed that atherosclerotic plaques had decreased. In patients from the usual treatment group, they increased.
1476: This was not an anecdote or anecdotal experience. It was measured, documented, published in one of the most respected medical journals in the world.
1477: Why didn't this article change medicine as radically as, for example, the discovery of antibiotics? The answer lies in the nature of change.
1479: James Prochaska and Carlo DiClemente, studying how people quit smoking in 1983, described the transtheoretical model of change—one of the most empirically supported frameworks for understanding behavioral change.
1481: 1. Precontemplation: “I don’t have a problem. I don't need to change." The person does not see the need for change or denies it.
1482: 2. Contemplation: “Maybe something needs to change... but not now.” Duality: understanding of necessity and resistance.
1483: 3. Preparation: The person has made a decision and is preparing for action. Explores options, makes a plan.
1484: 4. Action: Active behavior changes. This is the most noticeable stage, but not the most difficult.
1485: 5. Maintenance: Maintaining new behavior patterns. Preventing relapses.
1486: 6. Termination: The new behavior has become part of the identity. Going back to the old way is not possible.
1487: A key insight of the model: most interventions are aimed at people in the “Action” stage—those who are already ready to change. But most patients are in the Precontemplation or Contemplation stage. Without recognizing this, any recommendations turn into a doctor’s monologue that the patient does not hear.
1489: Why is it so difficult to change lifestyle? Because most of our behavior is not conscious choices, but automatic patterns anchored in the basal ganglia. A person is a slave to education if he is not in a dream state (meditation, dreaming during sleep).
1490: Anne Graybeal of MIT—one of the leading neuroscientists studying habits—has shown that when a behavior becomes a habit, it literally “moves” from the prefrontal cortex (where conscious decision-making occurs) to the striatum, a deeper, more automatic structure.
1491: It's economical: habits don't require attentional resources. But this is the problem: habits do not lend themselves to a simple “solution” to change them. They are embedded in neural structures formed by years of repetition.
1492: The mechanism of habit formation is the “habit loop”, described by Charles Duhigg based on Graybill’s research:
1493: —Trigger: a signal that initiates a behavior
1495: —Reward: positive reinforcement that reinforces the loop
1496: Evening stress (trigger) → sweets (routine) → temporary relief (reward). The loop is secured. After a month, it’s automatic. A year later, it’s “that’s the kind of person I am.”
1498: William Miller and Stephen Rollnick developed motivational interviewing (MI), an approach to talking to patients about change that is now one of the most evidence-based behavioral counseling techniques in medicine.
1501: —Development of discrepancy (helping the patient see the gap between current behavior and his goals)
1504: MI Key Observation: People change when they hear themselves talk about the reasons for change. Not when the doctor convinces them, but when they themselves articulate their values.
1505: A meta-analysis by Heckman and colleagues (2010), which included 72 randomized trials, showed that MI is effective for a wide range of problems—substance abuse, diabetes, hypertension, obesity, HIV treatment adherence.
1507: Ornish included one component of his program that at first seemed “soft”—group support. It turned out that this was one of the most powerful factors.
1508: Nicholas Christakis and James Fowler showed in a series of epidemiological studies that obesity, smoking, loneliness—and, conversely, happiness and healthy habits—spread across social networks like infections. Your habits affect your friends' habits, which affect their friends' habits.
1509: This is a fundamental observation: lifestyle change is not an individual project. This is a social process. A supportive environment is not an option, but a necessity.
1510: Research from the American College of Living (ACLM) shows that community support is one of the most powerful predictors of long-term maintenance of behavior change.
1512: Lifestyle Medicine—lifestyle medicine—has emerged as an independent medical specialty. The American Board of Lifestyle Medicine (ABLM) has been certifying physicians since 2017. It is based on six pillars:
1519: Each of these factors has measurable physiological mechanisms of action—they are not “healthy lifestyles” as a societal desire, but therapeutic interventions with measurable biomarkers.
1520: The PREDIMED study (Spain, more than 7,000 participants) showed that the Mediterranean diet reduces the risk of cardiovascular events by 30%—comparable to drug therapy.
