1529: CHAPTER 30. FROM DISEASE TO MEANING: INTERNAL TRANSITION
1535: Viktor Frankl survived Auschwitz, Dachau and two other concentration camps. He lost his wife, parents, brother. In inhuman conditions, he observed people—and himself—and made an observation that became the basis of one of the most influential psychological movements of the 20th century.
1536: Those who survived—physically and psychologically—under conditions of extreme deprivation had something in common: they found meaning. It is not about denying suffering. Not in his absence. In its meaning.
1537: “He who knows the “why” of living can bear almost any “how”,” Nietzsche Frankl turned this thought into a clinical method. Logotherapy—meaning therapy—became the third Viennese school of psychotherapy.
1538: Today there is a significant body of evidence that the meaning of life (sense of meaning, sense of purpose) is not a metaphor, but a measurable biological factor of health.
1540: Patrick Hill and Nicholas Turiano from the University of Rochester conducted a prospective study, following more than 6,000 adults for 14 years. The result: people with a high sense of purpose in life lived longer—regardless of age, gender, and income.
1541: A study by Rachelle Cohen in the Archives of General Psychiatry found that a high sense of meaning reduced the risk of Alzheimer's disease by 52%—comparable to the effect of the best neuroprotective interventions.
1542: Mechanisms? Research from Barbara Fredrickson's group has shown that people with a high sense of meaning (eudaimonic well-being) have a favorable immune system gene expression profile—fewer pro-inflammatory genes, more antiviral genes. For people with hedonic well-being (pleasure without meaning), the picture is the opposite.
1544: Serious illness—especially chronic or life-threatening illness—inevitably raises existential questions.
1545: “Why did this happen to me?” is a question of justice.
1546: “What should I do now?” is a question about the meaning of suffering.
1547: “Who am I now, sick?”—a question about identity.
1548: “What will remain after me?” is a question about legacy.
1549: Psychologist Richard Tedeschi and Lawrence Calhoun described the phenomenon of post-traumatic growth (PTG): a significant proportion of people who have experienced severe shocks—illness, loss, disaster—experience not only recovery, but also growth. They become deeper, appreciate life more keenly, and build more authentic relationships.
1550: This is not a romanticization of suffering. This is an empirically supported phenomenon: a 2014 study combining data from 77 samples (more than 70,000 participants) confirmed a moderate but stable association between difficult experiences and growth on key PTG measures.
1552: Rita Sharon from Columbia University created a movement called narrative medicine. Her initial thesis: illness is a story that a person tells. And the ability to hear this story is no less important a clinical skill than the ability to read tests.
1553: Sharon has found that doctors trained in narrative work—reading literature, writing reflective texts—hear patients better, experience less burnout, and make more accurate clinical decisions.
1554: The patient comes with a symptom. But behind the symptom is a story. The story is about what he had to change in his life. A story about fear. The story is about what he is afraid of not becoming. The disease is inscribed in the biography—and without understanding the biography it is impossible to understand the disease.
1555: “Medicine that cannot acknowledge suffering, share grief, and admire the patient’s resilience is unable to heal,” writes Sharon.
1557: Marsha Linehan's Dialectical Behavior Therapy is built on a paradoxical balance: acceptance and change at the same time.
1558: Acceptance is not resignation or capitulation. It is accepting reality as it is, without fighting the inevitable. Psychologically: reduction of secondary suffering—pain about pain.
1559: Change is what a person can do with this reality.
1560: In a medical context: a patient with a chronic illness cannot “accept” and “ignore” a diagnosis at the same time. But he can stop fighting the fact of illness and direct his energy to what can really be influenced.
1561: This is not a philosophical thesis. Research on acceptance for chronic pain (ACT—Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, Stephen Hayes) shows that accepting pain—as opposed to fighting it—reduces the intensity of perceived pain and improves function.
1563: Integrative medicine recognizes that treating a person means helping them not only cope with symptoms, but also find or restore meaning.
1564: This is not the “psychologization” of medicine. This is its extension.
1565: Support groups for cancer patients (Spiegel et al., 1989) showed that a psychosocial intervention to help patients cope with existential questions significantly increased survival—at 10 years of follow-up, the median survival rate in the intervention group was twice as high.
1566: This observation remains one of the most controversial in oncology. But its mechanism is clear: the meaning is: reduces stress → reduces cortisol → reduces immunosuppression → improves immune surveillance of the tumor.
1567: Meaning is not consolation. This is therapy.

1581: For most of the history of medicine, food was thought of pragmatically: calories, protein, fat, carbohydrates. Energy substrate for a biological machine.
1582: This model explains a lot. But not everything.
1583: In 2010, a group of researchers led by David Sinclair from Harvard showed that resveratrol, a polyphenol found in red wine, activates sirtuins, proteins that regulate longevity, including SIRT1. Mechanism: Resveratrol mimics the molecular signal of calorie restriction, one of the most reproducible factors for prolonging life. However, the clinical effectiveness of resveratrol in humans when taken orally remains unconfirmed due to the low bioavailability of the molecule—randomized studies in humans provide conflicting results.
1584: This means: food carries information. It doesn’t just “burn”—it communicates with the genome.
1585: Every meal is a message that cells receive and interpret. This changes gene expression, enzyme activity, microbiome composition, and inflammation levels. Nutrition is an epigenetic regulator.
1587: Nutrigenomics is a young science about the interaction of nutrients with the genome. Her main question is: How does what we eat change the way our genes work?
1589: Folate. Folic acid (vitamin B9) is essential for DNA methylation. Folate deficiency—improper methylation → gene dysregulation → increased risk of tumors, neural defects in the fetus. This is not an abstraction: adding folate to the diet of pregnant women reduced the incidence of neural tube defects by 70%.
1590: Omega-3. Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) fatty acids compete with arachidonic acid for cyclo- and lipoxygenase enzymes. Result: a shift in the synthesis of eicosanoids from pro-inflammatory (leukotrienes, series 2 prostaglandins) to anti-inflammatory (series 3 prostaglandins, resolvins).
1591: Meta-analysis by Balk et al. (2006), which covered 48 randomized studies, confirmed that omega-3s reduce triglycerides, improve endothelial function, and reduce inflammatory markers.
1593: Modern medicine has established that chronic low-level inflammation—subclinical inflammation—is a key element in most chronic diseases: atherosclerosis, type 2 diabetes, neurodegenerative diseases, cancer, obesity.
1594: Nutrition is one of the main regulators of inflammation.
1596: —Refined carbohydrates (white sugar, white flour) → increased insulin → activation of inflammatory pathways
1598: —Saturated fats in excess → activation of TLR4 (toll-like receptors) → inflammatory signal
1599: —Ultra-processed foods (additives, emulsifiers) → impaired intestinal barrier function → systemic endotoxemia
1602: — Legumes (fiber → butyrate → anti-inflammatory effect in the intestines)
1604: —Olives (not olive oil) (oleocanthal is a natural COX inhibitor)
1605: MEDITERRANEAN DIET: THE BEST EVIDENCE BASE
1606: Of all the eating patterns studied in randomized trials, the Mediterranean diet has the strongest evidence base.
1607: The PREDIMED study (Spain, 7,447 participants at high cardiovascular risk, ~5 years follow-up) showed that a Mediterranean diet with additional walnuts reduced the risk of cardiovascular events by 30% compared with a low-fat diet. The results were so convincing that the study was stopped early—it was ethically impossible to continue keeping the control group on the less effective diet. A distinction should be made between population-based data and individualized clinical protocols: a number of products that are useful at the population level may be limited for specific conditions (biliary dysfunction, autoimmune disorders).
1608: Other mechanisms for the benefits of the Mediterranean diet include:
1612: —Reduced risk of depression (meta-analysis by Opie et al., 2015)
1614: Nutrition is the main regulator of the intestinal microbiome. And the microbiome, as discussed in the chapter on the gut axis, influences immunity, mood, inflammation, and metabolism.
1615: Tim Spector from London, one of the leading researchers in the nutritional microbiome, has shown that dietary diversity directly correlates with microbiome diversity, and microbiome diversity directly correlates with immune resistance and reduced risk of chronic diseases.
1616: His recommendation: 30 different plant-based foods per week. It's not the diet—the principle of diversity. Season your food with a variety of herbs and spices allowed by proper nutrition.
1617: Fermented products (fermented cucumbers, zucchini, squash) are direct suppliers of living microorganisms. A study by Sonnenburg and Wastyk (2021, Cell) found that a diet rich in fermented foods for 10 weeks increased microbiome diversity and reduced 19 markers of inflammation.

1630: Before the invention of electricity, people slept differently. Historian Roger Ekirch, in his seminal study At Day's Close: Night in Times Past, compiled evidence from diaries, court records and medical treatises showing that before the 18th century, people had biphasic sleep.
1631: The first sleep is from dark until about midnight. Then an hour-long period of wakefulness—for prayer, reflection, intimacy. Then a second sleep until dawn.
1632: Electricity killed biphasic sleep—and created the conditions for a global epidemic of chronic sleep deprivation.
1633: But more importantly, every time researchers study what happens during sleep, they discover processes so fundamental that calling sleep “rest” is a gross mistake.
1634: SLEEP AS AN ACTIVE BIOLOGICAL PROCESS
1635: In 2013, Maiken Nedergaard from the University of Rochester published a discovery in the journal Science that revolutionized the understanding of sleep function.
1636: During sleep, the glymphatic system, a system of channels around blood vessels through which cerebrospinal fluid circulates, opens in the brain. When active, this system literally flushes the brain, flushing out metabolic waste—including β-amyloid and tau protein, the accumulation of which is associated with Alzheimer's disease.
1637: The brain literally cleanses itself during sleep. And only during sleep—this process practically does not work in the waking state.
1638: Chronic sleep deprivation → accumulation of β-amyloid → risk of Alzheimer's disease. This has already been shown in several cohort studies, including the study by Spira et al. (2013) with PET scan: less sleep = more β-amyloid in the brain.
1640: Sleep is not a uniform state. This is a sequence of cycles of ~90 minutes, each of which includes the following phases:
1641: N1 (drowsiness): transition to sleep, slowing down of EEG waves.
1642: N2 (light sleep): sleep spindles—bursts of activity associated with memory consolidation.
1643: N3 (deep, slow-wave sleep): delta waves; maximum secretion of somatotropin (growth hormone), tissue repair, physical recovery; maximum activity of glymphatic cleansing.
1644: REM (rapid eye movement) sleep: eye movements, dreams; consolidation of emotional memory, integration of emotional experiences; neuroplasticity.
1645: Early night cycles are rich in N3; late—REM. Shortened sleep disproportionately deprives the REM phase. It is REM deprivation that explains cognitive and emotional disorders in chronic sleep deprivation.
1647: Matt Walker, a neuroscientist at UC Berkeley and author of Why We Sleep, summarized the evidence on the effects of chronic sleep deprivation:
1648: —Immune system: one night of 4 hours of sleep reduces NK cell activity by 70%
1649: —Oncology: people with less than 6 hours of sleep have a 40% higher risk of prostate and colorectal cancer
1650: —Cardiovascular system: changing clocks to daylight saving time (losing 1 hour of sleep) → 24% increase in heart attacks the next day
1651: —Metabolism: two weeks of 6 hours—impaired glycemic control equivalent to early diabetes
1652: —Cognition: after 17 hours of wakefulness, cognitive impairment is equivalent to alcohol intoxication 0.05‰
1654: The 2017 Nobel Prize was awarded for the discovery of the molecular mechanism of the circadian clock. Every cell in the body has an internal clock—a feedback loop made up of the CLOCK, BMAL1, PER, and CRY genes, which gives the cell information about the time of day.
1655: The main “conductor”—the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus—synchronizes all peripheral clocks through two main signals:
1658: Blue light from screens in the evening suppresses the secretion of melatonin, the hormone of darkness that signals the brain that night has come. This is the underlying physiological mechanism that explains why smartphones before bed disrupt sleep.
1659: Social jetlag (Ronneburg, University of Munich): difference between biological and social sleep times. Most people sleep and wake up on weekdays at odds with their chronotype. Chronic social jetlag is associated with obesity, depression, and metabolic syndrome.
1660: CODE-19: INSOMNIA AS A MASS PHENOMENON
1661: The COVID-19 pandemic has caused a global increase in sleep disorders—coronasomnia. According to international epidemiological studies, the frequency of insomnia during the isolation period increased by 2-3 times.
1662: Mechanisms: chronic anxiety (hyperactivation of the amygdala → activation of the sympathetic nervous system → blocking sleep), disruption of daily routine, change in light exposure (less daylight), screen time.
1663: This is further evidence of the connection between the psychological state and sleep—systems working as a single whole.

1675: THE ONLY AUTONOMOUS FUNCTION WE CONTROL
1676: The heart beats on its own. The stomach digests itself. The kidneys filter—without our knowledge. But we breathe, both automatically and of our own free will.
1677: This is a unique physiological feature: breathing is controlled both by the somatic nervous system (through the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, subordinate to the will), and by the autonomic one. This means that through breathing—in the only direct, non-drug, instantaneous way—we can influence the autonomic nervous system.
1678: When you lengthen your exhalation, you activate the parasympathetic nervous system. When you breathe shallowly and frequently, you activate the sympathetic. This is not a metaphor. This is the physiology of the Hering-Breuer reflex and the vagal brake.
1680: Heart rate variability (HRV), the main measurable indicator of the autonomic nervous system, is directly related to breathing. The phenomenon is called “respiratory sinus arrhythmia”: when you inhale, the heart rate accelerates, and when you exhale, it slows down. This oscillation is a sign of healthy vagal regulation.
1681: Resonant breathing (about 6 respiratory cycles per minute, which corresponds to approximately 4-5 seconds inhalation / 5-6 seconds exhalation) maximizes HRV—transfers the cardiovascular system to a state of greatest vagal activity.
1682: Stephen Porges, who developed the polyvagal theory, has shown that vagal tone—measured through HRV—is a predictor of emotional regulation, social interaction, cognitive flexibility, and health.
1683: Practicing resonant breathing for several weeks increases baseline HRV and improves stress response regulation.
1685: In 2017, a research team led by Mark Krasnow at Stanford discovered a specialized neural cluster in the medulla oblongata—a “respiratory pacemaker”—that both regulates breathing and sends signals to the locus coeruleus, the brain’s arousal and anxiety center.
1686: This means: the rhythm of breathing directly affects the level of arousal of the brain. Slow breathing—reduced arousal of the locus coeruleus → less anxiety, more calm. Fast, superficial—the opposite.
1687: This is a neurobiological mechanism that explains thousands of years of experience: why slow, deep breathing is soothing. Not because “that’s the way it’s supposed to be”—but because the neural circuitry literally connects the respiratory center with the anxiety center.
1688: Additional mechanism: through the olfactory nerve, the rhythm of nasal breathing modulates the activity of the hippocampus (memory, learning) and amygdala. Nasal breathing synchronizes the oscillations of these structures with the respiratory rhythm—Laal Karmouz from Northwestern University has evidence that memory for images is better when inhaling through the nose than when inhaling through the mouth.
1690: Paradox: Most people who suffer from anxiety breathe “too much”—too often, too deeply, through their mouths. The result is a decrease in CO₂ in the blood.
1691: Carbon dioxide is not just a metabolic waste product. This is the most important regulator:
1692: —Vascular tone (CO₂ is a powerful vasodilator; its decrease → vasospasm of the brain and heart)
1693: —Dissociation of oxyhemoglobin (Bohr effect: with low CO₂, hemoglobin transfers oxygen to tissues worse)
1694: —blood pH (decrease in CO₂ → alkalosis → muscle tetany, paresthesia, dizziness—symptoms of panic)
1695: Chronic mild hyperventilation—habitual, shallow, rapid breathing—maintains chronically low CO₂ levels and, accordingly, a physiological predisposition to anxiety states.
1696: The Buteyko method—normalization of breathing through teaching nasal breathing and reducing tidal volume—has a sufficient evidence base for bronchial asthma and anxiety disorders.
1698: Randomized studies have confirmed the effectiveness of breathing practices for:
1699: —Anxiety disorders and panic disorder: diaphragmatic breathing and resonant breathing reduce anxiety rates comparable to drug treatment in mild cases
1700: —Hypertension: meta-analysis by Elliot et al. (2011)—slow breathing (6 cycles/min) reduces systolic pressure by 8-10 mmHg. Art.
1701: —Chronic pain: HRV biofeedback reduces pain intensity and improves tolerance in fibromyalgia
1702: —PTSD: Pranayama (yogic breathing) included in VA protocols reduces PTSD symptoms in several randomized trials
1703: —Epilepsy: Lehrer et al. showed that HRV training reduces seizure frequency in patients with uncontrolled epilepsy
1706: Diaphragmatic breathing: the stomach rises as you inhale, the chest remains motionless. Activates the diaphragm—the main breathing muscle—and reduces stress on the auxiliary muscles of the neck and chest. Direct vagal activator.
1707: Breathing 4-7-8 (Andrew Weil): inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec. A long exhalation is a powerful parasympathetic stimulus. Effective for acute stress.
1708: Resonance breathing: 6 cycles per minute (~5 sec inhale / 5 sec exhale). Maximizes HRV. Effective for anxiety and hypertension.
1709: Box Breathing: 4-4-4-4 (inhale-hold-exhale-hold). Used in military training (Navy SEALs) for stress management in high pressure situations.

1716: CHAPTER 34. MOVEMENT AS A WAY OF THINKING
1722: Immanuel Kant took the same walk every day - so punctually that the residents of Königsberg set their watches by him. Socrates, Plato, Aristotle and his disciples, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Henry David Thoreau, Charles Dickens—all were dedicated and tireless walkers who said they thought as they walked.
1723: Until recently, this seemed like an anecdote or a personal habit. Today it is neuroscience.
1724: In 2014, Marilee Oppezzo and Daniel Schwartz of Stanford published a study that found walking—including on a treadmill, without a change of scenery—increased divergent thinking (the ability to generate multiple ideas from a single source) by 81% compared to sitting. Convergent thinking (searching for a single correct answer) does not improve. Only what requires association and creativity.
1725: Movement literally changes your thinking. Not metaphorically—neurochemically.
1727: During physical activity, a whole cascade of events occurs in the brain:
1728: BDNF (Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor) is a neurotrophic factor that John Ratey of Harvard calls “brain fertilizer.” Exercise—especially aerobic exercise—is the most potent known stimulator of BDNF production. This protein:
1729: —Stimulates neurogenesis in the hippocampus (formation of new neurons)
1732: Adult neurogenesis is a discovery that overturns the dogma that neurons do not regenerate. Fred Gage of the Salk Institute showed in 1998 that new neurons are born every day in the adult hippocampus. And physical activity is the main stimulator of this process.
1733: Catecholamines (dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin) are released during exercise. This explains the immediate mood-enhancing effect ("runner's high", although the endorphin version has been exaggerated; endocannabinoids are more important).
1734: Lactate—during intense exercise, it crosses the blood-brain barrier and is a substrate for neuronal metabolism.
1736: In 2003, Danish physiologist Bente Pedersen published a discovery that changed the perception of muscle tissue. Muscles secrete myokines—signal molecules that act on distant organs.
1737: The first myokine discovered is interleukin-6 (IL-6), released during exercise in quantities 100 times higher than the baseline level. Unlike IL-6 in inflammation (where it is pro-inflammatory), muscle IL-6 has an anti-inflammatory effect through the stimulation of anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, IL-1ra).
1738: Irispeptide, another myokine, transmits a signal from muscle to adipose tissue, turning white fat into brown (metabolically active), and to the brain, where it stimulates neurogenesis and cognitive functions.
1739: Pedersen called muscle a “virtual endocrine organ”—an organ involved in systemic regulation through hormone-like activity.
1741: Meta-analysis by Josefsson et al. (2014), which included 32 randomized trials, showed that physical exercise reduces symptoms of depression with an effect comparable to antidepressants.
1742: This is not a “healthy lifestyle”—it is a measurable clinical effect with studied mechanisms:
1743: —Normalization of the HPA axis (reduction of chronically high cortisol)
1744: —Increased BDNF → neurogenesis in the hippocampus, reduced in depression
1746: —Decreased inflammatory markers (IL-6, TNF-α), which are high in depression
1747: —Psychosocial factors: self-efficacy, social interaction, daily structure
1748: Important: physical activity works for depression as a prevention (in healthy people) and as a treatment (for mild-moderate depression), and as an augmentation (increasing the effect of antidepressants in severe depression).
1749: SEDIENT LIFE AS AN INDEPENDENT RISK FACTOR
1750: “Sitting is the new smoking”—this phrase entered medical culture after a meta-analysis by Biswas et al. (2015), who showed that prolonged sitting is an independent risk factor—even among people who meet norms of physical activity in their free time.
1751: Mechanism: When sitting for a long time, the thigh muscles are inactive. Lingin protein lipase (LPL), the enzyme responsible for capturing triglycerides from the blood and storing them in muscles, is inhibited in inactive muscle. Result: increased triglycerides, decreased HDL, metabolic disorders.
1752: A practical solution: break up prolonged sitting with 3-5 minute activity breaks every 30-45 minutes. Even standing is more effective than continuing to sit.
1755: —150-300 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week OR
1758: Dose-response curve: Most of the benefits of exercise are achieved when moving from “no” to “moderate” activity. The benefits continue to increase with increasing volume, but gradually. In professional athletes of very high volume—sport kills, physical education heals—excessive inflammatory reactions may occur.

1767: CHAPTER 35. DETOXIFICATION OF BODY AND MIND
1772: “Detoxification” is a word that has been hijacked by the wellness industry. Detox teas, juice cleanses, detox foot patches. Most of these products have no physiological basis.
1773: However, there is real physiology underneath the marketing layer. The body is constantly busy detoxifying—and this is a complex, multi-level biochemical process, the disruption of which has real clinical consequences.
1774: The purpose of this chapter is to examine what is real about the concept of detoxification and what is a myth.
1776: The liver is the main detoxification organ. Its work is organized in two phases:
1777: Phase I (cytochrome P450): chemical modification of toxic molecules—primarily oxidation. CYP450 is a family of more than 50 enzymes that process drugs, hormones, and xenobiotics. During this stage, toxic molecules are converted into reactive intermediates that can be more toxic than the parent compounds. Therefore, Phase I without Phase II is potentially dangerous.
1778: Phase II (conjugation): binding of reactive intermediates to water-soluble molecules—glutathione, sulfates, glucuronic acid, amino acids. Result: neutralized, water-soluble products, ready for excretion through the kidneys or bile.
1779: Phase III: transport of conjugates out of the cell through specialized transport proteins.
1780: Violation of the detoxification function of the liver occurs when:
1781: —Genetic variants of CYP450 (pharmacogenomics—why one patient metabolizes a drug 10 times slower than another)
1782: —Nutrient deficiency (glutathione requires cysteine, glycine, glutamine; glucuronidation—magnesium and B-vitamins)
1783: —Overload—when the volume of toxic molecules exceeds the throughput of enzymes
1786: Modern man lives in a world saturated with xenobiotics—alien chemical compounds:
1787: Persistent organic pollutants (POPs): PCBs, dioxins, some pesticides. Lipophilic (fat soluble) → accumulate in adipose tissue. Bioaccumulation in the food chain. Proven connection with endocrine disorders and cancer.
1788: Heavy metals: lead (still present in old pipes and paints), mercury (large fish, dental amalgams), cadmium (tobacco, contaminated foods), arsenic (groundwater in some regions).
1789: Endocrine disruptors: bisphenol A (BPA), phthalates, some pesticides. They mimic or block hormonal signals. Growing evidence of links to obesity, diabetes, and reproductive disorders.
1790: The CDC's biomonitoring program in the US monitors the levels of more than 300 chemicals in the blood and urine of Americans each year. Bottom line: almost all people carry a significant “chemical load”—the question is its clinical significance.
1792: No “detox tea” can replace the physiological pathways of elimination. What really supports the detoxification function:
1794: —Raw sprouts of cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cauliflower, arugula): contain sulforaphane, a Phase II inducer of detoxification enzymes
1795: —Milk thistle (silymarin): hepatoprotector with moderate evidence base
1796: —Adequate protein intake (sources of glutathione, amino acids for conjugation)
1797: -N-acetylcysteine ​​(NAC): glutathione precursor; standard antidote for paracetamol poisoning; studied in chronic toxic load
1798: 2. Renal excretion: Adequate hydration (1.5-2 liters of water per day) accelerates the elimination of water-soluble conjugates.
1799: 3. Intestines: Regular bowel movements are critical for the elimination of bile acids (which carry fat-soluble xenobiotics). Stool retention is “enterohepatic recirculation” of toxins.
1800: 4. Sweat: sweating during physical activity in the evening and in a cedar barrel is an additional way to remove some heavy metals and organic compounds. Sauna data is limited; There have been no specific clinical studies on cedar barrels.
1802: Detoxification is not only physical. There is another “toxin” accumulating in the modern world: information overload.
1803: Daniel Levitin, in his book The Organized Mind, describes the neurobiological consequences of information noise: constant switching of attention depletes the stores of neurotransmitters, primarily norepinephrine in the prefrontal cortex. The result is a decrease in the ability for deep thinking, decision making, and empathy.
1804: A digital detox—the deliberate restriction of information flow—is neither a luxury nor a whim. This is the hygiene of the neural system.
1805: The media environment competes for our attention using neurobiological mechanisms: novelty, reward, social confirmation. Apps are designed to hold attention—that's their business model. Understanding this allows you to make informed decisions about use.
1807: Yoshinori Ohsumi received the 2016 Nobel Prize for his discovery of the mechanisms of autophagy—the process of “self-eating” by a cell of damaged organelles and protein aggregates.
1808: Autophagy is a real cellular “detox”: the cell sorts out accumulated “garbage” and recycles its components. A powerful inducer of autophagy is fasting (any type, from intermittent to multi-day).
1809: When fasting for 12-16 hours (intermittent fasting), autophagy is significantly enhanced. This is one of the mechanisms behind the benefits of intermittent fasting—along with normalizing insulin, enhancing fat burning, and reducing inflammation.

1823: Imagine a very complex orchestra—thousands of musicians playing synchronously. Enzymes are conductors and instruments. But every conductor has a baton—without it he is helpless.
1824: Microelements and vitamins are the conductor's batons of biochemistry. They are not “fuel”—they are cofactors and coenzymes, without which enzymes do not work. Their deficiency is not just “malnutrition.” This is a systemic disruption of biochemical cascades.
1825: In 2006, biochemist Bruce Ames formulated the “triage sorting theory”: when micronutrients are deficient, the cell prioritizes short-term survival over long-term health. Enzymes critical for immediate function receive cofactors first. Enzymes needed for long-term tasks—DNA repair, antioxidant protection—receive them on a residual basis.
1826: This explains why subclinical deficiency (not “vitaminosis”, but “insufficiency”) can remain clinically invisible for years—and at the same time quietly increase the risk of chronic diseases.
1827: VITAMIN D: NOT A VITAMIN, BUT A HORMONE
1828: Vitamin D is more correctly called a prohormone: it is converted into calcitriol, a hormone that acts through nuclear receptors (VDR), present in almost all cells of the body.
1829: Genetic studies (GWAS) have discovered more than 2,700 genes regulated by vitamin D. It is not a “bone vitamin”—it is a systemic regulator:
1830: —Immune function: activates innate immunity, modulates adaptive immunity, reduces autoimmune reactivity
1832: —Neuroplasticity: VDRs are present in the hippocampus
1834: Vitamin D deficiency (less than 10 ng/ml) is strikingly common, with estimates suggesting that 40-80% of the world's population have suboptimal levels. Reasons: high “norm”, insufficient insolation (living indoors, northern latitudes, covered clothing), food deficiency, decreased synthesis in the skin with dark pigment (which is normal, as it should be).
1835: On the other hand, the meta-analysis by Zhang et al. (2019): Vitamin D deficiency is associated with an increased risk of all causes of mortality, cardiovascular disease, cancer, autoimmune conditions, and infectious diseases. Because vitamin D can cause serious problems if used incorrectly, experts do not even recommend screening for vitamin D levels in healthy adults (Demay et al. 2024) to avoid the temptation to prescribe it. The same experts believe that justification is needed for prescribing and taking vitamin D, even in cases of proven deficiency or in risk groups. So for those with deficiency or risk groups, it is worth taking vitamin D in its natural form in the morning - 6-9 almonds for adults. If the body needs vitamin D from almonds, it will take it, if it is not needed, it will not take it. Tablets, unfortunately, do not have this ability - whether it is necessary or not, they force vitamin D into the body - which is dangerous.
1837: Magnesium is the fourth most abundant mineral in the human body. It is a cofactor of more than 300 enzymes, including the entire system of ATP-dependent reactions (ATP functions only in combination with magnesium—MgATP), the synthesis of DNA and RNA, proteins, and the activation of vitamin D.
1838: Magnesium deficiency is widespread: the modern diet with processed foods contains significantly less magnesium than the traditional diet. Deficiency is aggravated by stress (cortisol increases renal excretion of magnesium), alcohol, diuretics, and diabetes.
1840: —Sleep disorders (magnesium is involved in the synthesis of melatonin and the regulation of GABA receptors)
1845: Randomized trials show the effectiveness of magnesium supplements for migraine (Peikert et al., 1996), hypertension, sleep disorders, and premenstrual syndrome.
1847: Zinc is an essential cofactor for more than 300 enzymes and a structural component of “zinc fingers”—protein domains that regulate DNA transcription.
1848: Zinc has a special place in immunology: it is necessary for the maturation of T-lymphocytes, the function of NK cells, and the production of antibodies. Thymulin, a thymic hormone that regulates T-cell immunity, contains zinc as a structural element.
1849: Anandan Prasad, the discoverer of the importance of zinc for humans (1961), showed that zinc deficiency in the elderly significantly impairs the immune response, and zinc supplements restore it.
1850: Meta-analysis by Hemilä et al.: Zinc (in the form of acetate or gluconate) when taken in the first 24 hours of acute respiratory viral infection reduces the duration of the disease by an average of 33%.
1852: B-vitamins work closely together as coenzymes in energy metabolism and the synthesis of neurotransmitters:
1853: Folate (B9) and B12: key players in the methylation cycle. Methylation is the addition of a methyl group (-CH3) to DNA molecules, neurotransmitters, and phospholipids. Impaired methylation with B9/B12 deficiency → accumulation of homocysteine ​​(a marker and independent risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, dementia) → impaired synthesis of serotonin, dopamine, melatonin.
1854: About 10-15% of the population carry the MTHFR C677T variant, a single nucleotide substitution in the methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase gene that reduces enzyme activity by 30-70%. This option requires supplementation with the active form of folate (methylfolate, not folic acid).
1855: B6 (pyridoxal): cofactor for the synthesis of serotonin (from tryptophan) and GABA. B6 deficiency is associated with depression and anxiety.

1865: CHAPTER 37. PHYTOTHERAPY AND NATUROPATHY OF THE XXI CENTURY
1868: Plants cannot escape from predators, pathogens, or ultraviolet radiation. Over hundreds of millions of years of evolution, they have developed biochemical defenses—thousands of complex molecules capable of inhibiting enzymes, disrupting microbial membranes, and absorbing free radicals.
1869: When a person eats a plant, he receives this biochemical library along with nutrients.
1870: According to WHO, about 80% of the world's population uses herbal remedies as a primary or secondary method of medical care. Approximately 25% of modern medicines are based on plant compounds or are their synthetic analogues. Aspirin - from willow bark. Morphine - from the opium poppy. Taxol (paclitaxel)—from yew. Quinine - from the bark of the cinchona tree.
1871: The question is not whether plants “work”—they clearly do. The question is how to use them wisely, with an understanding of the pharmacology and limitations.
1873: Main classes of biologically active phytocompounds:
1874: Polyphenols are the largest class; over 8,000 identified compounds:
1875: —Flavonoids (quercetin, epicatechin in onions and cherries, anthocyanins in berries)
1878: Mechanisms: antioxidant activity, inhibition of pro-inflammatory enzymes (COX-2, LOX, NF-κB), microbiome modulation, epigenetic effects.
1880: —Artemisinin (Artemisia annua)—a revolutionary antimalarial drug (Nobel Prize to Tu Youyou, 2015)
1883: Alkaloids are nitrogen-containing compounds with powerful pharmacological effects; many are the progenitors of modern medicines.
1885: A rigorous evidence base exists for a number of herbal preparations:
1886: St. John's wort (Hypericum perforatum) for mild to moderate depression:
1887: Cochrane meta-analysis (Linde et al., 2008)—29 randomized trials (5,489 participants): St. John's wort is superior to placebo for mild-to-moderate depression and comparable to antidepressants with significantly better tolerability. Mechanism: inhibition of reuptake of serotonin, norepinephrine and dopamine (tricyclic profile).
1888: Important: St. John's wort is a powerful inducer of CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein → reduces the concentration of many drugs (oral contraceptives, warfarin, antiretroviral drugs, immunosuppressants).
1890: Meta-analysis by Fernandez-San-Martin et al. (2010): a moderate but statistically significant effect on improving subjective sleep quality without significant side effects. Mechanism: interaction with GABA receptors.
1892: Meta-analysis by Shah et al. (2007, Lancet Infectious Diseases): Echinacea reduced the frequency of colds by 58% and the duration by 1.4 days. Mechanism: immunostimulation through TLR-dependent pathways.
1894: A systematic review by Wider et al. (2009): reduction in total cholesterol by 1.3-4.2 mmol/l.
1896: “Adaptogen” is a term coined by Soviet pharmacologist Nikolai Lazarev in 1947: a substance that increases nonspecific resistance to stress without significant side effects.
1898: Rhodiola rosea: class—phenylpropanoids. Randomized trials: Improved cognitive performance and reduced mental fatigue during stress. Mechanism: monoamine oxidase inhibition, neuroprotective effect.
1899: Ginseng (Panax ginseng): the most studied adaptogen. More than 200 clinical studies. Effect: improved cognitive function, reduced fatigue, immunomodulation. The active ingredients are ginsenosides, which affect steroid receptors.
1901: Natural does not mean safe. Key risks:
1902: Drug interactions are the most pressing clinical problem:
1903: —St. John's wort + anticoagulants, contraceptives, immunosuppressants → reduced effect
1907: Hepatotoxicity: A number of herbs (pyrrolizidine alkaloids in senna when used chronically, kava kava) have documented hepatotoxicity.
1908: Oncology: Some plant substances can interact with chemotherapy—both potentiating and reducing its effect.
1909: Principle: The patient taking any herbal preparations should inform the doctor. “Natural” does not mean “consequence-free.”

1917: CHAPTER 38. BODY AND EMOTIONS: TOUCH, OSTEOPATHY, MASSAGE
1923: In the 1940s, American psychologist Harry Harlow conducted a cruel but historically important experiment. Baby monkeys were separated from their mothers and given a choice between two “surrogate mothers”: a wire one, but with a bottle of milk, and a soft, cloth-covered one, but without milk.
1924: The monkeys spent almost all their time with their soft “mother”—even when they were hungry. They “ate” at the wire one, and immediately returned to the fabric one. Touch—literally—is more important than food.
1925: This is not a metaphor for attachment. This is an evolutionary necessity. The skin, the largest organ of the body, contains specialized nerve fibers (C-tactile afferents) that respond specifically to slow, gentle touch and activate the “attachment system” of the brain—the insular cortex, the ventral striatum—causing the release of oxytocin and endogenous opioids.
1927: Tiffany Field of the Touch Research Institute at the University of Miami has dedicated her life to studying the physiology of touch. Her conclusions:
1928: —In premature babies, stroking (15 minutes three times a day) increased weight gain by 47% compared to a control group with no dietary changes. Mediator - vago-insular reflex: massage stimulates the vagus → insulin secretion → better utilization of nutrients.
1929: —In healthy adults, a 45-minute massage reduces cortisol by 31%, increases serotonin by 28%, dopamine by 31%, and reduces inflammatory markers.
1930: —Regular massage in patients with HIV improved immune function—increased CD4+ lymphocytes and NK cells.
1931: Mechanisms: vagal stimulation (via C-tactile afferents) → parasympathetic activation → reduction of cortisol → oxficine → anti-inflammatory effect.
1933: Andrew Taylor Still founded osteopathy in 1874 on principles that were ahead of their time:
1934: 1. Body as a unity: structure and function are inseparable
1936: 3. The artery is the dominant structure: impaired blood flow underlies most diseases
1937: 4. An osteopath treats the patient, not the disease.
1938: Modern osteopathy is not just working with the spine. This is a system for assessing and correcting “somatic dysfunctions”—disorders in tissues (muscles, fascia, joints) that affect the functioning of organs through nervous and vascular mechanisms.
1940: —Chronic low back pain: a Cochrane systematic review (Franke et al., 2014)—Osteopathic manipulative treatment (OMT) significantly reduces pain and improves function
1942: —Coronavirus infection and pneumonia: a number of studies in intensive care have shown a decrease in hospitalization time with OMT care
1944: Fascia—the connective tissue sheath surrounding muscles, organs, and nerves—until recently was perceived as a passive “package.” This has changed dramatically.
1945: Robert Schleip and a team of international researchers showed:
1946: —Fascia contains more proprioceptors than the muscles it surrounds
1947: —Fascia contains smooth muscle cells and can contract independently of muscles
1948: —Fascia is a sensory organ: its receptors transmit information about the state of tissues to the brain and participate in the formation of pain experience
1949: Fascial restrictions - compactions and adhesions in fascial tissue - due to injury, inflammation, chronic stress - can disrupt biomechanics and innervation at a considerable distance from the original site of the lesion.
1950: Myofascial release, foam rolling, and rolfing—methods aimed at restoring fascial mobility—have a convincing evidence base for chronic pain and limited mobility.
1952: Embryologically, the skin and the brain are related: both develop from the ectoderm. This connection persists in the mature organism.
1953: The skin synthesizes neurotransmitters: serotonin, dopamine, acetylcholine, endogenous opioids. It has receptors for cortisol, catecholamines, and neuropeptides. Acute stress causes vasoconstriction of skin vessels, while chronic stress disrupts the barrier function of the skin.
1954: “Skin” diseases—eczema, psoriasis, urticaria—often worsen under stress. This is not psychosomatic in the old sense—it is a neurocutaneous axis: nerve endings in the skin release neuropeptides (substance P, CGRP) that activate mast cells and trigger the inflammatory cascade.
1956: Acupuncture—the insertion of thin needles into specific points on the body—is one of the most studied traditional practices. Its evidence base is heterogeneous.
1958: —Chronic back pain: Acupuncture Trialists' Collaboration meta-analysis (2012, 17,922 patients): acupuncture significantly superior to placebo (sham acupuncture) and “no treatment” for chronic pain
1959: —Headache and migraine: a Cochrane systematic review—acupuncture is as effective as preventive drug therapy
1960: —Nausea during chemotherapy and pregnancy: a compelling evidence base
1961: Mechanisms: endorphin release (blocked by naloxone), neuromodulation through segmental reflexes, local anti-inflammatory reactions, vagal stimulation.
1963: Acupuncture Trialists' Collaboration, Vickers, A. J., Cronin, A. M., Maschino, A. C., Lewith, G., MacPherson, H., Foster, N. E., Sherman, K. J., Witt, C. M., & Linde, K. (2012). Acupuncture for chronic pain: Individual patient data meta-analysis. Archives of Internal Medicine, 172(19), 1444–1453. https://doi.org/10.1001/archinternmed.2012.3654

1974: In 2004, neuroscientist Richard Davidson of the University of Wisconsin-Madison published a study in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences that forever changed the way neuroscience approaches meditation.
1975: He invited experienced Buddhist monks—who had practiced meditation for thousands of hours—to MRI and asked them to enter a state of “loving-kindness meditation.” At the same time, the EEG was recorded.
1976: Result: the monks recorded gamma oscillations (25-40 Hz) of such high amplitude and synchrony that the researchers first thought it was an artifact. These patterns of brain activity—evidence of maximum neural synchronization—were not observed in any of the novice meditators in the control group.
1977: This was the scientific answer to the question “what happens to the brain during meditation”: it radically reorganizes itself.
1979: Neuroplasticity—the brain's ability to change in response to experience—is a fundamental property of neural tissue that was only fully discovered at the end of the 20th century.
1980: Before this, the dominant paradigm was that the adult brain is fixed. Neurons do not recover. The structure of the brain is determined genetically.
1981: Today we know: neural connections are formed and rebuilt throughout life. Chronic repetition of any mental pattern—thought, emotion, behavior—literally changes neural architecture.
1982: Donald Hebb formulated the principle: “Neurons that fire together, wire together.” Meditation is the systematic training of certain neural patterns. And just as physical training strengthens muscles, meditation “strengthens” the neural networks of attention, awareness, and emotional regulation.
1984: Sarah Lazar from Harvard showed that experienced meditation practitioners have significantly thickened cortex in the areas responsible for attention (right anterior insula, sensory cortex). These changes correlate with the number of hours of practice.
1985: Britton et al (Brown University): Mindfulness meditation increases prefrontal cortex activity and decreases amygdala reactivity—shifting the balance from automatic to mindful emotional response.
1986: Davidson and Kabat-Zinn (2003) conducted a study with employees of a biotechnology company: An 8-week MBSR (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction) program significantly:
1988: —Improved immune response to influenza vaccination (increased antibodies)
1989: —Shifted EEG activity from the right (negative emotions) to the left (positive) frontal lobe
1990: This was the first compelling neurobiological and immunological argument for meditation in clinical medicine.
1992: Jon Kabat-Zinn created MBSR—a mindfulness-based stress reduction program—at the University of Massachusetts Medical School in 1979. This became a bridge between the Buddhist meditation tradition and evidence-based medicine.
1993: MBSR—8-week program (2.5 hours weekly + 1 day of silence + home practice), including:
1999: —Chronic pain: reducing pain intensity and improving quality of life
2000: —Anxiety disorders: meta-analysis by Hofmann et al. (2010)—significant reduction in anxiety symptoms
2001: —Depression: MBCT (Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy) reduces the risk of relapse of depression by 43%—NICE (UK) approved as first-line treatment for chronic depression
2002: —Oncology: reduction of fatigue, anxiety, sleep disorders
2005: Elizabeth Blackburn, who received the Nobel Prize for her discovery of telomeres, collaborated with psychologist Elissa Epel to explore the connection between stress, meditation and telomeres.
2006: Chronic psychological stress accelerates telomere shortening—accelerating cellular aging.
2007: Meditation practices: Research by Blackburn & Epel (2017) found that regular meditation was associated with longer telomeres compared to controls.
2008: Meditation may be a biological practice that prolongs life.
2010: Focused Attention Meditation: concentration on an object—breath, mantra, image. Trains stability of attention.
2011: Open Monitoring Meditation: Accepting whatever arises in the mind without attachment. Develops metacognition (the ability to observe one's own thoughts).
2012: Loving-Kindness Meditation (Metta): Directed cultivation of kindness towards oneself and others. Barbara Fredrickson Research: Increases positive emotions, social connectedness, vagal tone.
2013: Transcendental Meditation (TM): use of mantra; The most researched technique in cardiology—a meta-analysis showed a reduction in blood pressure.

2021: CHAPTER 40. ENERGY ANATOMY AND HUMAN BIOFIELD
2027: The concepts of “biofield”, “prana”, “qi”, “chakras” have existed for thousands of years in different cultural traditions. They describe something real in experience—a sense of energy, flow, centers in the body, qualities that are not reducible to anatomical structures.
2028: The question is, do these concepts describe biological realities or phenomenology, a culturally determined way of interpreting bodily experience?
2029: Honest answer: This is an open question. Some components of traditional “energy” systems are receiving scientific confirmation in new formats. Others remain in the realm of hypotheses or have not been proven.
2030: WHAT DO WE KNOW ABOUT BIOELECTRICITY
2031: The body generates electric and magnetic fields—this is not a metaphor. This is physics.
2032: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is a recording of the heart's electrical field throughout the body. Electroencephalogram (EEG)—recording of the electrical activity of the brain. Magnetocardiography (MCG) and magnetoencephalography (MEG) record the magnetic fields of these organs—so weak that they require superconducting quantum interferometers (SQUID) in shielded chambers.
2033: Michael Levine of Tufts University has shown that bioelectrical gradients—electrical potential differences between cells—play a key regulatory role in morphogenesis (the formation of organs and tissues), regeneration, and tumor formation. This is a new field—bioelectrics—that has the potential to change the understanding of living systems.
2034: Measurements of the heart's magnetic field (magnetocardiography) show that it extends several meters from the body—a reproducible physical fact. This provides a physical basis for the idea of ​​"field interactions" between people—although the clinical significance of these interactions remains a matter of study.
2037: Living cells emit ultra-weak photon radiation - biophotons (Fritz-Albert Popp, 1970s). Their measurability is confirmed; the regulatory role remains a hypothesis. A detailed analysis of the mechanisms and evidence base is in Chapter. 17 “Information fields and cellular communication.”
2041: Chinese medicine describes chi (qi)—the life force that circulates along meridians—channels that do not have a clear anatomical basis in Western anatomy. Ayurveda describes prana, a similar concept associated with breath and life energy.
2043: —Meridians are not identified as anatomical structures by direct examination
2044: —However, there is evidence that acupuncture points are characterized by special electrical properties (reduced electrical resistance) and a high density of nerve endings
2045: —A number of researchers suggest that “meridians” may correspond to fascial planes—connective tissue structures rich in nerve fibers and piezoelectric materials
2046: Perhaps traditional systems described something real—but in the conceptual language of their time. The task of science is not to reject these descriptions, but to find their biological basis where they exist.
2048: The chakra system in the yogic tradition describes seven "energy centers" along the spine, each associated with specific physiological functions, psychological conditions and diseases.
2049: An interesting anatomical observation: the location of the chakras approximately corresponds to the location of large nerve plexuses and endocrine glands. Manipura chakra (solar plexus)—celiac plexus and adrenal glands. Anahata chakra (heart)—cardiac plexus and thymus. Ajna chakra (third eye)—pituitary gland and pineal gland.
2050: This does not mean that the chakras are "real" in the literal sense. But it is possible that early observers noted real psychosomatic correlations—connections between the state of a body region and psychological/functional states.
2051: ENERGY PRACTICES WITH AN EVIDENCE BASE
2052: Several practices originating from energetic traditions have studied clinical effectiveness:
2053: Tai chi and qigong: randomized studies show effectiveness for arthritis, hypertension, anxiety, depression, chronic pain. The mechanisms - movement, breathing, meditative component - have been studied within the framework of modern physiology.
2054: Yoga: extensive evidence base for anxiety, depression, PTSD, back pain, cardiovascular diseases. Mechanisms—vagal stimulation, GABA activity, cortisol reduction.
2055: Reiki and energy healing: systematic reviews show no convincing effects beyond placebo. Therapeutic presence and intentional touch alone can have an effect—but not through “energetic” mechanisms.

2064: CHAPTER 41. SYSTEMS THINKING IN MEDICINE: FROM SYMPTOM TO NETWORK
2068: THE MAP IS NOT THE TERRITORY
2069: Alfred Korzybski, a Polish-American scientist, formulated a principle that became the basis of systems thinking: “The map is not the territory.”
2070: In medicine, this means: a diagnosis is a map. Useful, necessary—but not reality itself. When we say “a patient has hypertension,” we are attaching a label—a diagnostic category—to a complex, multifactorial, dynamic system that includes genetics, nutrition, vascular biology, renal function, stress, sleep, the microbiome, life history.
2071: Treating “hypertension” as an abstraction—antihypertensive drugs—works in most cases. Treatment of a specific person with specific hypertension requires a systemic view: to understand which network nodes are disturbed in this person.
2073: Albert-László Barabási, one of the founders of complex network theory, applied network analysis to biomedicine. His team created the “human interactome”—a map of all known interactions between proteins in the human body.
2074: Discovery: genes whose mutations cause one disease are not randomly located in the network. They cluster into “disease modules”—densely connected regions of the interactome. Diseases whose modules overlap have common mechanisms and often accompany each other.
2075: This explains comorbidity—a regular combination of diseases—without resorting to chance. Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease are comorbid not because they are “common,” but because they involve overlapping molecular networks.
2076: Medical Implication: Treatment of chronic inflammation (a common node in many disease networks) may simultaneously affect multiple comorbid conditions.
2077: FUNCTIONAL MEDICINE: THE MATRIX OF A SYSTEM VIEW
2078: Jeffrey Bland, the founder of functional medicine, developed the "matrix" - a clinical tool for systemic understanding of the patient:
2085: —Structural integrity (from cell membranes to the musculoskeletal system)
2094: At the intersection of the axes, the doctor analyzes which biological systems are disturbed and through which modifiable factors.
2096: The “one disease, one cause” paradigm (Koch's postulates) revolutionized infectious disease medicine in the 19th century. But it doesn't work well for chronic diseases.
2097: Alzheimer's disease is not an "amyloid disease." Amyloid plaques are present in the brains of many cognitively intact older adults. The pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease is a systemic neuroinflammatory, metabolic and vascular disaster in which β-amyloid is one of the actors, but not the only one.
2098: Type 2 diabetes is not an “insulin disease.” This is a systemic metabolic-inflammatory dysfunction involving adipose tissue, liver, intestines, brain, and immune system.
2099: Systems thinking changes the goal of treatment: not “reduce amyloid” or “reduce glucose”—but restore the normal functioning of the disrupted network nodes.
2101: Linear thinking assumes: under x conditions, y result occurs. Double the cause, double the effect.
2102: Biological systems are nonlinear. They may remain stable under increasing stress and then suddenly move into a new pathological state—a “critical transition.”
2103: Mauro Laurenti and Felix Reed of the Dutch group have developed mathematical methods for predicting critical transitions in biological systems based on “early warning signals”—increasing fluctuations and slow recovery from small perturbations.
2104: Applied to health, a patient approaching a critical transition toward illness (diabetes, heart attack, psychotic episode) experiences a decrease in system reserve capacity—which can be measurable before clinical manifestation. This is a potential basis for early diagnosis.

2117: In 2022, the journal Mayo Clinic Proceedings published data from a national study: more than 60% of American doctors in commercial medicine show signs of professional burnout. This is not a personal weakness. This is a systemic crisis.
2118: Doctor burnout is killing patients. The connection is documented: doctors with signs of burnout make 2 times more medical errors. Burnt out doctors listen to patients worse, report mistakes less often, and leave the profession more often.
2119: But it's not just about patient safety. This is about a human tragedy: every year in the United States, about 300-400 doctors commit suicide—a rate significantly higher than the population average.
2121: Christina Maslach, a social psychologist and author of the most widely used burnout measurement tool (Maslach Burnout Inventory), described three components of burnout:
2122: 1. Emotional exhaustion: feeling empty, unable to give emotionally to others
2123: 2. Depersonalization (cynicism): distancing from patients, perceiving them as “cases” rather than people
2124: 3. Reduction of personal achievements: a feeling of incompetence and lack of progress
2125: Burnout is not depression, although they often coexist. Burnout is specific to the work context; depression permeates all areas of life.
2127: Putting out the fire by showering commercial medicine doctors with “stress resistance” is a popular but ineffective strategy. Research convincingly shows that burnout is a systemic problem, not an individual one.
2128: Key systemic factors of burnout in commercial medicine:
2129: Loss of autonomy: the doctor makes clinical decisions less and less—they are regulated by protocols, insurance requirements, and administrative rules. This violates the basic psychological need for autonomy (Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory).
2130: Administrative workload: According to research, American doctors spend more than 2 hours on administrative tasks (documentation, codes, authorizations) for every hour of direct contact with patients. This is “death by a thousand clicks.”
2131: Violation of the value balance: the doctor went into medicine to help people, but ended up in a system that measures his KPI in patients treated per hour.
2132: Chronic impact of suffering: Doctors regularly face death, pain, helplessness. Without adequate emotional support and resources—it accumulates.
2134: Simon Talbot and Wendy Dean introduced the concept of “moral injury”—taken from military psychiatry—into medical discourse. This is not burnout. This is something deeper.
2135: Moral injury occurs when the system (commercial medicine in particular) forces a doctor to act against his medical ethics—to give a patient less than necessary for administrative or economic reasons. When the doctor knows what needs to be done, he wants to do it, but the system does not allow it.
2136: This gives rise to a deep sense of betrayal—of oneself, the patient, the profession. This is not solved by yoga or meditation. This requires systemic changes - a complete and unconditional rejection of commercial medicine.
2138: Resilience is not the absence of stress, but the ability to recover. What doctors support her with:
2139: Meaning. Research by Shanone Lotton and colleagues: Physicians who maintain a connection to the original meaning of their work (“why I became a doctor”) are more resilient. Simple practices—writing about meaningful clinical stories—help maintain this connection.
2140: Community. Collegial support, the opportunity to talk about difficult cases, “Balint groups” (groups where doctors discuss emotionally difficult cases) protect against isolation.
2141: Your own health. Irony: Doctors who teach patients to take care of themselves often neglect sleep, nutrition, physical activity and their own mental health. Systemic support for physicians’ own well-being is not a privilege, but a clinical necessity.
2142: Autonomy and voice. The ability to influence your work environment is one of the most powerful defenses against burnout.
2144: The paradox: deep presence with the patient—that which requires the greatest resources—is also the source of restoration. Physicians who are able to be fully present in the clinical encounter describe it as a source of meaning, not just exhaustion.
2145: Ronald Epstein of the University of Rochester has shown that practicing mindfulness among physicians reduces burnout, improves clinical communication, and increases patient satisfaction. This is not just a technique—it is a philosophy of clinical presence.
2146: Self-care is not self-indulgence. This is a professional responsibility. It's difficult, but it's possible.

2160: A doctor is working with a vulnerable person. The patient comes in a state of pain, fear, loss—with the hope of help. This asymmetry of power defines all medical ethics.
2161: Integrative medicine expands therapeutic options—and at the same time expands ethical issues. If traditional medicine works primarily with proven interventions in a limited physiological space, then integrative medicine includes psychological, social, spiritual and biographical context.
2162: This is wealth—and risk. Wealth: the ability to see a person holistically. Risk: blurred boundaries of professional competence, potential for abuse of intimacy, temptation to promise more than is possible.
2164: Tom Beauchamp and James Childress formulated four fundamental principles in their book Principles of Biomedical Ethics (1979):
2165: 1. Autonomy: respect for the patient's ability to make informed decisions. The patient has the right to information, to participate in decisions, and to refuse treatment.
2166: 2. Beneficence: act in the best interests of the patient. It is not enough to “do no harm”—you need to actively do what brings benefit.
2167: 3. Non-maleficence: Primum non nocere. The risk of an intervention must be comparable to its expected benefit.
2168: 4. Justice: fair distribution of resources, non-discrimination.
2169: In integrative medicine, these principles take on new dimensions.
2171: Traditional medicine was often paternalistic: “The doctor knows best.” This reduced the patient's autonomy to a minimum.
2172: Integrative medicine declares patient autonomy as a value—and must back it up with real practices: informed consent, shared decision-making, recognition of the patient’s right to choose non-standard treatment.
2173: However, autonomy has limits. When a patient refuses removal of the gallbladder due to a polyp in favor of untested methods—is this his autonomous choice or a consequence of misinformation? Or an insistence on an integrative medicine specialist to approve the removal of the gallbladder for gallstones (prohibited by the Hippocratic Oath) due to an unscrupulous proposal from a commercial medicine worker.  Where is the line between respect for choice and the duty to report proven effectiveness or proven harm?
2174: This is not a theoretical discussion. This is a real clinical case that oncologists and integrative practitioners encounter every day.
2176: Integrative medicine is in a constant tension between openness to traditional and new methods—and responsibility to the evidence base.
2178: —Do not offer as a “treatment” something that has no evidence of effectiveness
2179: —Inform patients about what has been proven, what is probable, what has not been confirmed
2180: —Do not allow integrative practices to replace proven treatments
2181: —Recognize that “not proven” ≠ “doesn’t work”—but also: “not proven” means “no basis to recommend”
2182: A study by Fein et al: Patients using exclusively alternative methods for cancer had almost 2 times the mortality rate compared to those receiving standard treatment. This is ethically significant information that the patient should have.
2184: Research shows that most patients consider spirituality an important part of their lives and health. More than 70% of American patients would like their doctor to ask about their spiritual beliefs in the context of a serious illness.
2185: Ethical principles for working with spirituality in medicine:
2186: —Respect for the patient’s spiritual beliefs without imposition
2187: —Recognition of the role of spirituality in coping and quality of life
2188: —Clear distinction: the physician is not a spiritual director, but can recognize and respect the spiritual dimension of the patient's experience
2189: —Referral to religious leaders—as part of palliative and integrative care
2190: Dana King of the Medical University of South Carolina School of Medicine has shown that doctors who ask about spiritual needs in serious illness receive higher ratings of communication quality from patients.
2192: Integrative medicine is also a growing market. The wellness industry is valued at hundreds of billions of dollars. When a practitioner sells supplements that he recommends, a conflict of interest arises.
2193: Ethical position: transparency and separation. A physician who sells products that he himself recommends must inform patients about this. Ideally, the diagnostic and recommendation function should be separated from the commercial function.
2194: “The healer who places his own financial well-being above the interests of the patient violates the fundamental contract of the medical profession”—the formulation of the American Medical Society applies to all practitioners.

2203: CHAPTER 44. PALLIATIVE MEDICINE AND END OF LIFE
2208: Eighty percent of people surveyed say they would like to die at home. Eighty percent die in medical institutions—hospitals, nursing homes.
2209: This gap is not accidental. It reflects silence: we don't talk about death—not with doctors, not with family, not with ourselves. Treatment-oriented medicine perceives death as failure. A dying patient—statistically—receives the most intensive and expensive treatment in the last weeks of life, often spending them in intensive care, under sedation.
2210: Data from Harvard's Bloch showed in 2010 that patients with inoperable lung cancer who received early palliative care (along with standard cancer therapy) lived an average of 2.7 months longer than those who received standard therapy alone. And their quality of life was significantly better.
2211: Palliative medicine does not hasten death. Sometimes she puts it aside.
2213: Palliative medicine is a specialty dedicated to quality of life during serious illness. It is not opposed to treatment; it can accompany it from the very beginning of a serious illness.
2215: —Symptom control (pain, shortness of breath, nausea, fatigue)—for any serious illness
2216: —Psychological and spiritual support for the patient and family
2217: —Assistance in decision making (what the patient wants; advance care planning)
2218: —Support in transition to palliative care when treatment is no longer possible or desirable
2219: Palliative medicine expands the concept of “treatment”: not to defeat the disease, but to live well with the disease.
2221: Almost a third of cancer patients experience uncontrollable pain. This is not fate, this is a medical error.
2222: A “pain management ladder” has been developed—a stepwise protocol: from non-narcotic analgesics (step I) through weak opioids (step II) to strong opioids (step III). When used correctly, this protocol provides adequate pain control in 80-90% of cancer patients.
2224: —Opiophobia: fear of developing addiction in terminal patients (unfounded)
2226: —Insufficient training of doctors in the field of pain management
2227: The right to pain relief is a recognized human right. In 2013, a group of experts in the Lancet publicly stated: “Access to adequate pain relief is a matter of global justice.”
2228: CONVERSATIONS ABOUT THE END OF LIFE
2229: Elisabeth Kübler-Ross published On Death and Dying in 1969, a book that revolutionized the approach to dying patients. She described the five stages of accepting a diagnosis (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance)—not as a rigid pattern, but as possible reactions.
2230: Her main discovery: dying patients want to talk about their death. They need honesty and presence—not optimistic denial.
2231: Palliative narrative: Conversations About Difficult Diagnoses—training doctors on how to talk about the prognosis, the transition to palliative care, and what is important to the patient in the remaining time. It's a skill. And it can be developed.
2232: A study by Gray et al found that patients with whom doctors had structured conversations about treatment goals were less likely to receive aggressive treatment at the end of life and were more likely to die according to their wishes.
2234: Dame Cecily Saunders founded the first modern hospice, St Christopher's, in London in 1967. Her philosophy changed palliative medicine.
2235: “It’s not just how many days are added to life that matters, but how much life is added to days.”
2236: Hospice approach: comprehensive care for the dying person and his family, symptom control, psychological and spiritual support, the opportunity to die at home or in an environment as close as possible to home.
2237: Research shows that hospice patients live on average as long as (and sometimes longer) than patients with similar diagnoses who receive aggressive treatment—with a significantly better quality of life.

2249: “You have cancer”—three words after which the world becomes different.
2250: A cancer diagnosis is one of the most existentially devastating events in a person's life. It affects everything: the body, identity, relationships, the meaning of life. It activates our deepest fears—death, helplessness, loss of control.
2251: Standard oncology is highly effective in combating tumors. But a cancer patient is more than a tumor. This is a person with a biography, family, emotions, spiritual needs, side effects of treatment that radically change the quality of life.
2252: Integrative oncology is the medicine of the whole person.
2254: Integrative oncology is a specialty that combines standard oncological therapy with evidence-based additional approaches aimed at:
2259: Key point: additional methods—along with standard treatment, and not instead of it. Replacing proven cancer therapy with unproven “alternative” methods is ethically unacceptable and clinically dangerous.
2260: The Society of Integrative Oncology (SIO) develops clinical guidelines based on systematic reviews. In 2023, guidelines for complementary therapies in breast cancer were published—the first of their kind with levels of evidence.
2261: PSYCHOO-ONCOLOGY: THE CONNECTION OF THE MIND AND TUMOR
2262: Psycho-oncology studies the psychological aspects of cancer. Key questions: how do psychological factors influence the course of the disease? How does psychological help affect quality of life and, possibly, prognosis?
2263: Data on the effect of stress on oncological processes:
2264: —Chronic stress increases the level of norepinephrine → activates β-adrenergic receptors on tumor cells → stimulates tumor growth and angiogenesis (in a number of experimental models)
2265: —Cortisol disrupts immune surveillance (NK cells) of tumor cells
2266: —Stress stimulates metastasis through inflammatory and proangiogenic pathways
2267: Research by David Spiegel (Stanford): Psychological support groups for women with metastatic breast cancer improved quality of life and, according to the original 1989 study, doubled survival. Replication of the results has been incomplete, but the research direction remains important.
2269: According to SIO clinical guidelines and systematic reviews:
2270: Physical activity: Recommended as a strongly evidenced intervention. Reduces fatigue during chemotherapy (meta-analysis Mustian et al., 2017—exercise is more effective against cancer-related fatigue than pharmacological methods). Reduces the risk of relapse in breast cancer and colorectal cancer.
2271: Acupuncture: proven for chemotherapy-associated nausea/vomiting (Evidence Level A); for pain (level B); with hot flashes during hormone therapy.
2272: MBSR/meditation: significantly improves quality of life, anxiety, depression, sleep disturbances—in cancer patients.
2273: Nutrition: An anti-inflammatory diet is associated with better outcomes in a range of cancers; obesity is a proven risk factor for relapse; Intermittent fasting is being studied in oncology.
2274: Psychological interventions: CBT, group support, significantly reduce distress and depression.
2276: This is the most challenging area of ​​integrative oncology. Patients actively use supplements—vitamins, antioxidants, herbal preparations—often without informing the oncologist.
2278: —Some antioxidants in high doses can reduce the effectiveness of radiation therapy and chemotherapy (antioxidants protect tumor cells from oxidative stress, the mechanism of action of a number of cytostatics)
2279: —St. John's wort reduces the concentrations of several chemotherapeutic agents through induction of CYP3A4
2280: —A number of herbal preparations have immunomodulatory effects that can interact with immunotherapy
2281: Clinical recommendation: Full transparency—the physician should be aware of all supplements and herbal products. The oncologist should ask about this without judgment.

2293: In all cultures of the world, the heart occupies a special place—the organ of love, courage, and vitality. This is not an accident: the heart is the first organ to appear in embryogenesis and begins to beat even before the brain is formed. Its rhythm is the first sound that a child hears in the womb.
2294: Today, cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains the leading cause of death in the world—about 18 million deaths per year. But of all chronic diseases, atherosclerosis and ischemic heart disease are the most reversible through lifestyle changes.
2295: Dean Ornish showed this in 1990. However, the lessons from his research never became standard of care.
2297: For a long time, atherosclerosis was perceived as “clogged pipes”—the accumulation of cholesterol plaques in the arteries. Today we know: this is a simplification.
2298: Russell Ross, writing in the New England Journal of Medicine in 1999, defined atherosclerosis as an “inflammatory disease.” The plaque is formed not just from cholesterol—it is an active inflammatory process:
2299: —Endothelial damage (oxidized LDL, hypertension, homocysteine, tobacco, inflammation)
2300: —Penetration of LDL into the subendothelium → oxidation → “capture” by macrophages → foam cells
2304: This explains why people with “normal” cholesterol develop heart attacks (if there is inflammation) and why high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP), a marker of inflammation, is an independent predictor of heart risk (JUPITER study).
2306: Acute psychological stress can literally cause a heart attack. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (broken heart syndrome) is an acute, reversible dysfunction of the left ventricle caused by intense emotional stress. Mechanism: catecholamine release → coronary spasm + direct cardiotoxicity.
2308: —Chronic sympathetic activation → increased heart rate and blood pressure → load on the endothelium
2309: —Chronic increase in cortisol → dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, pro-inflammatory profile
2310: —Impaired vagal tone → decreased HRV → predictor of cardiovascular events
2311: Hostility is the most cardiotoxic emotion. Meta-analysis by Chida and Steptoe (2009): Hostility increases the risk of CVD by 19% in healthy people and by 24% in patients with CAD.
2312: Depression after a heart attack is an independent predictor of mortality: depression increases mortality by 2-3 times within a year after a heart attack.
2314: Integrative cardiology expands traditional drug therapy for CVD:
2315: Nutrition: Mediterranean diet (PREDIMED study—reduction of CVD by 30%), DASH diet for hypertension (reduction of blood pressure by 8-14 mm Hg without medications), restriction of sodium, saturated fat, refined carbohydrates.
2316: Physical activity: 150-300 minutes of moderate exercise per week reduces CVD mortality by 35%. Cardiac rehabilitation (structured programs after MI) reduces mortality by 25%.
2317: Stress management: MBSR reduces BP, improves HRV. Transcendental meditation—meta-analysis by Anderson et al. (2008): reduction in systolic blood pressure by 4.7 mmHg. Art., diastolic at 3.2 mm Hg. Art.
2318: Sleep: normalization of sleep for obstructive apnea (CPAP therapy) reduces blood pressure and the risk of CVD. Sleep deficiency of less than 6 hours is an independent risk factor for hypertension and CVD.
2319: Omega-3: meta-analysis—reduces the risk of myocardial infarction by 8-13% when taken regularly.
2321: Heart rate variability biofeedback (HRV biofeedback) has a moderate independent evidence base: a meta-analysis by Goessl et al. (2017, Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback) showed significant reductions in anxiety in randomized trials. The HeartMath Institute commercial protocol is based on similar principles; specific claims about heart-breath-brain “synchronization” have not been replicated by independent research groups.
2322: Practice: resonant breathing (6 cycles per minute) + focusing on the heart area with positive emotional intention. Randomized studies have shown a decrease in blood pressure, cortisol, and anxiety.
