2330: CHAPTER 47. NEUROLOGY AND PSYCHIATRY: AN INTEGRATIVE VIEW
2333: THE GAP BETWEEN BRAIN AND MIND
2334: In 1949, neurosurgeon Egas Moniz received the Nobel Prize for his lobotomy—the surgical cutting of connections between the frontal lobes of the brain and other structures. It was supposed to treat mental disorders.
2335: By the time lobotomy was recognized as a terrible mistake (already in the 1950s), tens of thousands of people had been subjected to it. Many have lost their identity.
2336: This story is a warning: simplifying mental disorders into biological mechanisms without understanding human psychological complexity leads to tragedy.
2337: Biological and psychological psychiatry require both. Integrative neuropsychiatry tries to unite them.
2339: For half a century, depression has been explained by the “serotonin deficiency”—monoamine hypothesis. She justified the use of SSRIs. Problem: Antidepressants help about 40-50% of patients. What about the others?
2340: In 2012, Joel Cooperman of Emory University and Andrew Miller developed an inflammatory theory of depression: a significant proportion of patients with depression have elevated inflammatory markers (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, hs-CRP). Pro-inflammatory cytokines:
2341: —Reduce the synthesis of serotonin and dopamine (through activation of IDO-indoleamine-2,3-dioxygenase, switching tryptophan to the kynurenine pathway)
2343: —Causes “sickness behavior”—fatigue, anhedonia (decreased or complete loss of the ability to experience pleasure), social avoidance—symptoms indistinguishable from depression
2344: Clinical implication: Patients with depression and high inflammation respond less well to SSRIs but may respond better to anti-inflammatory interventions (including exercise, omega-3, lifestyle).
2346: Alzheimer's disease is a major threat to the cognitive health of the aging population. By 2050, the number of patients worldwide could exceed 130 million.
2347: Until recently, individual attempts to create an “Alzheimer's drug” (primarily targeting β-amyloid) systematically failed. A new approach: prevention through modifiable risk factors.
2348: The FINGER study by Kivipelto et al (2015) is the first large randomized trial to show that a multi-domain intervention (nutrition, exercise, cognitive training, vascular risk factor control) over 2 years improves cognitive function in at-risk older adults.
2349: Modifiable risk factors for dementia (Lancet Commission, 2020, updated 2024): 12 modifiable factors explain up to 45% of dementia cases:
2362: Anxiety disorders are the most common mental disorders.
2363: Standard treatment: CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) + SSRI/SNRI. Efficiency has been proven. But the availability of psychotherapy is limited, SSRIs have side effects, and about 30% do not respond.
2365: —Exercise: a meta-analysis by Stubbs et al. (2017)—significant reduction in anxiety
2366: —MBSR: meta-analysis by Hofmann et al. (2010)—significant reduction in anxiety
2367: —Magnesium: meta-analysis by Boyle et al. (2017)—moderate but significant reduction in anxiety in magnesium deficiency
2368: —Caffeine restriction: in anxious patients—significant contribution of caffeine to sympathetic hyperactivation
2370: One of the most exciting medical stories of our time is the return of psychedelics to psychiatry.
2371: Psilocybin (the active ingredient in magic mushrooms) has been shown in randomized trials by Roland Griffiths (Johns Hopkins University) and the Imperial College London group:
2372: — Significant (often one-time) reduction in depression and anxiety in cancer patients with existential distress
2373: —Efficacy in the treatment of persistent depression (superior to escitalopram in an open study)
2374: —Reducing cravings for alcohol in patients with alcohol dependence
2375: Mechanism: Psilocybin stimulates serotonin 5-HT2A receptors → enhances neuroplasticity → temporarily weakens the default mode network (the brain's default mode network associated with intrusive self-referential thinking).
2376: The FDA granted psilocybin Breakthrough Therapy designation for refractory depression in 2018.

2384: CHAPTER 48. AN INTEGRATIVE APPROACH TO AUTOIMMUNE DISEASES
2388: WHEN THE IMMUNITY ATTACKS ITS FRIENDS
2389: Rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, multiple sclerosis, Hashimoto's disease, inflammatory bowel disease, psoriasis, type 1 diabetes—more than 100 known autoimmune diseases that have one thing in common: the immune system attacks the body's own tissues.
2390: The global prevalence of autoimmune diseases is rising—and it's not just about better diagnosis. This is most likely a consequence of changes in the microbiome (reduced diversity), increased intestinal permeability (“leaky gut”), chronic stress and environmental factors.
2391: Women make up about 80% of patients with most autoimmune diseases—a hormonal factor is obvious but not fully understood.
2393: Autoimmune disease does not appear out of thin air. The generally accepted “three strikes” model:
2394: 1. Genetic predisposition: A number of HLA (human leukocyte antigen) genes significantly increase the risk of specific autoimmune diseases. But genes are only a predisposition, not destiny.
2395: 2. Trigger: infection (molecular mimicry—when the immune system, attacking an infection antigen, “mistakenly” attacks body proteins similar to the antigen), stress, trauma, pregnancy, exposure to toxins.
2396: 3. Violation of barriers: primarily the intestinal barrier. Alessio Fasano of Harvard has shown that impaired intestinal permeability (through a zonulin-dependent mechanism) is a predisposing factor for many autoimmune diseases—allowing antigens to enter the blood and provoke an inappropriate immune response.
2398: "Hygiene Hypothesis" (David Strachan, 1989): Reduced exposure to microorganisms in childhood (cleanliness, antibiotics, cesarean sections, formula feeding) impairs the maturation of the immune system and increases the risk of allergic and autoimmune diseases.
2400: —Children raised on farms (high microbial load) have a significantly lower risk of asthma and atopy
2401: —Introduction of antibiotics in the first 6 months of life is associated with an increased risk of asthma, obesity and inflammatory bowel disease
2402: —Cesarean section (the baby does not pass through the birth canal—the main source of initial colonization of the microbiome) is associated with an increased risk of allergies and autoimmune diseases
2403: New concept: “old friends” (Robert Reed)—microorganisms with which humanity has coexisted for millennia (including parasites) are necessary for the correct adjustment of immune tolerance. Their disappearance from the environment disrupts this setting.
2405: The connection between chronic stress and autoimmune flare-ups is well documented:
2406: —In women with rheumatoid arthritis, exacerbations correlate with psychological stress
2407: —The onset of lupus and multiple sclerosis is often associated with difficult life events
2408: —Psychological stress reduces the threshold for activation of the immune system, disrupts Treg function (regulatory T cells that suppress autoimmune reactions)
2409: Meta-analysis by Stojanovich & Marisavljevich (2008): 80% of patients with autoimmune diseases report unusually high levels of stress shortly before the onset of the disease.
2411: Integrative medicine for autoimmune diseases does not replace but complement basic therapy:
2412: Elimination diets: In a number of autoimmune conditions (celiac disease - direct link, inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis), certain food antigens provoke inflammatory activity. The Autoimmune Protocol (AIP), a variant of the paleo diet, has been shown to be effective in a number of studies for IBD and Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
2413: Restoration of the intestinal barrier: fiber, fermented foods, L-glutamine (substrate for enterocytes), elimination of pro-inflammatory factors (alcohol, NSAIDs, some food components).
2414: Stress management: MBSR, biofeedback, psychotherapy reduces inflammatory markers and improves quality of life.
2415: Omega-3: proven anti-inflammatory effect in rheumatoid arthritis—reducing the pain index and the number of inflamed joints.

2424: CHAPTER 49. METABOLIC DISORDERS: AN INTEGRATIVE VIEW
2429: In 1977, less than 5% of American adults had type 2 diabetes. Today—about 11%. Another 38% have prediabetes. Metabolic syndrome—a combination of obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and glycemic disturbances—affects more than 30% of adults in developed countries.
2430: This is not a genetic catastrophe. The genome does not change in 50 years. This is an environmental disaster: the environment in which a person lives has changed radically—and his metabolism does not have time to adapt.
2431: Type 2 diabetes was previously called “adult-onset diabetes”. Now it is diagnosed in children 8-10 years old.
2432: INSULIN RESISTANCE: THE ROOT OF THE PROBLEM
2433: Insulin resistance—impaired cells' ability to respond to insulin—is a central mechanism of type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome.
2435: —Excess fat in muscle cells and liver cells (intracellular triglycerides → blocking insulin signal)
2436: —Chronic inflammation of adipose tissue (lipotoxicity, proinflammatory cytokines from visceral fat)
2437: —Lack of physical activity (active muscles absorb glucose independent of insulin)
2438: —Sleep disturbance (one night of partial sleep deprivation reduces insulin sensitivity by 20-25%)
2439: —Chronic stress (cortisol increases glycemia, stimulates the deposition of visceral fat)
2440: Insulin resistance precedes diabetes by 10-15 years. This is a “window of opportunity” for reversible intervention.
2442: One of the most important discoveries of recent decades in metabolic medicine: type 2 diabetes is reversible in some cases.
2443: DIRECT study (Lean et al., Lancet, 2018): An intensive weight loss program (very low calorie liquid diet, 825-853 kcal/day for 3-5 months) resulted in remission of type 2 diabetes in 46% of participants after 1 year. After 2 years, remission remained in 36%.
2444: Mechanism: reduction of intrahepatic fat → restoration of liver sensitivity to insulin → normalization of fasting glucose. Reduction of intrapancreatic fat → restoration of insulin secretion by beta cells.
2445: Roy Taylor from Newcastle (developer of the “double cycle” hypothesis of type 2 diabetes) showed that remission is possible not only through a strict diet, but also through any approach that reduces visceral and intraorgan fat.
2447: Integrative medicine views metabolic disorders in their systemic context:
2449: —Reduction in refined carbohydrates (the main stimulator of insulin load)
2450: —Mediterranean or low-carb diet—both have been shown to be effective for type 2 diabetes
2451: —Limited eating window (intermittent fasting 16:8)—improving glycemic control through restoration of circadian metabolic rhythms
2454: —Strength exercise: increasing muscle mass → “sink” of glucose
2455: -HIIT (High Intensity Interval Training): Particularly effective in improving mitochondrial function and insulin sensitivity in a minimal amount of time.
2456: Sleep: normalization of sleep (treatment of obstructive apnea, sleep hygiene) is an underappreciated component of metabolic treatment.
2457: Stress management: decreased cortisol → decreased visceral fat and improved glycemic control. This has been documented in studies of MBSR in patients with type 2 diabetes.
2458: Micronutrients: magnesium (insulin signal cofactor; deficiency increases insulin resistance), chromium (potentiates insulin action), berberine (alkaloid from several plants—comparable to metformin in some studies via AMPK activation).
2460: Modern medicine finally admits: obesity is not “weakness of will.” It is a complex neurobiological and metabolic disorder.
2461: Leptin, a satiety hormone produced by adipose tissue, does not reduce appetite in obesity because the brain becomes leptin-resistant. This is not “deception” of the body; it is the same mechanism as insulin resistance.
2462: Set point theory: The brain actively protects a certain body weight through neuroendocrine mechanisms. After weight loss, basal metabolism decreases and appetite increases—the body strives to return to its original weight. It's biology, not lack of motivation.
2463: This doesn't deny the possibility of change—but it does explain why "just eat less" is insufficient and unfair advice.

2471: CHAPTER 50. THE FUTURE OF MEDICINE: PERSONALIZED, PREDICTIVE, PREVENTIVE
2475: Commercial medicine is the medicine of disease. She is excellent at treating acute conditions and quite good at managing chronic ones. But it is ill-equipped for its main task: helping a healthy person stay healthy.
2476: The future of medicine is health medicine.
2477: Leroy Hood, one of the first researchers in the field of systems biology, introduced the concept of “P4 Medicine”—medicine of the four Ps:
2478: - Predictive: predicting risks before clinical manifestations
2480: - Personalized: decisions based on individual data
2481: - Participatory: the patient as an active participant in his health
2482: Some add a fifth P—Precision, which reflects a shift from population standards to individual decisions.
2484: “Omics” technologies—genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, microbiomics—create an unprecedentedly detailed biological portrait of a person.
2485: Genome (full sequencing): Cost has dropped from $3 billion (Human Genome Project, 2003) to less than $1,000 today and falling. Genomic data allows:
2486: —Identify genetic risk factors for chronic diseases
2489: The metabolome—the complete set of metabolites in a biological fluid—reflects the current functional state: nutrition, microbiome, physical activity, stress, medications.
2490: A study by Snyder and colleagues (Cell, 2012): full multi-omics monitoring of one individual over 14 months showed thousands of dynamic changes that preceded the development of type 2 diabetes—long before clinical signs.
2492: Wearable devices (smart watches, biosensors, continuous glucose monitors) create a new opportunity: continuous physiological monitoring in real life.
2494: —Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM): revolutionized diabetes management; today it is also used by healthy people to optimize nutrition
2495: — Heart rate and HRV monitoring: prediction of stress, overtraining, infections
2496: —Sleep monitoring: detailed analysis of sleep phases
2497: —ECG monitoring: continuous detection of rhythm disturbances
2498: A Stanford study (Shamout et al., 2019): analysis of data from wearable devices in 31 thousand people identified early warning signs of disorders even before clinical symptoms, including signs of Lyme disease, diabetes, and RA.
2500: Artificial intelligence (ML, deep learning) demonstrates amazing results in a number of clinical medicine tasks:
2501: Image-based diagnostics: AI systems for analyzing X-rays, MRI, CT, and dermoscopic images achieve accuracy comparable to or superior to experts in specific tasks.
2502: Cancer: Google's DeepMind system for detecting breast cancer from mammograms reduced the number of false negatives by 9.4% and false positives by 5.7%.
2503: Diabetic Retinopathy: The FDA-cleared IDx-DR algorithm—the first autonomous AI diagnostic tool—detects retinopathy without physician intervention with 87% sensitivity.
2504: Prediction: AI models predict sepsis, acute kidney failure, and in-hospital death based on data patterns long before clinical manifestations.
2505: Limitations: AI “explains” correlations, not mechanisms. It may be biased (depending on the data it was trained on). It is not a substitute for clinical judgment and empathy.
2507: When we know genetic risks in advance, we can act preventively.
2508: BRCA1/BRCA2: Women with mutations in these genes have a 60-80% risk of breast cancer and a 40-60% risk of ovarian cancer. This knowledge allows you to make preventive decisions (more frequent screening, preventive surgeries, chemoprophylaxis).
2509: Polygenic risk scores (PRS): summing up the effect of thousands of genetic variants allows one to predict the risk of coronary artery disease, diabetes, and breast cancer with significant accuracy—decades before the disease.
2510: But predictive genetics also raises ethical questions: the right to know and not to know; psychological consequences of knowledge of genetic risk; possible discriminatory consequences in insurance and employment.
2512: The medicine of the future is not technological cold monitoring and not a return to shamanic warmth. This is synthesis.
2513: Technology provides data. But data does not heal; it treats relationships, meaning, lifestyle, human presence. AI makes a diagnosis—but the doctor shares the experience of the disease.
2514: The integrative medicine of the future uses the best tools of precision medicine to personalize each person's understanding—and the best of relational medicine to turn that understanding into healing.
2515: “Medicine must be both a science and an art and a philosophy. Each of us is both a unique biological system and a unique human history.”

2522: CHAPTER 51. ANATOMY OF A CELL: LIFE IN A MICROSCOPIC WORLD
2527: Take a page from a book. The period is at the end of this sentence. It contains about 1,000 typical cells of the human body.
2528: There are about 37 trillion cells in your body. Each of them is a completely autonomous biochemical system: produces energy, reads DNA, synthesizes proteins, responds to signals, self-heals, reproduces, dies at a programmed moment.
2529: A cell is not the “brick” of an organism. It is a living, self-sufficient unit of unimaginable complexity. Understanding how it works changes the way we look at disease: most diseases begin at the cellular level.
2531: The plasma membrane is a cell boundary, but not a wall: it is an active, dynamic barrier of a phospholipid bilayer. Fat molecules form two layers with their “tails” inward—the hydrophobic core is impermeable to most water-soluble molecules. Proteins are built into the membrane: ion channels, receptors, transporters, enzymes.
2532: The cytoskeleton is the “skeleton” of the cell, but not rigid. Three types of protein threads:
2533: —Actin filaments: cell movement, cell shape, division
2534: —Microtubules: “rails” for transporting cargo inside the cell; they are formed by tubulin
2536: The cytoskeleton is constantly being rebuilt—the cell “flows,” changes shape, and moves.
2537: The nucleus is a repository of genetic information. It is surrounded by a double nuclear membrane with nuclear pores—giant protein complexes that control the transport of molecules between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. Inside is chromatin (DNA + histone proteins) and the nucleolus (where ribosomal RNA is synthesized).
2538: MITOCHONDRIA: POWER PLANT AND COMMAND CENTER
2539: Mitochondria are organelles that provide about 90% of cellular energy in the form of ATP through the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation.
2540: Their evolutionary history is unique: mitochondria are former free-living bacteria that entered into symbiosis with the ancestor of the eukaryotic cell about 2 billion years ago. They retained their own DNA (mitochondrial DNA, mtDNA) and their own ribosomes. Mitochondrial DNA is inherited only through the maternal line.
2542: —They regulate apoptosis (programmed cell death)—it is in the mitochondria that the self-destruction signal is triggered
2543: —They generate reactive oxygen species (free radicals) as signaling molecules—in small quantities
2544: —They are involved in calcium signaling, inflammation, immune response
2545: —They are dynamic: constantly merging and dividing (mitochondrial dynamics)
2546: Mitochondrial dysfunction—impaired ATP production and increased oxidative stress—underlies many diseases: neurodegenerative, metabolic, cancer, and diseases of aging.
2548: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a branched network of membrane channels:
2549: —Rough ER (ribosome-coated): synthesis of proteins intended for secretion or insertion into the membrane
2550: —Smooth ER: lipid synthesis, fat metabolism, calcium depot, detoxification (in the liver—CYP450 enzymes are located here)
2551: ER stress—accumulation of misfolded proteins—is an important pathogenetic mechanism of type 2 diabetes, neurodegenerative diseases, and atherosclerosis.
2552: The Golgi apparatus is the “mail” of the cell: it receives proteins from the ER, modifies them (glycosylation, phosphorylation) and sends them to their destination—to the membrane, to lysosomes, for secretion.
2553: Lysosomes are the “digestive tract” of the cell: they contain enzymes that break down proteins, fats, carbohydrates, and nucleic acids. Autophagosomes—specialized structures for processing damaged organelles—merge with lysosomes. This is autophagy—the cellular “detox” described in Chapter 35.
2555: Ribosomes are molecular machines that synthesize proteins. There are tens of millions of them in a cell. Each ribosome reads mRNA (messenger RNA) and, using tRNA (transfer RNA), strings amino acids into a protein chain.
2556: Speed: The ribosome adds about 20 amino acids per second. A protein of 500 amino acids is synthesized in 25 seconds.
2557: Ribosomes are the target of many antibiotics (tetracyclines, aminoglycosides, chloramphenicol): they suppress bacterial ribosomes (70S) without affecting human ribosomes (80S). This is the principle of selective toxicity of antibiotics.
2559: A cell is not just a collection of organelles. It is an integrated system in which all components interact:
2560: —External signal (hormone, neurotransmitter) → receptor in the membrane → cascade of intracellular signals → change in gene expression → synthesis of new proteins → change in cell function
2561: —DNA damage → activation of ATM/ATR kinases → cell cycle arrest → DNA repair → resumption of division OR → apoptosis (if the damage is irreparable)
2562: —Nutrient deficiency → activation of AMPK → inhibition of mTOR → triggering of autophagy → “recycling” of cellular “garbage” → release of resources
2563: Disease is a cellular system out of balance. Treatment is about helping the system return to normal.

2571: CHAPTER 52. ANATOMY OF THE NERVOUS SYSTEM: WIRES AND SOMETHING MORE
2576: Your brain contains about 86 billion neurons. Each neuron forms on average 7,000 synaptic connections with other neurons. The total number of synapses in the human brain is about 100 trillion.
2577: For comparison: the number of stars in the Milky Way is about 300 billion. The number of synapses in your brain is 300 times greater than the number of stars in our galaxy.
2578: “The brain is the most complex object in the universe, as far as we know,” says neuroscientist Christoph Koch. And he adds: “This creates an obvious problem for studying it.”
2580: A neuron is a specialized cell adapted to transmit electrical and chemical signals.
2582: —Soma (cell body): contains the nucleus and the main metabolic apparatus
2583: —Dendrites: branched processes that receive signals from other neurons; one neuron can have thousands of dendrites
2584: —Axon: a long process that transmits a signal from the cell body to the terminal; can be from fractions of a millimeter to 1 meter long (sciatic nerve axons)
2585: —Myelin sheath: the fatty sheath of the axon formed by Schwann cells (peripheral NS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS); increases the speed of conduction by 50-100 times through “jumping” conduction between nodes of Ranvier
2586: The neuron is a polarized cell: at rest, there is a negative potential inside (-70 mV). When activated, an action potential: a wave of depolarization propagating along the axon.
2587: Synapse is the point of contact between two neurons:
2588: 1. The presynaptic neuron releases a neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft
2589: 2. The neurotransmitter binds to the receptors of the postsynaptic neuron
2590: 3. Receptors open ion channels → change in postsynaptic membrane potential → excitation or inhibition
2592: The brain is structurally organized into a hierarchy:
2593: Brain stem (medulla oblongata, pons, midbrain): the oldest part. Controls vital functions—breathing, heartbeat, swallowing, wakefulness. The nuclei of most cranial nerves are located here.
2594: Cerebellum: motor coordination, balance, some aspects of cognition and emotion. Contains about 69 billion neurons—more than the rest of the brain.
2596: —Thalamus: “switching station”—all sensory signals (except olfactory) pass through the thalamus on their way to the cortex
2597: —Hypothalamus: connection between the nervous and endocrine systems; regulation of temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep, sexual behavior, stress response (HPA axis)
2599: —Hippocampus: episodic memory formation, spatial navigation, neurogenesis
2600: —Amygdala (amygdala): emotional assessment of stimuli, fear, social perception
2602: Neocortex (new cortex): 80% of the human brain volume. Six layers of neurons. Higher cognitive functions:
2603: —Frontal lobe: planning, decision making, behavioral control, speech (Broca's area)
2604: —Parietal lobe: integration of sensory information, spatial perception
2608: The ANS is part of the nervous system that regulates the functioning of internal organs. Is not subject to conscious control (except for breathing, as described in Chapter 32).
2609: Sympathetic department: “hit and run.” Mobilization of resources under stress. Activates the heart, dilates the bronchi, redistributes blood to the muscles, suppresses digestion, dilates the pupils.
2610: Parasympathetic department: “rest and digest.” Recovery and maintenance. Slows down the heart, stimulates digestion, constricts the pupils. The main nerve—vagus (vagus nerve, X cranial nerve)—innervates the heart, lungs, intestines, liver, and pancreas.
2611: Porges's polyvagal theory (Chapter 11): to these two systems is added the “ventral vagus”—an evolutionarily young system of social involvement that connects heart rate with facial expressions, voice, and hearing.
2612: Enteric nervous system: the “second brain”—about 500 million neurons in the intestinal wall that can operate autonomously from the central nervous system.
2614: Neuroglia make up about 85% of brain cells (not neurons). It has long been considered “glue” (literally: glue in Greek). Today, the key participants in brain function are:
2615: Astrocytes: support neurons with nutrients, regulate synaptic transmission (synaptic glia), participate in the formation of the blood-brain barrier, form the glymphatic system (sleep and brain cleansing).
2616: Microglia: immune cells of the brain. Normally, it is in a “resting” state, constantly scanning its environment. When damaged, they activate, migrate, and absorb debris. Chronic activation of microglia is a key mechanism of neuroinflammation in depression, Alzheimer's disease, and autism.
2617: Oligodendrocytes: form myelin in the CNS. Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune destruction of oligodendrocyte myelin.

Equal numbers of neuronal and nonneuronal cells make the human brain an isometrically scaled-up primate brain. Journal of Comparative Neurology, 513(5), 532–541. https://doi.org/10.1002/cne.21974

2621: Kandel, E. R., Schwartz, J. H., Jessell, T. M., Siegelbaum, S. A., & Hudspeth, A. J. (Eds.). (2013). Principles of neural science (5th ed.). McGraw-Hill.

2623: Squire, L. R., Berg, D., Bloom, F. E., du Lac, S., Ghosh, A., & Spitzer, N. C. (Eds.). (2012). Fundamental neuroscience (4th ed.). Academic Press.
2625: CHAPTER 53. ANATOMY OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM: THE ARMY WE DO NOT NOTICE
2630: While you are reading these lines, there is a war going on in your body.
2631: Right now, the immune system detects and destroys several thousand cells that have become cancerous (mutant cells appear in the body every day—and almost all of them are destroyed by immune surveillance). Right now, neutrophils are ingesting bacteria that have penetrated through microscopic breaks in the skin. Right now, T cells are “remembering” the viruses you encountered twenty years ago.
2632: The immune system is not “defense” in the passive sense. It is an active, learning, adapting, intelligence and response system distributed throughout the body.
2634: Innate immunity is an evolutionarily ancient, fast, nonspecific defense. It does not require prior knowledge of the pathogen—it responds to common molecular patterns characteristic of microbes (PAMPs—Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns).
2636: Neutrophils are the most numerous leukocytes (60-70% of all). They arrive first at the site of infection through chemotaxis. They absorb and destroy microbes (phagocytosis)—and die in the process (pus = dead neutrophils).
2637: Macrophages are “big eaters” (literally). Present in all tissues under different names: Kupffer cells in the liver, microglia in the brain, osteoclasts in the bones, alveolar macrophages in the lungs. They phagocytose, present antigens, and secrete cytokines.
2638: NK cells (natural killers)—destroy cells lacking the “self” signal (MHC I), which is typical for tumor and virus-infected cells. Mechanism: perforin + granzyme → target apoptosis.
2639: Dendritic cells are “border guards”. They sit in tissues at the borders with the external environment (skin, intestines, lungs). They capture antigens, migrate to lymph nodes, and “train” T cells.
2641: Adaptive immunity is an evolutionarily young system, characteristic only of vertebrates. It is specific, slow (1-2 weeks for the first response), but forms immune memory—the basis of vaccination.
2642: The two main players are T lymphocytes and B lymphocytes.
2643: T cells mature in the thymus (thymus—hence the “T”). Three main types:
2644: —CD4+ T helper cells: “commanders” of the adaptive immune response. Activate B cells, CD8+ T cells, macrophages through the secretion of cytokines. Target of HIV.
2645: —CD8+ Cytotoxic T cells: directly kill virus-infected and tumor cells. The mechanism is the same as that of NK: perforin + granzyme.
2646: —Regulatory T cells (Treg): suppress the immune response, preventing autoimmunity and allergies. Their dysfunction is a central mechanism of autoimmune diseases.
2647: B cells mature in the bone marrow (bone marrow). When activated, they turn into plasma cells that secrete antibodies—immunoglobulins (IgG, IgM, IgA, IgE, IgD). Antibodies:
2653: The immune system is distributed throughout the body through the lymphatic system:
2654: —Bone marrow: production of all blood cells and immune cells (hematopoiesis); B cell maturation
2655: —Thymus: maturation of T cells; most active in childhood, after puberty—gradually involutes
2656: —Lymph nodes: “processing centers”—here antigens meet T and B cells; located throughout the body; inflamed “glands” during infection—that’s what they are
2657: —Spleen: filters blood, “checks” for antigens, stores a supply of lymphocytes
2658: —Tonsils and adenoids: “advanced outpost” at the entrance to the respiratory tract and digestive tract
2659: —Gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT): 70% of the immune system is located in the gut
2661: Inflammation is a fundamental immune response. Classic signs (Celsus, 1st century AD): redness (rubor), heat (calor), swelling (tumor), pain (dolor), dysfunction (functio laesa).
2662: Acute inflammation: a protective, time-limited response to injury or infection. Necessary and useful.
2663: Chronic inflammation: persistent, systemic, low-level inflammatory process—silent inflammation. It is a common pathogenetic mechanism of atherosclerosis, type 2 diabetes, neurodegenerative diseases, cancer, and depression.
2665: Causes of chronic inflammation: obesity (adipose tissue is a pro-inflammatory “organ”), microbiome disorder, chronic stress, sleep disturbance, lack of movement, pro-inflammatory diet.

2673: CHAPTER 54. ANATOMY OF THE ENDOCRINE SYSTEM: HORMONAL ORCHESTRA
2678: The nervous system controls the body through electrical signals—fast, precise, and wired. The endocrine system controls through chemical signals—slower, more diffuse, but often more stable and deeper.
2679: Hormone (from the Greek hormao - “I excite”) is a chemical substance secreted by the cells of one tissue into the bloodstream and acting on the cells of other tissues containing the corresponding receptors.
2680: More than 80 hormones have been identified in the human body. They regulate everything: metabolism and growth, reproduction and stress, sleep and appetite, mood and immune function. The endocrine system and nervous system are so intertwined that their interaction is classified as a separate field—neuroendocrinology.
2681: CHIEF CONDUCTOR: HYPOTHALAMUS AND PITUITARY Gland
2682: The hypothalamus—a small structure (4 g) at the base of the brain—is the “master” of the endocrine system. It receives signals from throughout the brain—emotions, stress, circadian rhythms, temperature, nutrient levels—and translates them into hormonal commands to the pituitary gland.
2683: Hypothalamic-pituitary axis: The hypothalamus secretes releasing hormones (liberins) and inhibitory hormones (statins) that act on the cells of the anterior pituitary gland.
2684: The pituitary gland is the “conductor of the orchestra”—the master of the gland. Two beats:
2692: The posterior lobe (neurohypophysis) does not produce, but stores and secretes hormones synthesized in the hypothalamus:
2693: —ADH (antidiuretic hormone, vasopressin): regulation of water balance, social behavior
2695: ADRENAL GLANDS: HORMONES OF STRESS AND LIFE
2696: The adrenal glands—two small organs above the kidneys—produce critical hormones.
2698: —Cortisol (glucocorticoid): the main stress hormone. Mobilizes glucose, suppresses immunity (temporarily), affects mood and cognition. Secreted according to a circadian rhythm—peak in the morning.
2700: —DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone): a precursor of sex hormones, a neurosteroid; decreases with age
2704: HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis: the classic stress response system. During stress: CRH (hypothalamus) → ACTH (pituitary gland) → cortisol (adrenal glands). Chronic activation → chronically high cortisol → systemic consequences (Chapter 28).
2706: The thyroid gland—the “butterfly” at the base of the neck—produces:
2707: —T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine): regulate basal metabolism, growth, brain development, mood
2708: —Calcitonin: reduces calcium levels in the blood
2709: T4—prohormone; in tissues it is converted into active T3 (5'-deiodinase). This transformation is disrupted by stress, selenium and zinc deficiency, and inflammation.
2710: Hypothyroidism: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, bradycardia. Often - Hashimoto's disease (autoimmune destruction of thyroid tissue). The prevalence in women is up to 10%.
2711: Hyperthyroidism: tachycardia, weight loss, anxiety, diarrhea, proptosis in Graves' disease (also autoimmune).
2712: Screening: TSH is a “litmus test” for thyroid function. With a normal TSH, the pituitary gland is “happy” with the level of thyroid hormones.
2714: Estrogens (estradiol in women): reproduction, but also cardiovascular protection (explains the lower risk of CHD in women before menopause), neuroplasticity, mood, bone density.
2715: Progesterone: pregnancy maintenance, neuroprotection (GABAergic action), regulation of the menstrual cycle.
2716: Testosterone (in men and, in smaller amounts, in women): muscle mass, libido, mood, cognition, cardiovascular metabolism. Decreases with age (andropause): fatigue, depression, decreased libido.
2717: Insulin is a hormone of the pancreas. Often perceived only as a “sugar hormone,” but it is a broad-spectrum anabolic hormone: it stimulates the synthesis of proteins, fats, and glycogen.
2718: Leptin—adipose tissue hormone: satiety, energy level, immune function. In obesity, leptin resistance (Chapter 49).

2726: CHAPTER 55. ANATOMY OF THE INTESTINE: THE SECOND BRAIN
2731: The digestive tract is essentially a long tube that is open at both ends. From a topological point of view, the inside of the intestine is the outside of the body: the contents of the intestine are never “inside” in the strict sense until they are absorbed through the epithelium.
2732: The total length of the digestive tract is about 9 meters. The surface area of ​​the intestinal mucosa—due to folds, villi, and microvilli—is about 32 square meters (the size of two tennis courts) packed into the abdominal cavity.
2733: This enormous contact area makes the intestine the main interface between the outside world and the body's internal environment. Every day, kilograms of food, liters of liquid, trillions of microbes pass through it. The intestines must simultaneously effectively absorb nutrients and protect against harmful agents.
2735: Oral cavity: mechanical grinding, beginning of chemical digestion of carbohydrates (salivary amylase), wetting, taste evaluation. The bacterial flora of the oral cavity—about 700 species—has an independent influence on systemic health (chapter on the microbiome).
2736: Esophagus: muscular tube, transport of bolus food through peristalsis. Lower esophageal sphincter: valve that prevents reflux.
2737: Stomach: reservoir and “mixer”. Hydrochloric acid (pH 1.5-2): denaturation of proteins, destruction of microbes, activation of pepsin. Pepsin: beginning of proteolysis. Intrinsic factor: necessary for the absorption of vitamin B12 in the small intestine; its absence in atrophic gastritis → B12-deficiency anemia.
2738: Small intestine (duodenum, jejunum and ileum—about 6 meters in total): the main site of digestion and absorption. The pancreas secretes enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases) and bicarbonate (neutralizes the acidic contents of the stomach). Bile from the liver (bile acids)—emulsification of fats.
2739: Villi and microvilli (brush border) of the small intestine: increasing the area of ​​absorption. Damage to the brush border in celiac disease, inflammatory bowel diseases → malabsorption syndrome.
2740: Large intestine: absorption of water and electrolytes, formation of feces. The main ecological niche of the microbiome is trillions of bacteria producing short-chain fatty acids (SCFA), vitamins, and neurotransmitters.
2742: The intestinal barrier is the body's first immune defense. A single layer of epithelial cells (enterocytes, goblet cells, Paneth cells)—one cell thick—separates trillions of gut bacteria from the body's sterile internal environment.
2743: Tight junctions—protein complexes between epithelial cells—regulate barrier permeability.
2744: Disruption of tight junctions (“leaky gut”)—increased intestinal permeability—allows bacterial antigens (LPS, peptidoglycan) to enter the bloodstream, activating systemic inflammation.
2745: Fasano et al: Zonulin, a protein that regulates tight junctions, is increased in:
2749: Increased intestinal permeability is associated with autoimmune diseases, IBD, type 1 diabetes, and depression.
2751: The enteric nervous system (ENS) contains approximately 500 million neurons—more than the spinal cord. It forms a complex neural network in the intestinal wall, capable of functioning autonomously from the central nervous system.
2757: 90% of the vagus nerve fibers come from the gut to the brain—not from the brain to the gut. The gut speaks to the brain, not just the brain to the gut.
2763: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS): a disorder of the neuromuscular function of the intestine. Psychological stress is a leading trigger: the brain-gut axis in both directions.

2771: CHAPTER 56. ANATOMY OF THE HEART AND VESSELS: RIVER OF LIFE
2776: The heart begins to beat on the 22nd day after conception—even before the brain has formed. And he doesn’t stop until the last second of his life.
2777: Over the course of 75 years of life, the heart makes about 2.7 billion contractions. Pumps about 200 million liters of blood. During intense physical activity, the cardiac output increases from 5 liters to 25 liters—fivefold—within seconds.
2778: The cardiovascular system is the delivery system for oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and immune cells to all cells of the body. Carbon dioxide, metabolic waste—from cells to excretory organs.
2780: The heart is a hollow muscular organ the size of a fist. Located in the chest, between the lungs, slightly shifted to the left. Weight—250-350 g.
2782: —Right atrium: receives venous blood from the systemic circulation (superior and inferior vena cava)
2783: —Right ventricle: pumps blood to the lungs (pulmonary circulation)
2784: —Left atrium: receives oxygenated blood from the lungs
2785: —Left ventricle: the main “pump”—pumps blood into the aorta for systemic circulation. The wall is much thicker than the right ventricle (the pressure of the aortic blood flow is 5-6 times higher than that of the pulmonary one).
2786: Heart valves (tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, aortic): provide unidirectional blood flow. Their defeat in rheumatic endocarditis, calcification, infective endocarditis is the cause of heart defects.
2787: The myocardium—cardiac muscle—is unique: cardiomyocytes are connected by intercalary discs with gap junctions, which allows the entire myocardium to contract synchronously as a single functional syncytium.
2789: Heart rhythm is set by specialized cells:
2790: Sinus node (sinoatrial node): “pacemaker”—located in the right atrium; generates 60-100 pulses per minute. Automaticity is its property: it beats without external stimuli.
2791: AV node (atrioventricular node): delays the conduction of the impulse by 0.1 seconds - allows the atria to contract and fill the ventricles with blood before they contract.
2792: Bundle of His → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers: rapid conduction through the ventricles → synchronous contraction.
2793: The ECG reflects the total electrical activity of the heart: P-wave (atrial depolarization), QRS complex (ventricular depolarization), T-wave (ventricular repolarization). ECG analysis is the basis for diagnosing arrhythmias, ischemia, and heart attack.
2794: Heart rate variability (HRV): subtle variations in the intervals between beats influenced by the autonomic nervous system. High HRV is a sign of healthy vagal regulation. A decrease in HRV is an independent predictor of cardiovascular events.
2796: The total length of blood vessels in the human body is about 100,000 km. If you stretch them in a line, you can go around the Earth twice.
2797: Arteries: carry blood away from the heart. Powerful muscle walls can withstand high pressure. Aorta—main arterial trunk, diameter ~2.5 cm, pressure ~120/80 mm Hg. Art.
2798: Arterioles: the “tap” of blood flow—the main site of resistance. Their tone regulates blood pressure.
2799: Capillaries: wall—one layer of endothelial cells. Here gas and nutrient exchange occurs between blood and tissues.
2800: Veins: carry blood to the heart. Low pressure. Valves prevent backflow. Varicose veins are incompetent venous valves.
2801: The endothelium—the inner lining of blood vessels—is not just a passive wall: it is an endocrine organ that produces NO (nitric oxide—a powerful vasodilator, Nobel Prize 1998), prostaglandins, thrombomodulin, PAI-1. Endothelial dysfunction is an early sign of atherosclerosis.
2803: Blood is a liquid connective tissue. Compound:
2804: Plasma (55%): water, proteins (albumin, globulins, clotting factors), electrolytes, nutrients, hormones, waste.
2805: Erythrocytes (red blood cells): 4-6 million per mm³. Nuclear-free, they contain hemoglobin, a carrier of oxygen and CO₂. They live for 120 days, then are destroyed in the spleen.
2806: Leukocytes (white blood cells): 4,500-11,000 per mm³. Cells of the immune system (described in Chapter 53).
2807: Platelets: 150,000-400,000 per mm³. Fragments of megakaryocytes. Participate in primary hemostasis (platelet clot when a vessel is damaged).
2808: Hemoglobin: four subunits, each with heme, an iron-containing porphyrin. The iron at the center of the heme binds O₂. Disorders: anemia (iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, hemolysis, bone marrow failure), sickle cell anemia (genetic defect of hemoglobin).

2816: CHAPTER 57. PHYSICS OF THE BODY: PRESSURE, WAVES, ELECTRICITY
2821: Physics is the science of nature: movement, forces, energy, waves. The human body obeys physical laws as strictly as any other physical object. But it uses physics with unimaginable sophistication.
2822: The laws of thermodynamics determine how cells produce and use energy. The laws of hydrodynamics—how blood flows through vessels. The laws of electromagnetism—how neurons transmit signals. Laws of mechanics—how muscles generate force.
2823: Understanding the physics of the body is not academic knowledge. This is the basis for understanding how medical instruments and physical therapy interventions work.
2825: Blood pressure is the force with which blood presses against the walls of the arteries. Measured in mmHg. Art. (historically, the height of the mercury column raised by this pressure).
2828: In the cardiovascular system: Blood pressure = Cardiac output × Peripheral resistance
2829: Systolic (~120 mmHg)—pressure at the moment of contraction (systole) of the ventricle
2830: Diastolic (~80 mmHg)—pressure at the moment of relaxation (diastole)
2831: Pulse pressure = Systolic − Diastolic. Increased pulse pressure (>60 mmHg) is a sign of aortic stiffness and a predictor of cardiovascular risk in the elderly.
2832: Poiseuille's law describes the flow of liquid through a tube: Q = (πr⁴ΔP) / (8ηL), where r is the radius of the tube. Reducing the radius by half reduces the flow by 16 times. This explains why the narrowing of the coronary arteries in atherosclerosis is so critical: even a small decrease in the lumen dramatically reduces blood flow.
2834: Sound is a mechanical pressure wave propagating in an elastic medium. The stethoscope, discovered by Laennec in 1816 (he rolled sheets of paper into a tube), uses sound waves to pass through tissue. Korotkoff sounds when measuring pressure are sound phenomena.
2835: Ultrasound (>20,000 Hz) in medical diagnostics uses the difference in acoustic impedance of tissues. At the boundary of two tissues with different acoustic resistance, part of the wave is reflected—the echo is recorded by the sensor. Echocardiography, ultrasound of the abdominal cavity, Doppler study of blood flow - all this is acoustics.
2836: The physical principle of Doppler: the frequency of the wave received by a moving observer (or reflected from a moving object) differs from the original one. Blood flowing to the transducer reflects higher frequency sound; from the sensor—lower. From this frequency shift the blood flow velocity is calculated.
2838: Neural signals are electrochemical. Ion channels—Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻—create a potential difference across the membrane (-70 mV at rest). The action potential—a wave of depolarization—propagates along the axon at a speed of 0.5 to 120 m/s (depending on myelination).
2839: The ECG measures the total electrical field of the heart—the integral vector of all cardiac cells projected onto the lead axis. This is the physics of bipolar and unipotential leads.
2840: EEG measures the potential difference between electrodes on the surface of the scalp. Brain rhythms—delta (<4 Hz, deep sleep), theta (4-8 Hz, sleep/meditation), alpha (8-12 Hz, relaxed wakefulness), beta (13-30 Hz, active thinking), gamma (>30 Hz, information integration).
2841: Biofeedback is the use of EMG, EEG, ECG to provide a person with real-time information about his physiological parameters, allowing him to consciously regulate them. Evidence base for migraines, ADHD, anxiety, chronic pain.
2843: The first law of thermodynamics: energy is neither created nor destroyed, only transformed. Chemical energy of food → heat + mechanical work + biochemical reactions.
2844: Second law: the entropy of a system tends to increase. Living organisms are open systems that maintain low entropy due to the continuous absorption of energy from the environment.
2845: Muscle efficiency: ~25% of the chemical energy of ATP is converted into mechanical work; the rest is heat. This explains overheating during physical activity and the need for thermoregulation.
2846: Thermoregulation—maintaining body temperature within a narrow range (36.5-37.5°C). Mechanisms: vasoconstriction/vasodilation of cutaneous vessels, sweating, shivering (muscle contractions to produce heat), metabolic changes. The brain thermostat is in the hypothalamus.
2848: Muscle is a mechanochemical converter: chemical energy ATP → mechanical work. Sliding filament model (Huxley): actin and myosin filaments slide past each other—sarcomere shortens → muscle contracts.
2849: Bone can withstand a compressive load of about 170 MPa—more than concrete. This is a consequence of the hierarchical structure: from collagen fibrils to trabecular and compact bone. Piezoelectric effect of bone: Bone deformation under load generates an electrical potential that stimulates osteoblastic activity—a mechanism of bone adaptation to load (Wolf's law).
2850: Using the principle of leverage with joints: the knee joint as a hinge, the spine as a balancing rod, the lower jaw as a second-class lever.
2852: Bear, M. F., Connors, B. W., & Paradiso, M. A. (2020). Neuroscience: Exploring the brain (4th ed.). Jones & Bartlett Learning.

2855: Nordin, M., & Frankel, V. H. (2001). Basic biomechanics of the musculoskeletal system (3rd ed.). Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.
2856: Schrödinger, E. (1944). What is life? The physical aspect of the living cell. Cambridge University Press.
2858: CHAPTER 58. PHYSICS OF DIAGNOSTICS: MRI, PET, X-RAY
2863: Before 1895, the only way to see what was going on inside a living person's body was to open it surgically. One random experiment changed everything.
2864: In November 1895, Wilhelm Roentgen, while studying cathode rays, noticed that a fluorescent screen at the other end of a dark room was glowing, although the cathode tube was in a casing. X-rays—X-rays—passed through paper, cardboard and fabrics. A few weeks later, he took a picture of his wife Bertha's hand—the first x-ray in history. Bertha looked at the picture and said: “I saw my own death.”
2865: For his discovery, Roentgen received the first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901.
2867: X-rays are electromagnetic radiation with a wavelength of 0.01-10 nm (between ultraviolet and gamma radiation). They penetrate soft tissues, but are absorbed by dense structures (bones, metals).
2868: The principle of radiography: a film (or digital detector) behind an object records the intensity of the transmitted radiation. Dense structures (bones, calcifications) are white. The air is black. Fabrics are intermediate shades of gray.
2869: Computed tomography (CT): a series of X-rays from different angles around the body, mathematically reconstructed into a three-dimensional image. A CT scan provides a detailed cross-sectional image—“illuminating” the abdominal cavity and seeing organs individually.
2870: Risks: Ionizing radiation damages DNA. One chest CT scan is equivalent to 200 radiographs and corresponds to ~18 months of background radiation. Rationale: The benefit of the clinical decision must outweigh the potential radiation risk.
2872: MRI, the most informative method for imaging soft tissue, does not use ionizing radiation. The physical principle is nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR).
2874: 1. Protons (hydrogen nuclei in water and fats) have a magnetic moment—behave like tiny magnets
2875: 2. In the strong magnetic field of an MRI (1.5-3 Tesla—50,000 times stronger than the Earth's magnetic field), protons line up along the field
2877: 4. Returning to their original position, protons emit a radio signal that is detected by coils
2878: 5. Relaxation time (T1, T2) varies in different tissues → image contrast
2879: Different MRI modes (T1-weighted, T2-weighted, FLAIR, DWI) detect different pathologies. fMRI (functional MRI) measures changes in blood oxygenation (BOLD signal) as an indirect marker of neural activity—the basis of most modern research into brain function.
2880: Contraindications: metal implants (pacemakers, a number of surgical clips, metal fragments)—due to a strong magnetic field.
2882: PET is a nuclear medicine method that evaluates the metabolic activity of tissues.
2884: 1. The patient is administered a radiopharmaceutical—a metabolically active substance labeled with a positron-emitting isotope. Most often—FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose, an analogue of glucose with F-18)
2885: 2. Cells with high metabolism (tumor, brain when activated) absorb FDG
2886: 3. F-18 emits a positron → annihilation with an electron → two gamma photons at 180° → detected by PET ring detectors
2887: 4. The computer reconstructs the three-dimensional distribution of radioactivity
2888: PET provides a functional image (what works, not what looks like). PET-CT—a combination of PET and CT—allows you to combine functional and anatomical information.
2889: Applications: oncology (detection of metastases, assessment of response to treatment), cardiology (myocardial viability), neurology (Alzheimer's disease, epilepsy).
2891: Ultrasound is the safest method: no ionizing radiation, no strong magnetic fields. Widely used in obstetrics, cardiology, and abdominal diagnostics.
2892: Physics: A piezoelectric sensor generates ultrasonic waves and records the reflected echo. Delay time → distance to structure. Amplitude → tissue density.
2893: Doppler ultrasound measures the speed of blood flow by the frequency shift of reflected waves. Duplex scanning combines B-mode (anatomy) with Doppler (blood flow).
2894: Limitations: does not pass through bones and air (lungs, intestines) well. Image quality depends on the operator.
2898: —CT: emergency diagnostics, abdominal cavity, vessels (with contrast)
2899: —MRI: brain, spinal cord, joints, soft tissues, without radiation exposure
2901: —Ultrasound: abdominal cavity, heart, blood vessels, obstetrics, soft tissues
2902: The decision to select a method is a balance between diagnostic value, availability, cost and safety for a particular clinical question.
2904: Bushberg, J. T., Seibert, J. A., Leidholdt, E. M., Jr., & Boone, J. M. (2020). The essential physics of medical imaging (3rd ed.). Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

2910: CHAPTER 59. QUANTUM BIOLOGY: ON THE THRESHOLD OF A NEW UNDERSTANDING
2915: Quantum mechanics describes the behavior of particles on subatomic scales—and is only related to classical physics by its mathematical rigor. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle, the quantum tunnel effect, the superposition of states are phenomena that seem completely alien to the “common sense” of the macrocosm.
2916: For a long time it was believed that quantum effects disappear in warm, moist, many-particle biological systems. Quantum coherence is a fragile phenomenon that exists only in extreme conditions (ultra-low temperatures, vacuum).
2917: This belief began to crumble in the 2000s. It turns out that living systems are—possibly—exploiting quantum effects for surprisingly efficient functions.
2919: In 2007, Graham Fleming and his group at Berkeley published data in Nature that shocked physicists and biologists at the same time.
2920: In photosynthetic pigment complexes of plants, algae and bacteria, when a photon is absorbed, energy is transferred from the antenna pigment to the reaction center with almost 100% efficiency. This is striking—classical mechanisms predict much lower efficiency.
2921: Fleming showed that this occurs through a quantum phenomenon—coherent energy transfer. The photon “tests” several paths simultaneously (like a wave) and chooses the most efficient one. This is a quantum superposition in a warm, wet biological molecule—something that was thought impossible.
2922: The implications of this discovery for the understanding of biology remain to be assessed.
2924: The quantum tunneling effect is the ability of a particle to “pass through” an energy barrier that classically it could not overcome.
2925: Judith Claypool and her group have shown that a number of enzymes (including alcohol dehydrogenase and amine oxidase) catalyze hydrogen transfer reactions through a tunnel mechanism—transfer occurs much faster than classical chemical kinetics predicts.
2926: This means: the quantum tunneling effect is not exotic, but perhaps a basic mechanism of enzymatic catalysis, providing the speed of biochemical reactions necessary for life.
2927: If this is true, then life uses quantum mechanics at every step.
2929: European robins migrate annually from Scandinavia to Africa and back, accurately guided by the Earth's magnetic field. The physical mechanism of this navigation has long remained a mystery.
2930: Schulten-Ritz-Hore hypothesis: the retina of birds contains a protein called cryptochrome, in which a photon generates a pair of “entangled” (correlated) radicals. Their spin state is sensitive to the Earth's magnetic field → signal to the nervous system → information about the direction of the field.
2931: This is quantum entanglement as a biological compass—if the hypothesis is confirmed, it will be the first proven example of the biological use of quantum entanglement.
2932: Experimental data from Rottertdam: Placing robins in an electromagnetic field that disrupts quantum coherence disrupted their navigation. This is indirect but convincing evidence.
2934: Traditional theory of smell: odor molecules bind to receptors based on shape (lock and key) → nerve signal.
2935: Luca Turin proposed an alternative theory: odor receptors work as molecular spectrometers, “sensing” the characteristic vibrational frequencies of molecules through a tunneling effect. This would explain why different molecules with similar shapes (stereoisomers) smell different, and molecules that smell similar have completely different shapes.
2936: The scientific community is divided: some studies confirm the predictions of the quantum theory of smell, while others refute them. The question remains open.
2938: Quantum biology is a young science. Its practical medical implications are still speculative. But the direction is important:
2939: Enzyme catalysis: Understanding the quantum tunneling mechanism in enzymes opens up new avenues for drug development—for example, isotopic substitutions that disrupt the tunneling effect in pathogen enzymes.
2940: Photosensitizers in oncology: Photodynamic therapy uses molecules that absorb light and generate reactive oxygen species. Understanding quantum energy transfer could make them more efficient.
2941: DNA and mutations: tunneling transfer of protons in DNA hydrogen bonds—a possible mechanism for spontaneous mutations (theory of Löwdin, 1963). If mutations are partially of a quantum nature, this changes the understanding of oncogenesis.

2947: Turin, L. (1996). A spectroscopic mechanism for primary olfactory reception. Chemical Senses, 21(6), 773–791. https://doi.org/10.1093/chemse/21.6.773
2949: CHAPTER 60. THERMODYNAMICS OF HEALTH: ENERGY, ENTROPY, ORDER
2954: In 1944, Erwin Schrödinger, one of the founders of quantum mechanics, gave a lecture in Dublin, later published as the essay “What is Life?” This essay had a huge influence on young scientists, including Watson and Crick, who discovered the structure of DNA.
2955: Schrödinger formulated the question thermodynamically: living organisms violate the second law of thermodynamics—they maintain order (low entropy) in a world tending toward disorder (high entropy). How do they do this?
2956: Answer: living systems are open systems. They maintain their orderliness by absorbing “orderliness” (negentropy) from the environment—in the form of food, sunlight. And they release “clutter”—in the form of heat and waste.
2957: Life is a local decrease in entropy due to its increase in the environment.
2959: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the universal “currency” of biological energy. All cellular processes that require energy use ATP: muscle contraction, active ion transport, protein synthesis, DNA repair.
2960: ATP is “charged” through mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. Krebs cycle → electron transport chain → ATP synthase.
2961: ATP synthase is a molecular machine that literally acts like a turbine: the flow of protons through the membrane rotates the central subunit of the enzyme → conformational changes → synthesis of ATP from ADP + phosphate. Rotation frequency is about 150 revolutions per second.
2962: At rest, a person produces and consumes his weight in ATP every day (≈40 kg!). Under intense load, up to 0.5 kg of ATP per minute. The ATP molecule “lives” for about a minute and is constantly recycled.
2964: Disease is a disorder of biological organization. Thermodynamically: an increase in entropy in a system.
2965: This is not just a metaphor:
2966: Cancer: transformation, distortion of cellular differentiation (increased disorder), disruption of cellular architecture, shutdown of apoptosis (programmed “order” of death), abnormal metabolism (Warburg effect—tumors “prefer” glycolysis even in the presence of oxygen).
2967: Neurodegeneration: The accumulation of aggregated, misfolded proteins (amyloid, tau, α-synuclein) is literally a pathological accumulation of molecular disorder. Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington's diseases are “diseases of protein misfolding.”
2968: Atherosclerosis: disruption of the regular structure of the vascular wall, disordered accumulation of foam cells, calcium, fibrosis—biological “architectural degradation.”
2970: Arnold Goldberger of Harvard developed the concept of fractal physiology: many biological signals and structures of a healthy body exhibit fractal self-similarity—a pattern that resembles itself at different scales of viewing.
2971: The bronchial tree, the vascular tree, neural networks, the heart rhythm of a healthy person—they are all fractal: large branches repeat the pattern of small ones, large-scale fluctuations repeat small-scale fluctuations.
2972: Disease → loss of fractality. Heart rhythm in heart failure becomes more monotonous and regular (loss of complexity). EEG for epilepsy is more regular and synchronous. The “paradox” of health: health is complexity and variability, but disease is a loss of complexity.
2973: Goldberger introduced the concept of “complexity loss” as a conceptual framework for aging and disease. Biomarkers of complexity—HRV, respiratory apnea pauses, EEG fractal dimension—decrease with age and disease.
2975: Ilya Prigogine received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1977 for his theory of dissipative structures. She explains how order emerges from chaos.
2976: A dissipative structure is a system far from thermodynamic equilibrium that maintains its organization through a constant flow of energy/matter, dissipating (“dissipating”) energy.
2977: A living cell is a dissipative structure: it maintains its organized state through a constant flow of ATP, nutrients, and information. If the flow stops, the cell will die and decompose to an equilibrium state.
2978: The organism as a whole is a dissipative structure: it maintains its organization through the flow of food, air, water, and information. Health is a stable non-equilibrium state. Death—return to thermodynamic equilibrium.
2980: Goldberger, A. L., Amaral, L. A., Hausdorff, J. M., Ivanov, P. C., Peng, C.-K., & Stanley, H. E. (2002). Fractal dynamics in physiology: Alterations with disease and aging. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 99(Suppl 1), 2466–2472. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.012579499
2981: Prigogine, I. (1977). Self-organization in nonequilibrium systems: From dissipative structures to order through fluctuations. Wiley.
2982: Schrödinger, E. (1944). What is life? The physical aspect of the living cell. Cambridge University Press.
2983: West, G. B. (2017). Scale: The universal laws of growth, innovation, sustainability, and the pace of life in organisms, cities, economies, and companies. Penguin Press.
2984: Warburg, O. (1956). On the origin of cancer cells. Science, 123(3191), 309–314. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.123.3191.309
2986: CHAPTER 61. BIOMECHANICS AND POSTURE: BODY IN SPACE
2989: Architects say: a good building should “stand on its own,” not in spite of gravity, but using it. The foundation becomes stronger under load; arched structures convert compressive force into load-bearing capacity.
2990: The human body is an ideal architecture. The skeleton is not a rigid frame, but a dynamic system of levers, arches, and shock absorbers. The spine is not a straight column, but an S-shaped curve: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis—each curve increases stability and shock-absorbing capacity 10 times compared to a straight axis.
2991: Violation of this architecture through incorrect posture, muscle imbalance, and a sedentary lifestyle is not only “bad posture.” This is a change in the pattern of loads on the entire musculoskeletal system, with cascading consequences.
2993: Spine—33 vertebrae (7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, 4 coccygeal), connected by intervertebral discs, ligaments, and muscles.
2994: The intervertebral disc is a hydraulic shock absorber. Consists of:
2995: —Nucleus pulposus (nucleus pulposus): high water content (80% at a young age), creates hydrostatic pressure
2996: —Annulus fibrosus (annulus fibrosus): multilayered collagen fibers holding the nucleus
2997: During compression, the disc flattens out, “spreading” to the sides. The annulus fibrosus inhibits this expansion. With age: disc loses water, height decreases → degenerative disc disease.
2998: Disc herniation: rupture of the fibrous ring, protrusion of the nucleus pulposus → root compression → radiculopathy (pain, numbness, weakness along the nerve).
3005: This explains why sitting for long periods of time and improper lifting are major risk factors for low back pain.
3007: Posture is not just “how” a person looks. This is a neuromuscular program: constant work of muscle spindles, vestibular apparatus, proprioceptors of joints and skin, integrated by the cerebellum and brain stem.
3008: Normal standing posture: ear canal—acromion—greater trochanter of the femur—anterior edge of the knee joint—anterior surface of the ankle joint—all on the same vertical line.
3010: —Forward head posture: for every centimeter the head moves forward, the load on the cervical spine increases by ~10 kg. With a 5-centimeter displacement, the neck “carries” an additional 50 kg. It is a chronic trigger for neck pain and headaches.
3011: —Kyphosis (stooping): compression of the chest → decreased vital capacity of the lungs
3012: —Anteversion of the pelvis: shortening of the hip flexors, weakness of the gluteal muscles—chronic overload of the lumbar region
3013: Posture and Psychology: The work of Amy Cuddy (2010) initially showed that “power poses” affected hormonal levels (although the replication was partial). But the connection between posture and sense of self is real and bidirectional.
3015: Donald Ingleber of Harvard applied the principle of tensegrity—tensional integrity, described by engineer Richard Buckminster Fuller, to cells and biological tissues.
3016: Tensegrity structures: rigid elements of compression (bones) + continuous network of tension (muscles, fascia, ligaments). Force is applied to the structure → evenly distributed throughout the network → no local stress points.
3017: This explains why an injury in one part of the body can cause pain in a completely different location: the fascial network is continuous.
3018: Body “tension chart”: long arch lift → plantar tendon → calf muscle → hamstring → sacroiliac ligament → spine → occiput. Tension at any point in this circuit is felt along its entire length.
3020: Chronic back pain is the second most common reason for visits to the doctor. And one of the main examples of the failure of a purely biomechanical model.
3021: MRI studies have shown that degenerative changes in the spine, protrusion and herniated discs are present in 60-80% of people without pain—as findings that do not correlate with symptoms. The correlation between anatomical “pathology” and pain is weaker than between pain and psychological factors (depression, catastrophizing, fear of movement, job dissatisfaction).
3022: The biopsychosocial model of chronic pain includes:
3024: —Psychological: catastrophizing, kinesiophobia (fear of movement due to pain), beliefs about pain
3025: —Social: work environment, family patterns, compensation systems
3026: Treatment of chronic back pain: according to modern recommendations—active movement, patient education (removing fear of movement), psychological assistance for catastrophizing, physical therapy. Bed rest and activity restriction are outdated recommendations.

3034: CHAPTER 62. CYBERNETICS AND REGULATION: FEEDBACK IN LIVING SYSTEMS
3037: In 1948, mathematician Norbert Wiener published the book Cybernetics, or Control and Communication in the Animal and the Machine. This title is not a metaphor: Wiener argued that living organisms and machines are governed by the same principles.
3038: The key principle is feedback: the output signal of the system returns to the input and influences the further behavior of the system.
3039: Negative feedback: The output suppresses the input. This is a mechanism of stabilization—homeostasis.
3040: Positive feedback: The output reinforces the input. This is a mechanism of growth—crisis or growth.
3041: The living body is a network of thousands of feedback loops that maintain a stable internal state in a constantly changing environment.
3043: Claude Bernard introduced the concept of the "internal environment" (milieu intérieur) in 1865. Walter Cannon in 1932 called its maintenance “homeostasis.”
3044: Each physiological variable is maintained by a negative feedback loop:
3046: —Sensor: thermoreceptors of the skin and hypothalamus
3048: — Performer: if it’s cold → vasoconstriction + shivering; if it's hot → vasodilation + sweating
3052: —Executor: hyperglycemia → insulin → glucose utilization; hypoglycemia → glucagon + adrenaline → glycogenolysis
3054: —Sensor: baroreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid sinus
3055: — Performer: increase in blood pressure → depression of sympathetic tone + increase in vagal tone → decrease in heart rate and peripheral resistance
3057: The classical concept of homeostasis describes the maintenance of constancy by correcting deviations. But biologist Peter Sterling and neuroscientist Joseph Ayer proposed a more complex concept: allostasis.
3058: Allostasis: stability through change. The body doesn't just return to the set point; it proactively changes the "set points" depending on the context and predicted needs.
3059: Example: before waking up in the morning (even before the alarm clock), cortisol already begins to rise, blood pressure rises, heart rate accelerates—the body is preparing for an active day. This is not a reaction to stress—it is preventative regulation.
3060: Allostatic load: The price the body pays for prolonged or ineffective regulation. Chronic stress → chronically elevated cortisol → hippocampal damage, insulin resistance, immunosuppression. This is not “burnout” as a metaphor—it is a measurable biological payback for chronic allostatic stress.
3062: Positive feedback is an amplifier. In healthy physiology, it is used to a limited extent and only temporarily (for example, the birth process: contractions → release of oxytocin → stronger contractions).
3063: In illness, positive feedback loops become vicious circles:
3065: Endothelial damage → inflammation → macrophage recruitment → foam cells → inflammatory cytokines → more endothelial damage → more inflammation...
3067: Depression → social withdrawal → loneliness → worsening depression → more isolation...
3068: Chronic pain and fear of movement:
3069: Pain → kinesiophobia → decreased activity → weakened muscles → decreased spinal support → more pain...
3070: Intervening in the positive feedback loop is the goal of treating chronic diseases. Not just to reduce the symptom, but to break the vicious circle.
3072: Cybernetics is the science of control through information. In this sense, disease is a disruption of information flows in the body.
3074: —Oncological disease: the cell stops “hearing” signals about the cessation of division (loss of contact inhibition), about the need for apoptosis. Information chains are broken by mutations.
3075: —Type 2 diabetes: cells stop responding to the insulin signal. The information is there (insulin is released), but the receptor “does not respond.”
3076: —Autoimmune disease: the immune system receives “false information”—mistaking its own for someone else’s.
3077: Treatment as restoration of information flows: immunotherapy for oncology—restoration of immune “vision” to tumor cells (removal of immune suppression). Insulin for type 1 diabetes—replacing a lost signal. Targeted therapy—blocking a specific mutated signaling protein.
3079: Cannon, W. B. (1932). The wisdom of the body. W. W. Norton.
3080: McEwen, B. S., & Lasley, E. N. (2002). The end of stress as we know it. Joseph Henry Press.

3085: CHAPTER 63. COMPLEXITY AND EMERGENCE: WHEN THE WHOLE IS MORE THAN PARTS
3088: In 1972, Philip Anderson, Nobel Prize winner in physics, published a short but revolutionary paper, “More Is Different.”
3089: His thesis: at each level of the organization of matter, new properties arise that cannot be predicted from the properties of the components of the lower level. According to all the rules of physics and chemistry, not a single property of a living cell follows from the atoms of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen. Neurons that obey electrochemical laws do not produce consciousness.
3090: This is emergence: the appearance of qualitatively new properties at a higher level of organization.
3091: Medicine that looks only at components (genes, proteins, cells) misses the emergent properties of systems—that which emerges only as a whole.
3093: Consciousness: A neuron does not “think” or “feel.” But 86 billion neurons in a certain organization think and feel. No study of a single neuron will predict qualia—the subjective experience of “the redness of red” or “the pain of pain.” This is an emergent property of a neural network.
3094: Immune memory: no B lymphocyte “remembers” the previous infection. Immune memory is a property of a population of cells and their interactions, and not of an individual cell.
3095: Life itself: individual molecules are not alive. The cell lives. Life is an emergent property of molecular organization.
3096: Psychosomatic connections: neither “stress” is located in the hypothalamus, nor “immune response” is in the thymus. The relationship between stress and immunity is an emergent property of the PNIE network.
3098: Biologist Stuart Kaufmann and physicist Per Buck developed the theory of complex adaptive systems (CAS):
3102: —Adaptive: the system learns and changes in response to the environment
3103: —Self-organization: order arises without a central manager
3104: —Emergence: the whole is qualitatively different from the sum of the parts
3105: Examples of CAS: immune system, brain, microbiome, ecosystems, markets, social systems.
3106: The immune system is like a CAS: billions of cells interact through cytokines and receptors, self-organizing into a specific response to each new antigen—without a central “commander” to read the instructions for combating that particular microbe.
3107: THE THEORY OF CHAOS IN MEDICINE
3108: Chaotic systems are deterministic (subject to laws), but unpredictable over a long horizon due to sensitivity to initial conditions.
3109: Edward Lorenz's "butterfly effect": small initial change → exponentially increasing divergence of trajectories.
3110: In physiology, chaotic dynamics are a sign of health:
3111: —The rhythm of a healthy heart is deterministically chaotic: not monotonous, not random, but unpredictable over a long horizon
3114: In case of illness—loss of chaos:
3115: —Heart failure: the rhythm becomes more monotonous and regular—“loss of complexity”
3116: —Epilepsy: pathologically synchronous and regular neuronal activity—“too much order”
3117: The health paradox according to Goldberger: “health is neither order nor chaos, but something in between—deterministic complexity.”
3119: Reductionism is methodologically powerful: decompose a system into parts and study each separately. This gave radiology, molecular biology, genomics, antibiotics.
3123: —It does not work well with multiple mutually influencing factors (chronic diseases)
3124: Systems thinking does not reject reductionism—it embraces it. Understanding the part is necessary. But understanding the system is not enough.
3125: Integrative medicine is systems medicine: it uses data from the “parts” (genomics, proteomics, biochemistry) to understand the whole (the person in his context) and make decisions at the system level.
