3134: CHAPTER 64. NETWORK BIOLOGY: FROM GENE TO DISEASE
3139: In 2003, the Human Genome Project ended. It seemed that knowledge of all genes would provide the key to understanding all diseases. This did not happen.
3140: A genome is a list of parts. But a car is not a list of parts; it's how the parts are connected and interact.
3141: Network biology studies interactions: how genes regulate each other, how proteins interact, how signaling pathways connect in networks. A disease is not a “broken gene,” it is a “broken network node.”
3143: “Interactome” is a complete set of protein-protein interactions in the body. Barabasi's group built a map of the interactome based on experimental data and bioinformatics.
3144: Interactome structure: scale-free network—most proteins have few interactions, but a small number of “hubs” interact with thousands of other proteins.
3145: Hubs are critical nodes. In network theory: random removal of nodes does little harm to a scale-free network. But the targeted removal of hubs destroys it. This principle of “reliability through redundancy with vulnerable hubs” is found on the Internet, in social networks—and in biological systems.
3146: Medical consequence: genes encoding interactome hubs, when mutated, cause more severe and pleiotropic (multisystem) diseases.
3148: Interactome analysis revealed the structure of “disease modules”: genes associated with one disease are located in a network in tightly connected clusters—modules.
3149: Comorbidity (the simultaneous presence of several diseases) is predicted by the overlap of modules: the stronger the overlap of modules of two diseases, the more often they occur together.
3150: Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease: overlapping modules (insulin resistance → inflammation → endothelial dysfunction) → high comorbidity.
3151: Alzheimer's disease and type 2 diabetes: overlap (insulin signal in the brain, inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction) → Alzheimer's disease is sometimes called "type 3 diabetes."
3152: This is not just theoretically interesting: it opens up the possibility of drug “repositioning”—using drugs developed for one disease in another disease with an overlapping network module.
3153: REGULATORY NETWORKS: A GENE IS NOT DESTINY
3154: The genome does not work as a static instruction. It is a dynamic regulatory network where genes turn each other on and off.
3155: Transcription factors are proteins that bind to DNA and activate or suppress gene transcription. A single transcription factor can regulate hundreds of genes. A single gene can be regulated by dozens of transcription factors.
3156: MicroRNA—short RNA (~22 nucleotides)—posttranscriptional regulators: bind to mRNA and suppress its translation. One microRNA can regulate hundreds of genes. Impaired microRNA expression is associated with cancer, heart disease, and neurodegeneration.
3157: Epigenetics (Chapter 6, 18)—additional level of regulation: DNA methylation, histone modifications. Environment, lifestyle, stress—all this changes the epigenetic “code” and, consequently, the activity of the genetic network.
3158: This means: “having a disease gene” is not a death sentence. This is a probability modulated by epigenetic regulation and lifestyle.
3160: Modern systems biology builds a multi-level model of the disease:
3162: Level 2—Epigenome: methylation, histone modifications (environmental influences on DNA)
3164: Level 4—Proteome: proteins—structural, enzymes, receptors, signaling molecules
3165: Level 5—Metabolome: metabolites—intermediate and end products of biochemical reactions
3166: Level 6—Microbiome: 38 trillion microbial cells and their metabolites
3167: Level 7—Phenotype: clinical manifestation of all levels in environmental context
3168: The disease “lives” at all levels simultaneously. Treatments that target only one level (eg, pharmacologically at the protein level) often have limited effect or relapse because they do not affect higher levels.
3170: Barabási, A.-L., Gulbahce, N., & Loscalzo, J. (2011). Network medicine: A network-based approach to human disease. Nature Reviews Genetics, 12(1), 56–68. https://doi.org/10.1038/nrg2918
3171: Goh, K.-I., Cusick, M. E., Valle, D., Childs, B., Vidal, M., & Barabási, A.-L. (2007). The human disease network. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 104(21), 8685–8690. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0701361104

3176: CHAPTER 65. INFORMATION FLOW: HOW THE BODY SPEAKS TO ITSELF
3179: “Life is information”—this phrase, uttered by one of the biochemists, at first sounds like a metaphor. But it's literally accurate.
3180: DNA is a molecular carrier of information. Genetic code is a way of encoding information in a sequence of nucleotides. Transcription—reading information. Translation is the interpretation of this information into a protein structure.
3181: The cell constantly “talks” to itself and to neighboring cells. Organ—with other organs. The brain—with the intestines, the immune system, the endocrine system. This constant communication through chemical, electrical and possibly biophotonic signals is life.
3184: Direct contact (juxtacrine signaling): molecules on the surface of one cell interact with the receptors of the neighboring one. Notch signaling is essential for tissue development. Immunological synapse between a T cell and an antigen presenting cell.
3185: Paracrine signaling: a cell releases a molecule that acts on neighboring cells. Neurotransmitters in the synaptic cleft are an example of a paracrine signal. Cytokines during inflammation—act within several cell diameters.
3186: Endocrine signaling: hormones are released into the bloodstream and act on distant organs. Insulin, cortisol, thyroid hormones—endocrine signals.
3187: Neural signaling: electrical potentials along axons + chemical signals at synapses. The fastest and most accurate system.
3188: Exosome signaling: cells release exosomes—nanobubbles containing proteins, RNA (including microRNA), and DNA. This is molecular “mail”—the delivery of biological messages over considerable distances.
3190: One molecular signal (a hormone that binds to a receptor on the cell surface) initiates an intracellular signaling cascade in which each step amplifies the signal:
3191: Receptor activated (1 molecule) → G protein activated (10 molecules) → adenylate cyclase activated → synthesis of cAMP (hundreds of molecules) → activation of protein kinase A → phosphorylation of many proteins (thousands) → changes in metabolism and gene expression.
3192: This is a “reinforcement system”: one hormonal signal → a thousandfold increase inside the cell.
3193: This principle explains why microscopic doses of hormones (picomol concentrations) produce large-scale physiological effects.
3195: —Oncogenic mutations often “stuck” the signaling cascade in a constantly active state (constitutive activation): the cell receives an incessant order to divide
3196: —Insulin resistance: disruption of insulin signal transmission within the cell at the level of the IRS-1/PI3K/Akt pathway
3197: GUT-BRAIN AXIS AS AN INFORMATION CHANNEL
3198: The gut-brain axis is one of the most striking examples of long-distance biological communication (described in Chapter 13). Information transmission channels:
3199: Vagus nerve: 90% of fibers are afferent (from the intestines to the brain). Intestinal bacteria influence vagal activity through SCFAs, neuroactive substances, mechanical and chemical receptors of enterocytes.
3200: Intestinal hormones: serotonin (90% in the intestines), GLP-1, GLP-2, PYY, ghrelin—signals about the state of the intestines to the brain through the blood.
3201: Immune cells: The gut immune system (GALT) constantly “reports” the state of the gut microbiome to the central nervous system through cytokines.
3202: Microbiome metabolites: SCFAs (butyrate, propionate, acetate), intestinal tryptophan metabolites (kynurenine, indole), secondary bile acids—all cross the blood-brain barrier and influence brain neurochemistry.
3204: If disease is a disruption of information flows, then information overload is a pathology of another level: overload of sensory and cognitive information processing systems.
3205: The brain processes about 11 million pieces of information per second. Of these, about 40-50 are available in consciousness. The rest are processed unconsciously or filtered.
3207: —The average smartphone user unlocks their phone 150 times a day
3208: —Each notification is a microstressor that activates the orienting reflex system → release of norepinephrine
3209: —The “always on” mode prevents the state of “mental rest” necessary for memory consolidation, creative thinking, introspection
3210: Default Mode Network: active when a person is “not busy”—thought wandering, introspection, creative thinking, consolidation of experience. Constant digital stimulation suppresses the DMN.
3211: “Digital hygiene”—conscious management of information flows—becomes part of integrative health.

3219: CHAPTER 66. EVOLUTIONARY MEDICINE: WHY DO WE GET SICK
3224: A QUESTION THAT IS RARELY ASKED
3225: Medicine traditionally answers the question “What happened?” (disease mechanism) and “How to treat?”. Evolutionary medicine asks a fundamentally different question: “Why do people have this disease in the first place?”
3226: Randolph Nesse of the University of Michigan and George Williams of the State University of New York—the founders of evolutionary medicine—formulated its central principle: the symptoms of disease are often adaptive responses, not just “glitches.”
3227: Temperature during infection: accelerates the immune response, inhibits bacterial growth. Forcibly reducing the temperature to 38°C is perhaps counterproductive.
3228: Cough during ARVI: a mechanism for clearing the respiratory tract. Suppressing it during a productive cough can worsen the course of the disease.
3229: Nausea of ​​pregnancy: Possibly protects the embryo from dietary toxins during the critical period of organogenesis. Women with severe toxicosis have a lower risk of miscarriage.
3230: EVOLUTIONARY GAP: INCOMPATIBILITY OF GENES AND ENVIRONMENT
3231: The main thesis of evolutionary medicine: our genome is optimized for the Pleistocene environment (~10,000 years ago). The environment has changed radically; genome - no.
3232: The “diseases of civilization”—chronic diseases that were virtually absent in prehistoric populations—are a consequence of this gap:
3233: Obesity: In a calorie-deficient environment, the thrifty genotype (Neale's hypothesis)—the tendency to efficiently store fat—was advantageous. In an environment of food abundance, it becomes pathogenic.
3234: Type 2 diabetes: insulin resistance in muscles (Chapter 49) could be adaptive—redirecting glucose to the brain under stress. In a chronically stressful environment with excess carbohydrates, this becomes a disease.
3235: Myopia: The epidemic of myopia in the 21st century is a direct consequence of children spending time indoors with books and screens instead of outdoor activities. Lack of natural light disrupts the normal development of the eyeball.
3236: Anxiety disorders: A nervous system optimized for survival in an unpredictable wild environment is chronically activated in a relatively safe but information-overloaded environment.
3238: Studies of traditional hunter-gatherers (Hadza in Tanzania, Kung in Botswana) show a striking health profile in sharp contrast to industrialized populations:
3239: —Virtually no atherosclerosis and hypertension (ECG data, autopsies)
3242: —Lack of caries (before contact with Western diet)
3243: —High physical activity (~15,000 steps per day, according to actigraphy by Pontzer et al.)
3244: This is not a “romanticization” of the past—infectious diseases, injuries, and maternal mortality were common. But chronic non-communicable diseases are rare.
3245: Conclusion: Many chronic diseases are not an “inevitable” consequence of age and biology. This is a consequence of an environment for which our body was not “designed”.
3247: Evolutionary medicine expands the concept of “self.” Our microbiome is a product of co-evolution with its host.
3248: “The Vanishing Microbiome”: Martin Blaser (New York University) showed that bacteria that humans have carried for millennia (including H. pylori) are disappearing in Western populations as a result of antibiotics and dietary changes. This may be due to the increase in allergic diseases, inflammatory bowel diseases, and obesity.
3249: Parasites and immunity: “Old friends”—parasitic worms (helminths)—co-evolved with the human immune system. Elimination of helminths from Western environments has disrupted immune regulation. “Helminth therapy”—intentional infection with harmless parasites—is being studied as a treatment for inflammatory bowel disease and multiple sclerosis.
3251: The theory of sexual selection (Darwin, 1871) is another tool of evolutionary medicine.
3252: Immune competence as a signal of health: facial symmetry (a sign of a “good” genome), smell (carrying information about MHC compatibility) are factors in partner choice associated with immune function.
3253: Sexual dimorphism of diseases: men are more vulnerable to most infections, cancer, and cardiovascular events in middle age. Possible explanation: Investment in the immune system competes with investment in competitive (risky) behavior optimized for reproductive success.
3255: Blaser, M. J. (2014). Missing microbes: How the overuse of antibiotics is fueling our modern plagues. Henry Holt.

3259: Stearns, S. C., & Koella, J. C. (Eds.). (2008). Evolution in health and disease (2nd ed.). Oxford University Press.
3264: Rachel Yehuda studied the children of Holocaust survivors. She found that they had abnormally low cortisol levels and altered HPA axis responses—the same biomarkers found in the survivors themselves. Children who never experienced trauma carried biological traces of it.
3265: This was one of the first convincing examples of transgenerational epigenetic transmission in humans—the transmission of biological “memories” of stress across generations.
3266: Since then, a number of studies have shown that the experiences of one generation—especially during early development—can influence the biology of subsequent generations through epigenetic mechanisms. The mechanism of transgenerational transmission through germ cells in humans (as opposed to nematodes and rodents) remains the subject of scientific debate; data from Yehuda et al. (2016) are discussed in the literature, although not refuted.
3268: Classical genetics: only the DNA sequence is passed on to offspring. Epigenetic marks—methylation, histone modifications—are “erased” during the formation of germ cells and fertilization through the process of reprogramming.
3270: Imprinting: a number of genes carry the parental “stamp”—methylation is maintained depending on whether the allele came from the mother or the father. This is a proven mechanism of epigenetic inheritance.
3271: Transgenerational germ cell transmission: Data from Michael Skinner (Washington State University) showed that exposure of pregnant rats to an endocrine disruptor (viclozoline) caused reproductive impairment in male offspring several generations later through changes in sperm methylation.
3272: Nematode C. elegans: proven transmission of epigenetic marks over several generations in response to stress, hunger, temperature shock—mechanism: heritable mRNAs and small RNAs (piRNAs).
3273: HUNGRY WINTER AS AN EPIGENETIC LESSON
3274: The Dutch “hunger winter” of 1944-1945 is one of the most studied epigenetic events in human history.
3275: The German blockade deprived the western Netherlands of food. Thousands of people died of hunger. Surviving pregnant women gave birth to children, who could then be identified and studied.
3277: —Children whose mothers fasted in the first trimester: increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, heart disease—after 60 years
3278: —Mechanism: changes in methylation of the IGF2 gene (insulin-like growth factor 2) and a number of other genes—measurable even 60 years after birth
3279: —Grandchildren of the “hungry” also had health changes—possible two-generation transmission
3280: "Thrifty Phenotype" (Barker): A fetus that has experienced nutritional deficiency programs its body to have a "frugal" metabolism. If nutrition becomes excessive after birth, this “lean” programming leads to obesity and metabolic syndrome.
3282: Animal studies have shown convincingly that maternal stress during pregnancy alters the epigenetic programming of the offspring.
3283: Michael Meaney: High LG mothers programmed their pups to have low anxiety and normal stress responses through demethylation of the glucocorticoid receptor promoter in the hippocampus. This programming was reversible—the cubs were “reprogrammed” by the new adoptive mother.
3284: In humans: meta-analysis by Yehuda et al. (2016) showed changes in FKBP5 (a regulator of the glucocorticoid receptor) in children of Holocaust survivors—comparable to changes in the survivors themselves, but with the opposite direction of methylation.
3285: This raises a profound question: are we passing on experiences as well as genes to our children?
3287: The downside of transgenerational epigenetics is encouraging: epigenetic marks are reversible.
3288: If a generation has inherited “disturbing” epigenetic programming, this is not a death sentence. This is a predisposition that can be modified through:
3290: —Psychotherapy: Data show DNA methylation changes following successful psychotherapy for PTSD
3291: —Meditation: Davidson and Ornish studies—changes in gene expression after meditation practices
3292: —Epigenetic drugs: inhibitors of methyltransferases and histone deacetylases are already used in oncology
3293: Epigenetics is both a limitation and an opportunity. The past influences us. But it does not define us unconditionally.

3301: CHAPTER 68. CHEMISTRY OF LIFE: THE MOLECULES THAT CREATE US
3304: 65% of the human body weight is oxygen. 18%—carbon. 10% is hydrogen. 3%—nitrogen. And a few percent—calcium, phosphorus, potassium, sulfur, sodium, chlorine, magnesium and traces of two dozen more elements.
3305: From these elements—the same ones that make up rocks, water, and air—life has built incredibly complex molecules. Squirrels. Nucleic acids. Lipids. Carbohydrates. Hormones. Neurotransmitters.
3306: Biochemistry—the chemistry of life—describes the molecular language that an organism “speaks.” Understanding this language is the basis for understanding the disease and treatment.
3308: Proteins are the most functionally diverse biomolecules. There are more than 100,000 different proteins in the human body.
3309: Building Blocks: 20 Standard Amino Acids. Their different sequences (primary structure) determine spatial structure (secondary → tertiary → quaternary) and function.
3311: —Structural: collagen (tendons, skin, bones), actin and myosin (muscles), keratin (hair, nails)
3312: —Catalytic: enzymes are biological catalysts that accelerate reactions billions of times
3316: —Receptor: cell surface receptors—interface between cell and environment
3317: Protein folding: The polypeptide chain folds into a specific three-dimensional structure determined by its amino acid sequence and interactions with chaperones. Incorrect folding → protein aggregates → prion diseases, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's diseases.
3318: DNA AND RNA: STORAGE AND TRANSMISSION OF INFORMATION
3319: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a repository of genetic information. A double helix of two antiparallel nucleotide chains connected by hydrogen bonds between complementary bases (A-T, G-C).
3320: Human genome size: 3.2 billion base pairs per cell. If you pull DNA from one cell, you get a thread 2 meters long. There are about 100 billion kilometers of DNA in all the cells of the body.
3321: DNA is stored in chromatin, a complex with histone proteins. Packaging levels: nucleosome → chromatin fibril → loops → domains → chromosome. This allows 2 meters of DNA to be packed into a 6 µm diameter core.
3322: CDC: the central dogma of molecular biology—information flow: DNA → RNA → Protein.
3324: —mRNA (template): a copy of a gene transported to ribosomes for translation into protein
3325: —tRNA (transport): “translator”—carries the amino acid to the ribosome
3327: —microRNA, siRNA, lncRNA: regulatory RNAs—regulate gene expression without translation into protein
3328: The discovery of RNA interference (siRNA, microRNA) by Fire and Mello (Nobel Prize 2006) opened up new insights into gene regulation—and new therapeutic options (siRNA therapeutics).
3332: —Energy: triglycerides → long-term energy storage (9 kcal/g vs 4 kcal/g carbohydrates)
3333: —Hormonal: steroid hormones (cortisol, testosterone, estrogens, aldosterone, vitamin D)—cholesterol derivatives
3336: Cholesterol is a critical molecule, despite its "bad reputation":
3337: —Structural part of all cell membranes: determines their fluidity
3338: —Precursor to steroid hormones and vitamin D
3341: The problem is not cholesterol itself, but its excess and oxidized forms in the wrong context.
3344: Glucose is the universal fuel of cells. The brain is especially dependent: it consumes about 20% of the body's glucose at 2% of body weight.
3345: Glycogen is the storage form of glucose in the liver and muscles. Limited supply (~400 g in muscles, ~100 g in liver).
3347: —Soluble (pectin, β-glucan): fermented by intestinal bacteria → SCFA → fuel for colonocytes + anti-inflammatory effects
3348: —Insoluble (cellulose): increases the volume of intestinal contents, accelerates transit
3349: Protein glycosylation: attachment of carbohydrate chains to proteins (glycoproteins) → important for immune recognition, receptor function, mucus structure. Excessive non-enzymatic glycosylation during hyperglycemia → tissue damage (diabetic nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy).

3355: Voet, D., & Voet, J. G. (2011). Biochemistry (4th ed.). Wiley.
3363: In 1909, Paul Ehrlich—the founder of chemotherapy—introduced the concept of the “magic bullet” (Zauberkugel): a molecule that precisely hits the pathogen without causing harm to the host. This was the dream of chemical medicine.
3364: Arsphenamine (Salvarsan, compound 606—the 606th molecule studied in the search) in 1909 became the first synthetic antibiotic against syphilis. The first "magic bullet".
3365: Since then, pharmacology has created thousands of “bullets” of varying degrees of accuracy. Understanding their mechanism is the basis for intelligent use.
3367: Most drugs act through specific interactions with molecular targets: receptors, enzymes, ion channels, transport proteins.
3369: —Agonist: binds to the receptor and activates it (mimics the action of the endogenous ligand). Morphine is an opioid receptor agonist. Salbutamol is an agonist of bronchial β₂-adrenergic receptors.
3370: —Antagonist: binds to the receptor and blocks it (competes with the endogenous ligand). Beta blockers are β-adrenergic receptor antagonists. Antihistamines—H₁ receptor antagonists.
3371: —Partial agonist: partial activation of the receptor—acts as a weak agonist in the absence of endogenous ligand and as a weak antagonist when its concentration is high.
3373: —ACEIs (angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors): block the enzyme that converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II → decrease in blood pressure
3374: —Statins: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors → decrease in cholesterol synthesis
3375: —NSAIDs (nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs): COX-1/COX-2 inhibitors → reduction of prostaglandins → pain relief, anti-inflammatory effect
3376: Dose-response concept: dependence of effect on drug concentration. EC₅₀—the concentration that causes 50% of the maximum effect. Therapeutic index (TI) = TD₅₀ / ED₅₀—the ratio of toxic and effective doses. The higher the TI, the safer the drug.
3377: PHARMACOKINETICS: HOW THE BODY PROCESSES DRUGS
3378: ADME concept: four processes that determine the fate of a drug in the body:
3379: Absorption: entry of a drug into the systemic circulation. Depends on the route of administration (oral, intravenous, transdermal), the physicochemical properties of the molecule, and first-pass metabolism.
3380: Distribution: How the drug is distributed throughout the tissues. Volume of distribution (Vd). Plasma protein binding: only the free (unbound) fraction is pharmacologically active.
3381: Metabolism: biotransformation, mainly in the liver via CYP450. May inactivate (prodrugs are activated) or activate (codeine → morphine via CYP2D6). Genetic variants of CYP450 → individual differences in metabolism.
3382: Excretion: excretion through the kidneys (renal excretion), bile (enterohepatic circulation), lungs (volatile substances, alcohol). T₁/₂ (half-life): time taken to reduce concentration by half.
3384: Different people react to the same medicine differently. A classic example: codeine (a pain reliever) is converted to morphine via CYP2D6.
3386: —“Poor metabolizers”: ~7% of Europeans. Codeine is practically not converted into morphine → no analgesic effect
3387: — “Ultrarapid metabolizers”: ~1-2%. Codeine quickly converts to high concentrations of morphine → potentially dangerous overdose
3388: In pediatrics, this is of critical importance: deaths of children from codeine after tonsillectomy were associated specifically with the ultra-rapid metabolism of CYP2D6.
3389: Pharmacogenomics allows you to select the right drug and dose based on genotype. The FDA is updating drug indications based on pharmacogenomic data.
3391: Drug interactions are one of the main problems of polypragmatics (use of many drugs).
3393: —Pharmacokinetic: one drug alters the ADME of another through effects on CYP450 or transporters (P-glycoprotein)
3394: - CYP3A4 inducer (rifampicin, St. John's wort) reduces the concentration of substrates (warfarin, contraceptives, immunosuppressants)
3395: - CYP3A4 inhibitor (grapefruit juice, itraconazole, clarithromycin) increases the concentration of substrates
3396: —Pharmacodynamic: interaction at the level of the mechanism of action
3398: - Antagonism: NSAIDs neutralize the antihypertensive effect of ACEIs
3399: Polypharmacy (>5 drugs) in the elderly is a serious clinical problem: the risk of adverse reactions increases exponentially.

Adverse drug reactions as cause of admission to hospital: Prospective analysis of 18 820 patients. BMJ, 329(7456), 15–19. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.329.7456.15
3405: Rang, H. P., Ritter, J. M., Flower, R. J., & Henderson, G. (2015). Rang and Dale's pharmacology (8th ed.). Churchill Livingstone.
3410: Oxygen is the molecule of life. Without it, most cells die within minutes. And it is also a molecule of death: the products of its incomplete reduction are among the most aggressive chemical agents in the cell.
3411: This paradox is called the "oxygen paradox": aerobic organisms have evolved to use oxygen to produce energy while also protecting themselves from its toxic byproducts.
3412: FREE RADICALS: THE CHEMISTRY OF HARM
3413: A free radical is a molecule or atom with an unpaired electron in its outer orbital. An unpaired electron makes the molecule extremely chemically reactive: it “wants” to take away an electron from a neighboring molecule.
3414: The main biologically significant free radicals:
3415: —Superoxide anion (O₂•⁻): formed when electrons leak from the mitochondrial respiratory chain
3416: —Hydroxyl radical (•OH): the most aggressive; formed from H₂O₂ in the Fenton reaction with the participation of iron ions
3417: —Peroxynitrite (ONOO⁻): formed by the reaction of O₂•⁻ with nitric oxide (NO)
3419: —Membrane lipids → lipid peroxidation → disruption of membrane function
3421: —Proteins → oxidation of amino acids → disruption of structure and function
3423: The concept of “oxidative stress” as a purely pathological phenomenon is outdated. Current understanding: ROS at low concentrations are important signaling molecules.
3424: “Mitohormesis” (Ristow): exercise generates ROS in muscles → activation of protective genes (Nrf2 pathway) → synthesis of antioxidant enzymes, heat shock proteins, mitophagy → adaptation and strengthening. Taking high doses of antioxidants during exercise may block these adaptations.
3425: H₂O₂ as a signaling molecule: acts through redox regulation of cysteine ​​thiol groups in proteins. Regulates the cell cycle, apoptosis, immune response.
3426: This explains why high doses of antioxidant supplements (vitamin E, beta-carotene) in clinical studies either have no effect or are harmful—they block not only pathological but also beneficial ROS signals.
3428: The body has an evolutionarily perfect antioxidant defense:
3430: —Superoxide dismutase (SOD): converts O₂•⁻ to H₂O₂ and O₂. Requires Mn (mitochondrial SOD2) or Cu/Zn (cytoplasmic SOD1).
3431: — Catalase: destroys H₂O₂ → H₂O + O₂. Mainly in liver peroxisomes and erythrocytes.
3432: —Glutathione peroxidase (GPx): reduces H₂O₂ and lipid peroxides using glutathione (GSH) → GSSG. Requires selenium.
3433: — Thioredoxin reductase: maintains thioredoxin in a reduced state; requires selenium.
3435: —Glutathione (GSH): tripeptide (glycine-cysteine-glutamate). The main intracellular “buffer” of oxidative stress. Concentration decreases with chronic stress, aging, and cysteine ​​deficiency.
3436: —Vitamin C (ascorbic acid): water-soluble antioxidant; regenerates vitamin E.
3437: —Vitamin E (tocopherol): fat-soluble antioxidant; protects membrane lipids.
3438: —Uric acid: a powerful antioxidant in plasma. Most likely, this is what explains the high levels of uric acid in humans (unlike most mammals)—compensation for the loss of endogenous synthesis of ascorbic acid.
3439: NRF2: MAIN REGULATOR OF ANTIOXIDANT DEFENSE
3440: Nrf2 (Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2) is a transcription factor called the “master switch” of cell antioxidant defense.
3441: Activation of Nrf2 induces the expression of more than 200 defense genes: SOD, catalase, GPx, glutathione synthase, detoxification enzymes (Phase II), proteasomal proteins.
3447: Chronic excessive oxidative stress depletes the Nrf2 system, leading to accumulation of damage.

3459: When a bacterium penetrates the skin, it initiates one of the most coordinated biochemical processes in nature. Hundreds of molecular players—cytokines, chemokines, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, complement components, coagulation factors—act in concert within seconds and hours.
3460: Inflammation is molecular warfare. Understanding its chemistry means understanding why anti-inflammatory drugs work (and why they sometimes work too well).
3462: The first step: recognizing the “alien” or “dangerous”.
3463: Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRR): receptors that recognize molecular patterns of pathogens (PAMPs) or damage (DAMPs):
3464: —Toll-like receptors (TLR): family of 10 receptors; TLR4 recognizes lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria. Discovery of TLR (Bruce Beutler, Jules Hoffman—Nobel Prize 2011)
3465: —NOD-like receptors: intracellular sensors of bacterial components and crystals (uric acid in gout)
3467: Binding to PRR → activation of signaling cascades → activation of the transcription factor NF-κB → transcription of pro-inflammatory genes (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, COX-2, etc.).
3469: Cytokines are signaling proteins that coordinate the immune response:
3470: —TNF-α (tumor necrosis factor): systemic alarm signal, activates endothelium, neutrophils, causes fever. Targeted drug for rheumatoid arthritis (TNF inhibitors: infliximab, adalimumab).
3471: —IL-1β: fever, acute phase response, neutrophil activation. Target for autoinflammatory diseases (anakinra).
3472: —IL-6: stimulates the synthesis of acute phase proteins (CRP, fibrinogen) in the liver, fever. Target for rheumatoid arthritis and COVID-19 cytokine storm (tocilizumab).
3474: —IFN-γ (interferon-γ): activates macrophages, enhances MHC II—the “combat mode” of immune surveillance.
3475: Chemokines—cytokines that direct the migration of immune cells to the site of inflammation:
3479: Arachidonic acid is a fatty acid released from membrane phospholipids when cells are damaged → substrate for two inflammatory enzyme systems:
3481: —COX-1 (constitutional): constantly present; participates in the protection of the gastric mucosa, platelet aggregation, regulation of renal blood flow
3482: —COX-2 (inducible): increases sharply with inflammation; synthesis of inflammatory prostaglandins (PGE₂, PGI₂) → pain, swelling, fever
3484: —Non-selective (aspirin, ibuprofen, diclofenac): inhibit COX-1 and COX-2 → pain relief + risk of gastropathy
3485: —Selective COX-2 inhibitors (coxibs: celecoxib): less gastrotoxicity but increased cardiovascular risk (role of COX-1 in endothelial protection)
3487: Arachidonic acid → 5-HPETE → leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄, LTE₄): bronchospasm, mucus hypersecretion → key mediators of bronchial asthma.
3490: Inflammation must not only begin, but also end. Chronic inflammation is a violation of the resolution phase.
3491: Charles Serhan (Harvard) discovered a family of “specialized pro-resolution mediators” (SPM):
3492: —Lipoxins: derivatives of arachidonic acid; signal to stop the neutrophil flow, stimulation of macrophage clearance
3493: —Resolvins (from EPA and DHA): powerful anti-inflammatory and pro-resolution; explain part of the anti-inflammatory effect of omega-3s
3495: This changes the understanding of inflammation: chronic inflammation is not excess inflammation, but defective resolution. Treatment is aimed not only at suppressing inflammation, but also at activating proresolution pathways.
3496: Omega-3 fatty acids → substrate for resolvins and protectins → explanation of the anti-inflammatory mechanism of fish oil at the molecular level.

3504: CHAPTER 72. NEUROCEMISTRY OF EMOTIONS AND MOOD
3508: Joy. Fear. Love. Yearning. These states seem infinitely far from chemistry. But they are reproducibly changed by chemical molecules—drugs, drugs, hormones.
3509: This doesn't mean that emotions are "just" chemistry. This means that chemical molecules are one of the languages ​​in which the brain “talks” to itself about the state of the world and the body.
3510: The neurochemistry of emotion is one of the most exciting frontier territories of modern science: where molecular biology meets the phenomenology of subjective experience.
3512: Monoamines are the most studied group of neurotransmitters with a proven connection with mood:
3514: Synthesized from tryptophan (9 steps → 5-hydroxytryptophan → serotonin). 90% of the body's serotonin is in the intestines. In the brain, the raphe nuclei send serotonergic projections to almost all parts of the brain.
3515: For many years it was believed that depression was a “lack of serotonin.” This idea was productive: it produced SSRIs that have helped millions of people. But the meta-analysis by Moncrieff et al. (2022) questioned this: the direct link between serotonin levels and depression is weaker than previously thought. This does not mean that SSRIs are ineffective: the largest meta-analysis by Cipriani et al. (2018, Lancet; 522 studies, 116,477 participants) confirmed the clinical effectiveness of all 21 second-generation antidepressants. The mechanism of their action is broader and more complex than replenishing “serotonin deficiency”. Depression is a multi-etiological disorder in which serotonin is involved, but is not the only player. [Researched]
3517: Synthesis: tyrosine → L-DOPA → dopamine (→ norepinephrine → adrenaline).
3518: Mesolimbic pathway (VTA → nucleus accumbens): “reward system”—anticipation, motivation, learning through reinforcement. Addiction disorders, schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease.
3519: Nigrostriatal pathway: motor control. Dopamine deficiency in substancia nigra → Parkinson's disease.
3520: Norepinephrine: synthesized from dopamine at the locus coeruleus → projections throughout the brain. Role in attention, wakefulness, stress response. Decline → depression, PTSD.
3521: GABA AND GLUTAMATE: BALANCE OF INHIBITION AND EXCITATION
3522: GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid): the main inhibitory neurotransmitter. 30-40% of synapses in the central nervous system are GABAergic.
3523: Deficiency of GABAergic inhibition → anxiety, epilepsy.
3524: GABA-A receptor—ionotropic chloride channel: benzodiazepines, barbiturates, alcohol enhance its effect → sedation, anxiolysis, anticonvulsant effect.
3525: Glutamate: the main excitatory neurotransmitter. NMDA receptors are key for synaptic plasticity (long-term potentiation—the basis of learning and memory).
3526: Ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, causes a rapid (within hours) antidepressant effect, especially in cases of suicidal ideation. This is an explanation for an alternative mechanism of action in depression that is independent of the monoamine system.
3527: Esketamine (Spravato) is the first new mechanism antidepressant approved by the FDA in 2019.
3528: OXYTOCIN AND ENDORPHINS: THE CHEMISTRY OF COMMUNICATION
3531: —Central: social connection, trust, empathy, anxiety reduction
3532: “Cuddle hormone”: oxytocin levels increase with hugging, skin-to-skin contact, breastfeeding, and sexual intimacy.
3533: Paul Zak showed that oxytocin increases during human interaction that is perceived as trusting. Intranasal oxytocin has been shown to increase trust and prosocial behavior.
3534: Endogenous opioids (endorphins, enkephalins, dynorphins): neuropeptides acting on opioid receptors (μ, κ, δ). Roles: pain relief, euphoria (runner's high), social connection.
3535: Endocannabinoids (anandamide—the “bliss molecule”, 2-AG): bind to the same receptors as THC in marijuana. Roles: regulation of pain, appetite, mood, memory. Physical activity increases anandamide levels—one of the mechanisms of “runner's high.”
3537: The limbic system-hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis—the “stress response”—is a neurochemical process:
3538: CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone): secreted by the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus → pituitary gland → ACTH → adrenal glands → cortisol.
3539: Cortisol feeds back into the brain through glucocorticoid receptors:
3540: —Hippocampus: chronically high cortisol → neurotoxicity, atrophy → memory impairment, depression
3541: —Amygdala: cortisol increases anxiety and fear consolidation
3542: —Prefrontal cortex: chronic stress impairs its function → impulsivity, impaired decision making
3544: —Exercise → BDNF → hippocampal neurogenesis → compensation for cortisol neurotoxicity
3545: —Meditation → change in amygdala activity → decrease in reactivity to stress

3554: CHAPTER 73. MEDICINE FOR GENERATIONS—THE JOURNEY CONTINUES
3559: THE END IS LIKE THE BEGINNING
3560: We have gone from cell to consciousness, from genome to generations, from particle physics to existential meaning. And having walked this path, we understand: medicine is not a set of facts. This is a way of seeing a person.
3561: Traditional medicine looks at a symptom and asks, “What’s broken?” Integrative medicine looks at the person holistically and asks, “What is going on in this system? What maintains the imbalance? What will help the system restore balance?”
3562: This is not a rejection of biochemistry and pharmacology. This is their expansion - to psychology, to biography, to relationships, to meaning.
3564: This book is based on several key principles, each of which is backed by scientific evidence:
3565: Unity of body and mind: psychological states have physiological correlates. Chronic stress is chronic inflammation. Forgiveness lowers cortisol. Meditation changes the structure of the brain. The meaning of life predicts life expectancy. This is not “new age”—this is neurobiology and immunology.
3566: The systemic nature of health: the body is not a set of organs, but a network of interacting systems. The disease is a network disruption, not a “part breakdown.” Treatment is to restore systemic balance, not just eliminate the symptom.
3567: Intergenerational dimension: we carry within ourselves the biological heritage of our ancestors. A great-grandmother's hunger, a grandfather's injury, a mother's stress—all this can affect our epigenome. But this legacy is not fatal—it can be modified.
3568: Lifestyle as therapy: nutrition, movement, sleep, stress management, social connection, meaning—these factors have measurable physiological mechanisms and clinical effectiveness comparable to drug therapy for many chronic diseases.
3569: Patient as participant: healing occurs not “with the patient,” but “with the patient.” Therapeutic alliance, self-efficacy, shared decision making are not “soft” aspects, but clinically significant variables.
3571: The title of this book, “Medicine of Generations,” has a double meaning.
3572: The first meaning is literal: medicine that understands that human health today has been shaped by generations. That what happened to his grandmother during pregnancy could affect his immunity. That attachment patterns learned in childhood determine the neurobiology of stress in adulthood.
3573: The second meaning is forward-looking: medicine that is responsible to the next generations. The choices we make today—how we eat, how we cope with stress, how we build relationships—leave epigenetic traces that our children will inherit not only in the form of memories, but also in the form of biological patterns.
3574: The third meaning is that a doctor is also a product of generations. For example, my ancestors on my father's side were continuously clergy in Atskuri earlier than the 16th century, and understood and practiced medicine. When the Bolsheviks banned religious rites, my paternal grandfather became a learned agronomist and plant healer. And my grandfather on my mother’s side was a military doctor. My parents are both doctors. Thus, generations raised the epigenetics of a healer in me. My parents also created the most valuable tool in my medical practice - the system of Proper Nutrition.
3576: Arnold Goldberger spoke about the “complexity of health”—deterministic chaos, fractality. Rita Sharon spoke of “narrative medicine”—the need to hear stories. Dean Ornish showed that the heart can be healed by lifestyle. Rachel Yehuda discovered the memory of the Holocaust in the DNA of the next generation.
3577: They all said one thing: a person is more than his illness. The disease is more than its symptoms. Treatment is more than applying a protocol.
3578: Medicine is a science and an art at the same time. Science provides tools. Art uses them wisely, in the context of a particular person, his history, his values, his generation.
3580: An honest book ends not with answers, but with questions:
3581: How exactly is transgenerational epigenetic information transmitted in humans? What is the clinical significance of quantum effects in biology? How will artificial intelligence change the doctor-patient relationship? How to incorporate the principles of integrative medicine into healthcare systems with limited resources? Where is the line between “natural” and “proven”?
3582: There are no answers to these questions yet. This means the medicine is not yet completed. And that's the good news: it continues to grow.
3583: In 1628, William Harvey published a treatise on the circulation of the blood, the first scientifically based mechanistic view of the heart. This marked the birth of “scientific medicine.”
3584: Almost 400 years have passed. We know incomparably more about the human body. And the more we learn, the more obvious one thing becomes: behind every molecule there is a person. Behind every symptom is a story. Behind every disease is an attempt by the system to restore balance.
3585: Medicine that understands this—and proceeds from this understanding—is medicine worthy of a person.
3586: EPILOGUE. DISEASE AS PROTECTION: TOWARDS A NEW UNDERSTANDING OF HEALING
3587: The concept of “illness as defense” outlined in this section is consistent with the biopsychosocial model of Engel (1977) and clinical psychosomatics: Fava & Sonino (2010, Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism) showed that unresolved psychological conflicts are independent predictors of chronicity of a number of somatic diseases. Within the DSM-5, this is designated as Somatic Symptom Disorder (ICD-11: Bodily distress disorder). The author's metaphor “disease as protection” is a clinical observation, and not a mechanistic theory, applicable to all nosologies: it is not applicable to infectious, oncological, genetic and traumatic diseases as the main cause. The reader should not interpret it as “the patient is to blame for his illness.”
3588: THE PATIENT I COULD NOT UNDERSTAND
3589: He was forty-two years old. An engineer, a father of two children, a man who has not missed a single day of work in eight years. He came with chronic fatigue that did not respond to anything. The tests are normal. Twelve specialists. “Stress,” everyone said. He nodded and left.
3590: At one of the consultations, almost by accident, he said: “I always wanted to start my own business. I've been wanting it for twenty years now. But every time I think about it, something like horror comes over me, and I put it off.”
3591: I asked, “What will happen if you try?” He was silent for a long time. Then he said quietly: “I can’t stand it. I know that. I just know."
3592: At that moment I realized something that redefined for me everything this book was written about.
3594: For seventy-three chapters we have been moving towards one question. We talked about stress and epigenetics, about the microbiome and mitochondria, about the consciousness of the doctor and the biography of the patient, about the fact that the body is not an enemy, but an ally. That a symptom is not a breakdown, but a signal.
3595: But there was a question that we had not yet asked directly. Not “what’s broken?” and not “what does the body want to say?”—but: what does the disease protect against? After all, all reactions in the body are protective and disease cannot be an exception due to its power, duration, and diversity.
3596: This is not a metaphor. This is an accurate question. And the answer to it changes everything—from how we understand the diagnosis to how we build treatment.
3599: An organism is a system whose main task is survival. For the sake of this task, the body is ready to sacrifice a lot: sleep, immunity, reproductive function, cognitive abilities. The history of medicine is full of examples of how the body turns off “unnecessary” functions in order to preserve the core.
3601: Not a breakdown. Not a punishment. Not an accident. Defensive reaction.
3602: The body develops a disease when, without it, a person moves towards a situation that the body assesses as existentially unbearable: to the loss of what constitutes the core of its identity, to an action that the psyche considers tantamount to destruction. Not to suffering - to what is deeper than suffering. And this distinction is fundamentally important.
3603: The disease says: stop. Not because the body is weak—but because it is smart enough to stop what will kill faster than the disease itself.
3604: This does not mean that behind every illness there is a psychological conflict. Infections, injuries, genetic disorders have their own mechanisms. But even in these cases, how the body reacts, how intensely, how protractedly, is largely determined by this principle: the body always does the best it can with the resources it has.
3606: Each of us has aspirations. Some are conscious. Many don't. These are not just “wishes.” These are deep-seated imperatives that feel like a calling, a duty, a necessity—and often have no words, because words would be too scary.
3607: Become who I was meant to become. Do what was expected of me. Don't disappoint your father. Realize what I promised myself when I was twenty.
3608: These aspirations do not disappear when we do not acknowledge them. They go deeper into the body. And there they continue to work like an engine: constant, quiet, non-stop.
3609: But there is a paradox: sometimes the fulfillment of this desire requires more strength than a person has. No more will. No more time. More fundamental life resources - biological, psychological, social.
3610: The body knows this. The body figured this out long before words came to consciousness. And the body develops a disease such that you simply cannot reach the dangerous goal.
3611: Disease protects a person from himself.
3612: WHY IS THE PATIENT NOT TO GUILTY?
3613: This needs to be said clearly, because it is easy to make mistakes here.
3614: This concept is not blaming the patient. She doesn’t say, “You created your own illness.” It does not add the burden of moral guilt to physical suffering.
3615: She says something fundamentally different: a person is not aware of the desire that drives him to the edge. Precisely because it is unconscious, he cannot control it, cannot refuse it, cannot simply “rethink” it. It works deeper than words, deeper than will.
3616: This desire itself is often born not of weakness, but of greatness. The desire to be true to yourself. The desire to finally stop living a life that is not your own.
3617: There is nothing pathological in this desire. The gap between the size of the goal and the availability of strength becomes pathological.
3618: It is not the patient's fault that the goal is greater than his current resources. It is not the patient's fault that the body chose illness instead of death. The body made the only right choice—with the input it had.
3619: Blame doesn't work here. This is where understanding works.
3621: This explains a phenomenon that has long baffled medicine.
3622: Two people. Same diagnosis. Same treatment protocol. One recovers, the other does not. Or: one is fast, the other is slow, with relapses, with complications.
3623: We are used to explaining this by genetics, immune status, and age. All this is true. But there is a dimension that these explanations do not cover.
3624: In order for the body to remove the protective reaction—to remove the disease—it needs confidence that the threat from which the disease protected is no longer relevant. Or because a person has found enough strength to go towards his goal without self-destruction. Or because the goal has been rethought. Or because the verdict “I can’t stand it” was called into question by real experience.
3625: In each specific case, the amount of this force is different. Because the threat is different. Because the depth of unconscious aspiration is different. Because the biographical context is different.
3626: This is why treatment for the same diagnosis requires fundamentally different resources. And that’s why two people with the same diagnosis are two fundamentally different cases.
3627: Medicine that does not take this into account treats disease. Medicine that takes this into account treats a person.
3629: If the disease persists until the body has received enough strength to face what it was defending against, then the question of therapy is formulated differently.
3630: Not “how to eliminate the symptom?”—but: where to get the strength?
3631: Biological resource: sleep, nutrition, balance of the nervous system, reduction of inflammatory load. Everything we talked about in previous chapters serves just that. Not treating the disease directly—relieving the load so that the system has a resource.
3632: Psychological resource: a space in which a person for the first time allows himself to name what he wants. Where the unconscious gets the right to become conscious—without the immediate demand to act.
3633: Understanding: that the desire that seemed deadly can be realized differently. Not at a pace that would kill. Humanly.
3634: Acceptance: that some goals are truly unattainable—and that's not failure, but honesty. That letting go of the goal that the body protected through illness is not surrender, but liberation.
3635: Healing is not a victory over the disease. This is finding enough strength so that the disease is no longer needed.
3636: TEN STEPS THAT CHANGE THE SYSTEM
3637: This is not an instruction for immediate recovery. This is a map. Every step is a direction, not a destination.
3638: 1. Ask yourself: what does my illness protect me from? Not “why did I get sick?” - this leads to a search for those responsible. “What does it protect from?” leads to understanding. What changed in your life around the time your symptoms started? What became possible—and at the same time unbearable?
3639: 2. Reduce biological load. Nutrition is an act of caring for the system. Sleep is a priority. Movement—daily, even minimal. This is not a treatment. This is the creation of conditions under which treatment becomes possible.
3640: 3. Find a doctor who asks about life. Not only about analyses, but about life. If your doctor doesn't ask these questions, look for another one. This does not mean giving up the first one. This means getting the full picture.
3641: 4. Keep a diary of symptoms and events in parallel. What happens in life is next to what happens in the body. After a few weeks, the pattern becomes visible.
3642: 5. Name the aspiration. What do you want, but have not allowed yourself to want for a long time? It can be scary. You can start on paper—just for yourself. No obligation to act. Just—name it.
3643: 6. Find a small, safe action towards this desire. Not a jump. Step. So small that the nervous system will not notice it as a threat. Ten minutes. One paragraph. One call.
3644: 7. Work with biographical context. Where did the fear come from? Who was the first to say “don’t make things up”? What moment from the past is behind the feeling “I can’t stand it”? This is work with a therapist—a psychologist, a psychotherapist. This is not weakness. This is the most accurate diagnostic test possible.
3645: 8. Distinguish between “established,” “researched,” and “hypothesis”—in medicine and in life. Don't trust those who offer simple answers to complex questions. And don’t believe those who say that nothing can be changed.
3646: 9. Remember: the patient is a partner, not an object. You have the right to understand your illness. Ask questions. Disagree. Seek a second opinion.
3647: 10. Give the process time. A system that has been formed over the years does not change in a week. But she is changing. Small changes add up. Neural connections are rebuilt. Epigenetic marks are modified. The body is plastic—at any age. This is no consolation. This is a fact.
3649: We began this book with Hippocrates, the man who looked at a patient with a cough and asked about dreams. Who saw life behind the pain.
3650: We went through molecular biology and neuroendocrinology, through biographical medicine and the philosophy of meaning, through quantum biology and systems thinking, through emotions and epigenetics, through fear and forgiveness.
3651: And we returned to the same thing: man is indivisible. Disease does not live separately from life. Healing does not happen in an organ—it happens in a system called a person.
3652: Disease is not the enemy. Disease is the body’s wisest decision in conditions when no other way out was visible.
3653: The task of medicine is not to defeat this decision. And help a person find something else.
3663: This book does not provide definitive answers—because there are none.
3664: She gives questions worth living with.
3665: And a way to see a person who makes treatment as treatment, and medicine as medicine.
3667: The nutrition protocol was developed by doctors Vasiko Tkemaladze and Lali Gegeshidze, therapists who worked for several decades in Tbilisi. Their son, Dr. Jaba Tkemaladze, updated the protocol with modern data on the microbiome, detoxification and nutritional chronobiology. Protocol version: 03/09/2026.
3668: The protocol is designed for use primarily in patients with functional disorders of the biliary tract, dysbiosis, chronic subclinical inflammation and autoimmune conditions. It is not a universal dietary standard; it requires individual consultation with a doctor.
3670: Three key principles define the logic of the protocol.  1. Biliary tract as an axis of detoxification. The gallbladder and bile ducts remove fat-soluble xenobiotics, excess hormones and drug metabolites. A number of products - tomatoes (alpha-tomatine, an irritating saponin), olive oil in large quantities, a number of hot spices - disrupt the motility of the bile ducts in predisposed patients, creating a systemic load.  2. Microbiome quality. Industrial baker's yeast, when consumed regularly, can support the growth of opportunistic fungi. Bread made with homemade wine yeast, which dies during baking, is free from this drawback. Casein in soft dairy products provokes unwanted immune reactions in some patients with autoimmune conditions; aged brine cheeses (casein destroyed during fermentation) are safe.  3. Chronobiology of nutrition. Fruits in the morning are metabolized by the active circadian clock of the pancreas. In the evening, the same fructose loads the liver during the detoxification period of daily metabolites. Sutton et al. (2018, Cell Metabolism) showed that an early eating window improves insulin sensitivity regardless of diet composition.
3672: - Sip water (200–250 ml) 20–30 minutes before meals; do not drink while eating - Each piece is chewed 21-40 times - Do not eat in a state of acute stress or conflict - Last meal - 3 hours before bedtime - Physical activity - no earlier than 2 hours after meals - Weekly fasting day or 16:8 format (intermittent fasting) - Fruits and berries - only in the first half of the day - Instant coffee - in the morning before meals or 2-3 hours after
3674: Animal protein: eggs (hard-boiled for 40–90 minutes or fried on both sides - at least well done); bloodless poultry (chicken), beef, lamb; fatty fish (mackerel, sardine, salmon).  Dairy products: butter, ghee; brine-aged cheeses (suluguni, feta cheese, feta, chechil) - casein is decomposed by fermentation.  Nuts: walnuts and almonds - only freshly chopped; oxidize quickly after opening.  Cereals and legumes: bread only with homemade wine yeast; wheat, millet, pearl barley, semolina, oatmeal (rolled oats), pasta; beans.  Vegetables: cucumbers, zucchini, green beans, broccoli, cauliflower, mushrooms, olives; stewed beets, stewed carrots (not raw).  Fermentations (without industrial yeast): cucumbers, squash, broccoli, green beans, jonjoli.  Greens: cilantro, dill, basil, savory (kondari), celery, leek, nettle, ekala, beet tops (phali).  Spices: hops-suneli, utskho-suneli, saffron, marigold (Imereti saffron), bay leaf, coriander (seeds), black cumin (gitsruli), cumin, cumin, thyme, vinegar; tarragon and garlic - only after heat treatment (5-15 minutes minimum).  Fruits (only before noon): apples, pears, plums, tkemali, tklapi, peach, quince, pomegranate, blackberry, dogwood, cherry, cranberry, barberry, watermelon, blueberry, currant, avocado; jams and dried fruits from those listed.  Fats: refined sunflower oil.  Drinks: instant coffee, wine (in moderation), sour mineral waters, infusions of linden leaves, blackberries, cranberries, currants, savory, saffron, caraway, basil, dill, celery.  Sweet: no more than 2–3 tsp. sugar (8–12 g) per day; sponge cakes, cookies, fruit pies from permitted products - in moderation.
3676: Yeast and bread: commercial yeast (baker's, brewer's, thermophilic); all industrial bread.  Meat: pork; game; blood and blood dishes; poultry and animal skin; abdominal fat.  Dairy products: any milk (cow, goat, sheep); kefir, yogurt, matsoni, fermented baked milk, sour cream, cottage cheese; soft cheeses without brine aging (ricotta, mascarpone, mozzarella).  Eggs: raw or soft-boiled (insufficient heat treatment).  Cereals: buckwheat; corn; soy; peas.  Vegetables: tomatoes; potato; eggplant; raw carrots; white cabbage; lettuce leaves; sweet potato.  Spices and herbs: spinach, sorrel, black pepper, parsley, raw tarragon (boiled/stewed is allowed), radish, raw garlic (heat-treated is allowed).  Fruits: grapes; citrus; bananas; apricot/dried apricot; figs; persimmon; kiwi; raisin; cherries; here; melon; peanut; hazelnut; any fruit in the afternoon.  Oils: olive oil; unrefined oils; margarine.  Drinks: brewed coffee (sediment and foam irritate the bile duct); tea; beer, champagne, liqueur, vodka; alkaline mineral waters; juices, compotes, lemonade, carbonated drinks.
3678: The protocol provides all key nutrients from food sources: Calcium: cucumbers, green beans, almonds, sardines, basil Magnesium: beet tops (phali), nettle, ekala, walnuts, almonds Iron (heme): meat, eggs; (non-heme): dark greens, nettle Zinc: beef, eggs Iodine, Selenium: sea fish, eggs Omega-3 (ALA): walnuts, almonds; (EPA/DHA): fatty fish Vitamin D: almonds, eggs, fatty fish B12, B9, B6: eggs, meat, fish B5 (pantothenic acid): homemade wine yeast bread Focus on food sources rather than additives is a fundamental position of the protocol: the bioavailability of nutrients from whole foods is on average higher due to synergistic cofactors in the food matrix.
